[Column 1]
MEMORIAL ANNIVERSARY.

REPORT OF THE RELIEF SOCIETY MEETING
HELD IN THE OGDEN TABERNACLE, JULY
19TH, 1888, IN COMMEMORATION OF THE
LAST PUBLIC VISIT AND INSTRUCTIONS OF
PRESIDENT BRIGHAM YOUNG, ON INVITA-
TION OF PRESIDENT JANE S. RICHARDS, TO
THE RELIEF SOCIETY AND YOUNG LADIES'
IMPROVEMENT ASSOCIATIONS OF THE
WEBER STAKE OF ZION, JUST ELEVEN
YEARS AGO THE 19TH INST.

(REPORTED BY JOHN M WHITTAKER.)

[Concluded.]
	Sister E. B. Wells, Editor of The WOMAN'S
EXPONENT, said:
	I feel glad to have the privilege of standing be-
fore you, just for a short time; and I am also glad
that there is but a few minutes left for me to
occupy.  I expect you are all tired, but no doubt
you will agree with me, that we have had a great
deal of good teaching.
	I feel to-day as I did eleven years ago to-day,
that this is a "red letter day" for the sisters of this
Stake of Zion.  I know that what was said on
that day has been like little seeds that have ger-
minated and grown up, and have since made a
harvest of good deeds, from the first appoint-
ment that was made here from this stand when
Sister Richards was chosen President of all the
branches of the Relief Society in this County, as
well as the Young Ladies'.  That appointment
was the first made in this dispensation of any
woman to preside over the Relief Society in an
organized capacity by President Young
on that occasion, and I bear testimony
that I heard him appoint Sister Jane S. Richards
to preside over all the societies and associations
in this Stake of Zion; and he also requested them

[Column 2]
at that time to make quarterly reports to be read
at quarterly conferences.  And it was here in
this Tabernacle that the first quarterly
conference of the Relief Society was called.
Bro. Richards gave out the notice by suggestion
of President Young, at the close of the meeting.
It was not long after that, that President Young
died.  And Sister Eliza went throughout this 
territory organizing in the several Stakes after
that pattern.
	Afterwards, I firmly believe it was revealed to
her, that she was to go forth and organize the
Young Ladies in a separate and distinct associa-
tion, but one in strict harmony and to work in per-
fect accord with the Relief Society.  And a great
work has been done since then.  I cannot help
repeating that this is a "red letter day" for the
women of Zion.  To speak of all the work that
has been done by the sisters, would take a great
deal of time.
	I know and can testify to the liberality of the
sisters of this Stake in giving means to the
Deseret Hospital.  The amount donated by them
has been next to that of the Salt Lake Stake, al-
though many Stakes have been very liberal.  And
when Brother Richards was speaking on that
subject, I thought of a little circumstance which
President Canute Peterson related when I was in
Sanpete, a short time ago.
	While he was absent on a mission in Scandina-
via, the sisters of Sanpete Stake, with all their
other donations, sent him a thousand dollars with
which to emigrate the poor from that country.  Do
you think we could ever have accomplished what
we have, unless we had been organized perfectly?
We should pray for those who are in responsible
positions. I feel positive that the sisters will sus-
tain Sister Zina D. H. Young in the position to
which she has been called—President over all the
Relief Society.  She will be at home before long.
I hope her health will be renewed.  I trust the
brethren and sisters will remember her and Sister
Richards in their prayers.  The position to which
Sister Richards has been called, has been filled
with efficiency.  And I trust the position of Sister
Zina will be filled with that efficiency with which
the work was performed by those who have gone
before.  Let the mantle of Sister Eliza fall upon
Sister Zina, that she may be able to satisfy the
people as Sister Eliza did in traveling among the
Saints, and assisting in doing good and in perform-
ing a great work; and it certainly is a great work.
I know that the sisters who labor in the organiza-
tions are striving to help build up the kingdom
and to work in harmony with the Bishops and
brethren; and that it is the desire of the sisters to
work in perfect harmony with the priesthood.
We must remember that there is a great deal to
do and a great amount of instruction to give; and
we are expected to work together.  There are
many things that it seems almost impossible in
our weakness to do; but when we receive encour-
agement from the brethren and are blessed of the
Lord, we persevere and go on with renewed
energy.  The sisters have just as essential a
work as the brethren, and while they do not go
abroad, preaching the Gospel to the nations of
the earth, they have a great work to do here at
home as missionaries.
	Since this meeting was called, I have looked
over some of the remarks of Presidents
Young and Taylor, who spoke eleven years ago
to-day, and have tried to get the spirit of that
meeting into my heart, so that I might realize it
here to-day.  And I think that the same spirit is
here.  It was a grand beginning, and I feel that
if Sister Richards wanted to resign her position,
it would be almost impossible.  Sometimes when
she has been sick and weak, and we have been
talking about these matters, she has said: "Well,
if I resign, somebody else can do it, better, per-
haps, than I."  Well, I don't think she should re-
sign a position she was called to by the President
any more than one of the Apostles should.  She
felt the same way in regard to the Hospital.  But
we all felt that we could not do without her in that
Board.  I pray God to bless and strengthen her more
abundantly.  She has given us a great treat here to-
day.  I think the Bishops and those who prepared the
dinner deserve great credit.  I heard several say
it was the best and most nicely arranged public
dinner that they ever attended.
	I feel to return my thanks to you, and especially
Sister Richards and those who proposed that
Brigham Young should come up here and speak
to the people assembled on that grand occasion we
now commemorate, and for the instructions he
then gave.  I hope you will all read about the
duties of the sisters, and all those who have pre-
served the WOMAN'S EXPONENT can read it from
that paper.  It was Bro. James Taylor who took
it down in short hand, and it was published after
President Young's death.  He spoke about home-
made bonnets and the manufacture of home-made
hats; and I remember very well when he came
and dedicated what was then called "The Co-
operative Millinery Establishment," but which
we generally speak of as the "Relief Society
Store."  He spoke of the silk culture, and urged
the sisters to plant mulberry trees, and about
storing up grain, and he seemed to remember
every little item that would come in the province
of the sisters to do, or which they could attend to,
I, for one, want to recollect these things; though,
perhaps, we do not carry out his instructions as
we should.  I remember hearing him say, that one
great mistake of his life was, that he paid all the
tithing for his family.  But if he had his life to
live over again, he would have every wife and 
every child each pay tithing.  Many of the other
leading brethren, when they paid their tithing,
would pay for their whole family also.  But things
are different in these days.  Every one should pay
tithing for himself or herself.  I remember an in-
stance of a young lady who taught school and
earned $100; I presume it was the most money
she ever had, and her mother said to her she had
better pay $10 for tithing.  But the young lady
thought it was too much to take $10 out of her
hard earnings, but purchased a nice switch of 
hair, and on her way home a sort of whirlwind
arose and took her switch and carried it away.
She advertised for it, and paid either fifty cents or
a dollar for advertising, but never found it
(Laughter).  She afterwards said if she had only
taken the advice of her mother and paid her tith-
ing, she would have been that much better off.
	I really feel that I am trespassing upon your
time; but I feel proud of this day.  It is a day
long to be remembered.
	President Jane S. Richards made some appro-
priate remarks about the anniversary, and called
upon Bishop E. Stratford, who was pleased to
have the happy privilege of meeting with the sis-
ters on so important an occasion, and trusted that
it would not be the last of such meetings.  He
was present eleven years ago, when President
Young delivered the sermon referred to.  He
had cause to appreciate the labors of the Relief 
Society in his ward, and felt to ask God to bless
the sisters in all they undertook to do.
	Sister M. A. Freeze, President of the Y. L. M.
I. A. of Salt Lake Stake, said:
	My brethren and sisters: I do, indeed, deem it
a privilege to stand before you and raise my voice 
in this assembly this day, and bear my testimony
that I know that Brigham Young was a prophet of
God, and that he was inspired of God in organiz-
ing the Relief and Young Ladies' Societies.  He
instructed them to work together with the same
purpose in view.  If I had no other proof of this
Gospel than this, I would know that it is the king-
dom of God, and that this is the work of God.
She felt to act in any and every capacity where
she might be called to labor, and prayed for the
welfare of Zion.


[Column 1]
Sister Maria W. Wilcox. Cor. Sec. Relief So-
ciety, Salt Lake Stake, said:
	My beloved brethren and sisters: I feel highly
honored and gratified in attending this meeting,
and feel this is a day long to be remembered; and
the instructions and counsel which have been
given, if we will follow them out in our lives,
will be beneficial to us all. These organizations
are timely; they have proved a great blessing to
the Saints. It has been the means of uniting the
people together in love and union and every good
and perfect work. And it is a pleasure to associate
in this capacity and realize the good we do from
time to time. I have been greatly blessed in
these associations, and I pray that the blessings of
our Heavenly Father may rest upon us at all
times. This is my desire, in the name of the Lord
Jesus Christ. Amen.
	Dr. R. B. Pratt said in substance, that she had
been pleased in what had been said, and that the
truths which had been uttered should be taken
home to every heart, and be lived up to in the fu-
ture. She desired to so live that her physical
and spiritual being would grow together in perfec-
tion.
	
She paid a high tribute to the work of the Re-
lief Society, and especially to the Deseret Hos-
pital.
	She thought we should so live as to be models,
not  only in morality and higher education, but
also in our physical beings. Our course should
be upward and onward, having high and noble
aims, and worthy purpose. Sister Pratt made
special reference to the labors in the Hos-
pital; that many had come there and been
marvelously healed by the power of God through
the ordinances of the Church. And also, that
that was a part of the treatment in the Hospital.
She believed that there was plenty of room in this
Church for all to labor in positions of trust, and
invoked the divine blessings of Providence upon
all, and especially the sisters, that they might gain
the crown that is awaiting the faithful.
	Sister Kimball said she desired to bear her testi-
mony to the truth of this work. I know Joseph
Smith was a prophet of God. I know no other
man could have done what he did. I know that
the principles which he taught are pure and holy.
I never have felt to be sorry for one moment,
although what I have had to pass through is known
to no one but God. Man could not realize it. I
could not express it. God knows I have done the
best I could. I know that Brigham Young was a
holy and righteous man. I would not give the
experience I have for all the wealth of this world.
I pray ever for exaltation and salvation in the
celestial kingdom of God. I feel, and have it in
my heart to be true to the end. I feel to bless
these my sisters. May God bless and comfort the
daughters of Zion, that we may be truly saviors
upon Mount Zion, is my prayer in the name of
Jesus Christ. Amen.
	Bishop Robert McQuarrie was pleased to be
here on so important an occasion, and he felt im-
pressed in looking over the people's faces to see
how promptly they would attend their meetings.
And he felt to thank God that there were those
who took an interest in such a great work as the
sisters have in hand. He did not feel to continue
his remarks, but asked the blessings of God upon
the people in the land of Zion.
	Sister Emily S. Richards expressed her kind
feelings, and was pleased to meet with those she
had learned to love while living in Ogden. She
desired to labor with the other sisters in the work
of God, and trusted she would, with the other sis-
ters, be blessed.
	President Jane S. Richards then expressed her-
self highly gratified with the proceedings, and
realized that she had been blessed this day, and
desired all those who had the same feeling to raise
their hands. (Every hand went up).
	She would like to hear from others, but it is

[Column 2]
late and we must close. She thanked those who
had so kindly contributed to the interest of the
occasion, the visitors and all who were present,
and prayed that God would ever bless the Relief
Society.
	The congregation joined in singing, "The Spirit
of God like a fire is burning." Benediction was
pronounced by Charles F. Middleton.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS.
	CASSIA STAKE.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our first Relief Society Conference, which
convened June 23 last, was quite an event in
the early history of this, the Cassia Stake of
Zion.
	Nearly all the Society’s officers were present,
also the Stake authorities and several Bishops
of wards. The several branches of the Relief
Society in the stake were represented with one
exception, and their presidents reported them
as doing well, being alive to their duties and
flourishing as well as ever circumstances would
permit.
	The Presidents of the.Y. L. M. I. A. and
Primary Association also represented their
calling although but  few organizations had
been effected as yet.
	The Conference was addressed in an interest-
ing manner by Prest. Louisa Haight and her
Counselors, also Prest. Hunter of the Tooele
Stake and Sister Todd of Utah. Prest. Hor-
ten D. Haight, his Counselors and other
brethren gave some good instruction concern-
ing the duties and privileges of the Sisters.
	The speakers expressed a hope of spending
many another such an enjoyable occasion as
was our first conference.
				Louisa Pickett, Sec.

	VISITING TOUR, UTAH STAKE.
	On the morning of July 18th, 1888, per
appointment, the Stake Officers made a tour
through Utah Stake.
	Prest. Annie K. Smoot, in company with
First Counselor Eliza Taylor and Secretary
Mary Stewart, visited the southern part of the
County, namely, Springville, Spanish Fork,
Salem, Benjaman, Payson, Santaquin, Lake
Shore and Goshen, and then, on July 30th,
Prest. A. K. Smoot, in company with Second
Counselor Sarah E. Cluff and Assistant Secre-
tary Grace L. Smith, visited the northern part,
namely: Pleasant Grove, American Fork,
Alpine, Fairfield and Cedar Fort.
	While absent they held fourteen meetings;
met 1,023 children and 103 officers; traveled
140 miles, 56 miles by rail and 80 miles by
team, being absent 7 days, 4 days south and
3 days north; reorganized two Associations.
At each place programmes were carried out by
the children, consisting of songs, recitations,
etc.
	The officers of each Association gave verbal
reports, showing that the Associations
were all in a flourishing condition, and that
the officers were a united band of sisters. The
sisters gave some excellent instructions at each
place, both to the parents and children. 
	Prest. A. K. Smoot spoke on the subjects of
order, faith, obedience, prayer, etc. Exhorted
the sisters not to get discouraged in the great
work of teaching the future hope of Zion.
	The Spirit of God prevailed throughout all
the meetings; the children performed their
parts with zest and good will. The good roads,
pleasant weather, and the hearty welcome with
which the visitors were received, greatly re-
lieved, the monotonous and tiresome journey.
	Much credit is due to the officers of the
different Associations, and the brethren who
kindly assisted us in the discharge of our duties.
	The Primary Conference of Utah Stake

[Column 2]
was held at Lehi, Aug. 29th, in the Lehi Meet-
ing House, commencing at 2 o’clock p.m. On
account of the illness of Prest. A. K. Smoot,
the meeting was called to order by Sister Re-
becca Standing. Programme, consisting of
songs, recitations, Scripture exercises, ete., was
carried out by the children. Addresses were
made by Counselors Eliza Taylor and Sarah
Cluff. A short speech and song by W. W.
Willis, of Salt Lake City, when the meeting
was adjourned for six months.
	The forenoon was occupied by a business
meeting of all the officers of the different as-
sociations of Utah Stake. Verbal reports
were given in and different methods discussed,
after which the officers were all invited to the
school house, where a repast had been provided
by the sisters of Lehi. Over one hundred sat
down to the first table. A good spirit pre-
vailed and we had a good time.
	MARY STEWART Sec.
	GRACE L. SMITH Asst. Sec.
	
	SALT LAKE STAKE.	
The Relief Society Conference of this Stake
of Zion was held in the 14th Ward Assembly
Rooms, on Thursday, September 13th, Mrs. M.
Isabella Horne presiding. After the usual
opening exercises, the Secretary called the
president’s roll. Twenty-five presidents and
five counselors reported, representing, thirty
branches of the Relief Society. Minutes of
previous conference read and accepted.
	Mrs. S. M. Kimball addressed the meeting.
She spoke of her recent visit to San Francisco
and other parts of California, and the ideas
people outside entertained of our people; de-
scribed an interesting visit to a celebrated
church, and remarked that her visit had en-
larged her charity for those who were not of
our faith.
	Mrs. Ellen Barton spoke a short time; she
had recently returned from a visit to England;
had enjoyed her visit very much indeed, but
was glad to be at home again; thought the con-
dition of the world pitiable, because of their
willingness to accept evil reports, and their
lack of desire to hear the Gospel.	
	Prest. Horne spoke particularly about the
semi-annual reports being prapared to send in
to the Secretary as early as the 25th of Feb.
and of August, so that a correct report may be
made in time to read at the Stake conference in
March before the General Conference in April;
also in September before the General Confer-
ence in October. Hoped the sisters who had
so liberally sustained the Hospital would be
blest fourfold, and advised them to continue in
their good work. 
	Adjourned to 3 p.m. Singing, ,‘Doxology.”
Benediction by Mrs. B. W. Smith.
	Afternoon session: After the usual exercises
of singing and prayer, Mrs. B. W. Smith ad-
dressed the conference; spoke of the encourag-
ing reports that had been read and of the
Primary Fair; thought it a good showing, and
urged those present to go and encourage the
little workers. Advised the sisters to teach
the children to be faithful, truthful, honest and
industrious.	
	Mrs. E. S. Taylor considered it one of the
first duties of the Relief Society to attend the
conference. The organizations were by the
Prophet Brigham Young, that we might be
prepared for our many duties. Spoke of the
carelessness and lack of energy of some who
had been heretofore more diligent, and urged
those present to exhort, persuade and encourage
others to know and understand, and gain the
reward we have started out for.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells thought it was very com-
mon, to excuse ourselves in not doing our duty;
felt that we ought to attend the meetings con-
vened for our improvement, that we might
gain intelligence, wisdom and understanding.


Urged the sisters to read more, and learn what 
was transpiring here and elsewhere; thought
our lives and characters and example should 
be so exemplary as to refute the evil reports
circulated against us.  Mentioned the proposed 
visit of Mrs. Saxon and Mrs. Colby, two dis-
tinguished suffrage women and public speakers, 
and hoped the sisters would attend their 
lectures.  Advised the mothers to teach the
children to be industrious, as it prevented a 
great deal of mischief, and also to see that the 
children attended the Sunday School and Prim-
ary Association.  
	Mrs. E. Yates, Prest.  of the Relief Society 
of Millard Stake, bore a strong testimony to 
the truth of this work, and spoke of the scat-
tered condition of the settlements in the Stake 
where she presided, and the difficulty of meet-
ing together often under those circumstances.  
She gave the mothers good counsel in regard 
to controlling their children, and teaching 
them when very young.  Spoke eloquently 
upon Temple work, required at our hands, and 
the preparation we ought to make in order to 
perform it.  Related an interesting incident 
that occurred to a brother in Sanpete, urging 
him to perform the work for the dead.  
	Mrs. M. A. Freeze thought we should obtain 
the records of our ancestors, and leave it in 
proper order for our children if we could not 
do the work ourselves.  Exhorted all to dili-
gence in the path that leads to eternal life. 
Said many good things in a happy manner.  
	Mrs. R. Jones occupied a few minutes in 
speaking of the labors and duties devolving 
upon the sisters; thought we were not aware of 
the great responsibilities; we had entered upon 
a labor of love and life; let us help each other.  
Referred feelingly to the training of the young
people in keeping the commandments of God.  
	Prest. Horne: "Our pure minds require to 
be stirred up by way of remembrance, that we 
may not fall into a state of lethargy, that we 
may receive the bread of life that comes from 
above, which we require every day."  She 
related a dream of Mrs. Saxon's, the lady who 
is about to visit Salt Lake, that she had told 
when here on a visit in 1882.  Mrs. Horne 
pointed out the meaning,etc.	*	*	*	Spoke
of her visit to the old Church of St. Martyn's
when in England, recently, and said that over the 
entrance was the inscription:  "Holiness to the 
Lord," sbowing that they still retained that 
ancient custom of Israel.  She spoke upon the 
principle of tithing, and said she well remem-
bered when a girl, that every 10th shock of 
wheat in a field had a green bough stuck in it, 
denoting that the 10th shock was held sacred
for the ministry.  Urged the sisters to attend to 
paying tithing; also to cultivate humility, that
they might not be easily overcome by the 
follies of life.  
	Con-erence adjourned until December.  Sing-
ing, "Our God, we raise to Thee."  Benedic-
tion by Mrs. E. Howard. 
		E. HOWARD, Stake Secretary R. S.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	The regular semi-monthly meeting was held
in the 14th Ward Assembly rooms, Aug. 18,
1888, Mrs.M. Isabella Horne presiding.  Singing,
"O My Father," etc. Prayer by Mrs. How-
ard.  Singing, "O say what is Truth," etc.
	President Horne addressed the audience
Hoping all were well; was very thankful to 
see them all again; had been very successful
while abroad in getting her records; had a very 
interesting experience by being able to meet
many who were interested in hearing about
the Gospel.  Had traveled 15,000 miles; met
many gentlemen and ladies,a very few ignorant
persons were rude and impolite. 
	"I also met many of the Saints.  I hope I
have sown some seed that will bring forth 
good fruit.  I visited an Industrial school


while in Liverpool which I very much ad-
mired for it is proving a great blessing to 1,000
little waifs who have no parents;  they are
taken good care of and taught different trades
that will help them to become useful members
to society.  I am very thankful that it is as 
well with us as it is.  I hope we will acknowl-
edge God in all things.  I pray you may all 
enjoy the blessings of God on you and that
you may have peace and the Spirit of God.
	Mrs. Silver sang  "We thank thee O God 
for a Prophet" etc.
	Mrs. E. S. Taylor.  "I had perfect confi-
dence in the blessing that was promised on
Mrs. Horne before she left by one of the Apos-
tles; I felt all would be well with her; she was
ignorant of our doctrines and way of living as
there are.  We hope that many who are in in-
tense darkness in the world will yet be brought
to the light of the Gospel.  The gifts we have
are from our Heavenly Father.  Let us be
humble and faithful and overcome self, and 
bring ourselves into perfect subjection to the
will of God.  I hope we will try and live by
the light of the Spirit of God."
	Mrs. Watmongh.  "We are thankful that
Sister Horne has come home in safety.  I have 
an idea that all our testimonies are recorded
above and they are accepted by the Lord, and 
the better it will be for us thereafter the more
we bear.  May the Lord keep us faithful."
	Mrs. H. M. Whitney.  "I also feel to rejoice
over sister Horne's safe return.  The Lord
helped me to come to meeting to-day.  I plead
continually for the youth of Zion that they
may be kept in the ways of the Lord.  Many
will keep the faith, but some will sell their
birthright for a mess of pottage.  May God
help our young men and young women to learn
the ways of God and walk therein."
	Sister Jane Perkins: "I know this is the work
of God and I rejoice in it continually, and
may God keep us safe to our religion." 
Mrs. B. W. Smith.  "Do good to our
neighbor; keep the Sabbath day holy; attend
to our prayers; walk in the path of duty."
	Mrs. Dr. Barney.  "How do we know which
is the true church?  By the signs which follow
the believer.  We know by the scriptures be-
ing fulfilled in our day and in our church."
	Mrs. H. C. Young.  "We rejoice that we 
can read and understand the scriptures as they
are written.  The greatest knowledge we can
have is to understand the ways of the Lord
and the law of God is true learning." 
	Mrs. Stevenson spoke a short time very in-
terestingly concernimg her visit to England
and fullfillment of prophecy.
	A letter was read asking for assistance to
emigrate a family from England. 
	Mrs. Horne said a few words in closing. 
Singing "Praise to the Man," ect.  Benedic-
tion by Mary Silver.                         E H.


[Column 1]
LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL.
BY M. J. TANNER.
[Continued.]
	Williamsburg, Thursday, May 20th.  We
are very tired after yesterday’s tramp.  I am
busy preparing to move on to-morrow.  I have
become much attached to my cousin and her
family; the boys think they would like to go to 
Utah; I should be so glad to have them; it
pains me very much to think that I may never
see them any more.  I expect to meet my
Aunt Cornelia, my father’s sister, at Lockport;
she is also aunt to cousin Ettie, and the rela-
tives, I have seen so far, are on my father’s
side.  Before reaching Lockport I shall visit
at Ithaca with my mother’s relatives.  I have
enjoyed my visit here very much, and seen 
such things as Ettie could show me, though we

[Column 2]
could be engaged many days more, and still
see but very little of the great city.
	In the afternoon we go over to New York to
take a last look.  It is quite like a museum to 
go through the large stores: they are on a 
magnificent scale.  We stop at Ridley’s, which
is one of the largest, and go through it, as we 
have done other places, just to see.  I make a 
few small purchases, and wish once more for a
longer purse.  Prices here are the lowest I
have seen.  The stores are closed at six oclock,
and an iron grating drawn in front of them.
We walk along, looking at many things of in-
terest on the sidewalk.  We see lovely things
in embroideries, laces and flowers; one large
show window is filled with dolls; the sign on it
reads, “The Babies on our block.” Dolls of
every size and description, some of them as 
large as a child two years old, and beautifully
dressed. 
	When we get tired of looking we return
home; call at the express office to have them 
take my trunk to the depot.
	Ithica, May 16th.  Weather cloudy; it rained 
a little in the morning.  I have said the last
goodbye.  It is always sad parting with those
we may never meet any more.  Cousin Charlie
came with me to the train.  We crossed the
river at Hoboken, the widest ferry I have
crossed yet.  I said goodbye to Charlie, and
sped away on the train to see new places and 
new faces.

	Farewell, friends, by distance parted,
	Who can part without regret?
	Memory claims the tender-hearted,
	And we may remember yet.  
			
	The trees and fields are green now, and nature 
is lovely after being shut in by high walls,
where one never steps on the ground and sees 
only houses.  After all the magnificence of
man, there is an exhilaration in nature’s differ-
ent aspects that gives to the the mind another
train of ideas.
	11:15 we stop at a nice little station.  The
hills are covered with grass and trees, farms
and orchards.  A cemetery is on the hillside,
and the white stones gleam among the foliage.
Pass the factory where the Beaty Organs are
made.  News agent going through the train
every five minutes with something for sale.  Pass
through a long tunnel and come out in a beau-
tiful country, “Lovely as a poet’s dream.” If
I could only place it on paper, but words can-
not convey an idea of the lovely scenery,
houses nestling among the trees, villages scat-
tered among the hills, farms wherever the loca-
tion will permit.  Pass through another long
tunnel and stop at a station.  Quite a number 
of people get on the train.  It looks strange to
see farms so high on the mountains; they look
like patchwork.  We pass a pretty cemetery; the
graves are covered with blossoms.  We are
traveling through the Blue Ridge Mountains.
Pass Portland, a pretty town; houses mostly
painted white.
	12 m.  Water Gap.  The Delaware River
runs through the mountains here.  The eve-
ning is fine.  We are going up a steep grade 
and the iron horse puffs hard.  We pass
through a timbered country.  Some one is try-
ing to clear a farm but it looks discouraging.
1 p.m.  Another tunnel.  The country is 
covered with verdure.  The Lightning Ex-
press goes by; it is well named.  We go
through pine woods and it looks as though we
were on the top of the mountains.  A few old 
frame houses are scattered along, but every-
thing denotes poverty.  Moscone, 1:30 A pic-
turesque town.  The mountains are not like 
ours; the hills are not so high, nor the canons
so deep. We are descending and pass through 
another tunnel.  This is five tunnels we have 
passed through.  Scranton Pa.  The train
stops for dinner.  Binghamton 4:15  A pretty 
city. Omega, 5 p.m. Change cars.  The coun-
try is hilly as we travel along the Susqeuhana


River. Reach Ithica at last and receive a 
kind welcome from my cousins. Find two let-
ters waiting for me.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HOW FAR?

	I have a daughter just launching into woman-
hood, and I feel, as every mother does, likely, 
that I should like it to be a noble womanhood
for her sake, also for my own, for I should like
to do my life's work well, and the moulding of 
characters seems to be the great work for the
mothers.
	An incident happened this morning that has
kept my thoughts busy all day; so, in the hope 
that an abler pen than mine will take up the 
subject, I submit these thoughts to the EXPON-
ENT.
	This daughter wished to wear an article of
dress, or ornament, that is certainly a fashion
of Babylon, to which I objected, when she re-
marked, that if she did not, she would appear
odd from all other girls, and she could not see
why I objected, for Mrs. P——, President of 
the Young Ladies' Association, wore one. If
the daughters of good women, and good women
themselves, follow some of the fashions of the 
ungodly, where can we draw a line so we can
know how far we dare venture and still be safe 
from danger of contamination from the spirit
that prompts these fashions?
	We want our daughters' lives to be as bright
and happy as possible, but should we not try to
instil correct doctrine into their minds, even on
the subject of dress. My faith is, that if ever
we become the instruments in God's hand to 
accomplish His great purposes, we shall even 
dress in "holiness to the Lord."
							E. T. G.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	The tight coat sleeve is entirely abandoned
in favor of the sleeve of puffs and plaits.
	A new bangle is made of gold wire in the
 shape of an arrow which curves around the
arm.
	Outside pockets will be worn with fall gowns.
As they are convenient and can be made orna-
mental they will be heartily welcome.
	Bows of plaited braid appear at the foot of 
the skirt in all new gowns. They are well re-
ceived, ae they look well and save the dress
from wear.
	Realism permeates all arts—even the milli-
ners. The latest decoration for bonnets is a 
bunch of red clover blossoms or thistleheads.


[Column 1]
NOTES AND NEWS.
	Miss Julia Wolfe, an English lady, has com-
posed an opera entitled "Carina." The Musical
Standard says it will be produced in London
shortly. 
	Charles Lamb's grave at Edmonton is sadly
neglected. The simple slab of limestone which
marks the spot is nearly hidden with weeds
and grasses. 
	Amelie Rives-Chanler confines herself to very
few books, but those she does read are selected
with great care. Shakspere, Poe, and George
Eliot are her favorite authors. 
	The Senate has printed Mr. Blair's report
from the committee on education and labor, re-
commending a prohibitory amendment and has
embodied in it Mrs. Bittenbender's arguments
before the committee on behalf of the W. C. 
T. U. 
	Mrs. Alma Tadema is an instance of the

[Column 2]
kind of women, constantly growing more num-
erous, who achieve success and fame in the
outside world and still are thoroughly the mis-
tress of their homes. She is a model house-
keeper and adopts for a motto, "Kitchen first, 
studio second." 
	The world of art owes Princess Torrearsa a 
great debt for the preservation of the famous
collection of Greek vases brought together by 
the late archaeologist Serradifaleo. Through
her efforts the collection has been placed in the
museum of Palermo, at Sicily, where it will be
doubtless kept for all time. 
	The poems of Miss Mary Robinson, the En-
glish verse writer, have been translated into 
French, and, as a fitting acknowledgment of
her indebtedness, she is about to marry her
translator, Prof. Darmesteter of the College
of France. Miss Robinson is the author of 
the biography of Emily Bronte. 
	A bill has passed both the senate and house
of representatives to provide for the appoint-
ment of three police matrons for the District
of Columbia, at a salary of $600 a year each. 
These matrons, who are to care for women
prisoners in the nation's capital, are to be ap-
pointed on the recommendation of ten women 
in good standing in the district. 
	There are not many men who have the business
ability necessary to the successful management 
of an income of $7,000, a month. No wonder
that her business managers are surprised at 
Mrs. George Westinghouse of Pittsburg, whose
income is considerably larger than the amount
mentioned and yet who handles it with wonder-
ful shrewdness and business tact. 
	The fact that women have clever hands and
nimble fingers as well as brains is being demon-
strated more and more clearly every day. Since
Rosa Bonheur first showed the world what a 
woman might do for art, the growth of the 
number of artists of the gentler sex has been
on the constant increase. The latest success
is that of Miss Bentley of Cincinnati, a
teacher of wood carving. A recent exhibition 
of the work done by her lady pupils shows
some remarkable originality of design and 
great firmness and delicacy of execution. 
	General Philip H. Sheridan died of "heart
failure" after several months' illness, August 
5th last. The remote cause of death was
disease in the valves of the heart. He was 
under sixty years of age, having been born at 
Somerset, Ohio, March 6, 1831. General 
Sheridan's parents came from Ireland and 
settled in Ohio in the first quarter of the cen-
tury. At twelve years of age the boy, Phil, 
found employment in a hardware store. Next
he passed to the dry goods counter. While
thus employed he heard of an opportunity to
go to West Point, and at once wrote to the 
Congressman of his district about it, and to his
surprise was nominated. Five years were
spent at the National Academy, but at twenty-
two he was graduated and assigned to duty on 
the frontier, where he was stationed at the
opening of the late war. His career during
the war was that of cavalry officer, a hard
fighter, and a most successful one. He exem-
plified his organization in a most striking way, 
at once earnest, courageous, persistent, cautious, 
quick, and spirited. He gave to his men an 
intrepidity, confidence, and audacity like his
own, which enabled him to get a great deal
more work out of ten thousand men than
another commander would get from twenty
thousand. His presence with a command
fairly doubled its strength. Thus another of 
the "Old Guard" is gone.—Ex.


OBITUARY.

	DIED, in Centerville, Davis Co., August 8th, 1888, of
typhoid pneumonia, Sister Emeline Leavitt Smith, wife of
President William R. Smith, aged fifty-six years. Deceased
was born in Canada, in 1832, was baptized when abont
twelve years of age; shared in many of the trials and per-
secutions of the Saints. She lived in plural marriage with
her husband, and was true to her covenants. She was a 
faithful wife, a loving mother, a kind neighbor, and was
beloved by all who knew her. She was counselor in the
Relief Society for many years, which position she held at
the time of her death   She discharged all her duties
with credit to herself and the satisfaction of others. She
will be well remembered by many who have shared her
hospitality. She leaves a husband, three sons and four
daughters, a number of grandchildren and a host of 
friends to mourn her loss.

	Mary Ann Reeves was born February 12th, 1812, in the
town of Birmingham, in the County of Warwick, Eng-
land. Embraced the Gospel in the year 1840; in the year
1852 she, with her husband and three children, embarked
on the ship Ellen Maria to New Orleans, from thence up
the river to Kansas City, crossed the plains in A. O.
Smoot's company; her youngest child died and was buried
in the Black Hills; arrived in the valley September 3rd;
lived in Salt Lake City two years, after which she and her
family moved to Centerville, Davis County, which was her
home up to the time of her death. When the Relief So-
ciety was organized in Centerville, she was appointed
treasurer of the Society; she held that office for several
years. About ten days before she died she went to pay a 
visit to her daughter, who had gone to reside at Brigham 
City, where she was taken very sick, from which she never 
recovered, but died August 11th, 1888. Her remains
were brought to Centerville for interment. She was a kind
and affectionate mother, wife and friend. She died as she
had lived, a true and faithful Latter-day Saint, with a sure
hope and faith of coming forth in the resurrection of the
just.										A. C.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.
Vol. 17.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, OCTOBER 1, 1888.	No. 9


THE AUTUMN.
Ah! this is the saddest time of the year,
Methinks I have heard, that the poets sing,
When days are gloomy, and landscape drear,
And the birds of passage are on the wing;
For the woods are brown and the fields are bare,
And the chill of Autumn is ev’rywhere.

I'll hie me away to some cosy nook,
’Mong the mossy rocks and the falling leaves,
And study awhile “Dame Nature’s book”
And gather a few of her choicest sheaves;
For this is her mellowest harvest time—
The season of sentiment, most sublime.

The hills are crowned with foliage gay,
Rich royal purple, brown, yellow and red;
But the skies are leaden, and dull and gray
And the summer flowr’s are lying dead;
Pale, withered garlands are round us strewn;
Yet a mantle of beauty is o’er them thrown.

 We think of the lov’d ones gone like the flow’rs,
Whose fragrance still lingers around the place;
And pensive and lonely are the hours,
As memorys’delicate lines we trace;
And the sere and yellow leaf entwine;
And lay the chaplet upon love’s shrine.

Oh; fanciful visions so far away,
Over the hills and the mountains too;
Farther and farthar my footsteps stray,
And quicker my heart doth the way pursue;
For the sunlight guides, and the shadows flee,
And the beautiful vista, is fair to see.

And I haste as it were with charmed feet,
While my heart beats high with passion wild,
List! I hear the music so soft and sweet,
I heard when a winsome, innocent child;
’Tis the rustle of leaves, and the sighing breeze,
And the murmur of voices ’mong the trees.

They whisper to me, and I answer too—
Though never a sound escapes from my lips
Yet, I know the message they bring is true
And I feel it thrill to my finger tips,
Ev’ry sense I possess affirms its truth,
And echoes back to the days of my youth.

Yes, the past with its gleam of love and light,
Its sunshine and shadow	its smiles and tears,
Has a rainbow of promise ever bright.
That lightens the path of my future years, 
And my faith grows stronger as time rolls by, 
That the goal I’m seeking is drawing nigh.

But I've flown so far in my dreamy flight,
That perhaps ’tis time to retrace my way;
I fear the present is lost to my sight,
Better come back to the scenes of to-day;
For mortals must deal with the things that are real,
Tho’ poets oft wander ’mid the ideal.

What’s this I’ve been dreaming here by the fire,
While in fancy I flew far o’er the hills? 
What symphony grand did my soul inspire, 
While a magic elixir my bosom thrills?
’Tis night and I’m sitting here quite alone,
And the beautiful visions I saw have flown.

I hear only the wierd and tragic wind,
As it whistles and shrieks and moans and sighs;
Ah, this is the potent power I find
Which brought me the genii, so wondrous wise
That opened the realm of poetic thought
And tender branches of evergreen sought.

These were remembrances loving and true,
Scattered at intervals over life’s way,
The pleasures we shared, the friends we know,
That are fresh and and green in our hearts to-day;
Fragments of beauty from out the dead past,
Which over our lives have a halo cast.
								E. B. W.


[Column 1]
FOREIGN LETTER.
Nottingham, July, 1888.
EDITOR MILLENNIAL STAR:
	DEAR BROTHER:— Among the events that
have recently taken place is one worthy of
mention in the columns of The Star. I refer
to the visit of my aunt, Sister Isabəlla M. 
Horne, to her native country (England), after
an absence of fifty-six years. She was accom-
panied by her sister, Harriet Ellis, of Wood's
Cross, Utah, and her son, Richard S. Horne.
	This venerable lady left her home in Salt
Lake City, Utah, on the 12th of May last, and
reached Glasgow on the 30th of the same month.
	During her stay in England she visited
Rainham, in Kent, her birthplace, went into
the old home of her girlhood, and attended
religious services in the old church, where she
was christened in February, 1819. Sister
Horne has availed herself of opportunities of
mingling with the Saints, particularly with the
sisters of the Relief Society, giving them good
counsel and words of encouragement, and re-
lating much of her experience among the
Saints from 1836 to the present. At London
she held quite a controversy with two of the
leading ladies of the woman's suffrage cause,
and succeeded in removing some prejudice and
imparting information concerning "Mormon-
ism," the "Mormons," and their beliefs and
practices, that was new light to them. 
One of the ladies was very curious to know
how polygamous families get along, and
pointedly asked Sister Horne if her husband
was a man of many wives. "My husband has
other wives," was the reply. With eyes wide
open, expressive of the greatest astonishment,
the lady asked, "How did you feel?" "Just as
you would feel," replied my aunt. "I am a
woman like yourself, and, but for my religious
convictions, would no more have consented for
my husband to take another wife than you 
would; but I know that the principle of plural
or celestial marriage is true and from God. 
God has spoken from the heavens, and raised
up the boy-prophet, Joseph Smith, to establish
His kingdom on the earth in these the last days,
and I have a testimony of the truth of these
things. I have had the honor of entertaining
the Prophet in my own home, and know that
he was a man of God." "Well, but you don't
expect to convert us to these things?" "Oh,
no, madame. If we wished to convert you to 
our religious belief, we would begin with the
first principles, not with the highest and most
exalted ones."
	At Shrewsbury one of the ladies whom Sis-
ter Horne met there stated that she had read a
book written against the "Mormons," in which
the declaration was made that President Brig-
ham Young was in the habit of murdering his
wives when he became tired of them. Sister
Horne looked the speaker in the face and asked,
"And did you believe such trash as that?" "I 
did, for I knew nothing to the contrary." "I
am surprised," said Sister Horne, "that any
sensible woman should credit such falsehoods.
No man loved his family more dearly than did
Brigham Young, and he was greatly beloved
by them." The lady expressed pleasure at
hearing these statements in emphatic refutation
of the outrageous slander. 
Mr. Editor, here was one of Utah's "down-
rodden" women, "escaped" from her home

[Column 2]
among the Mormons, defending their religion
and bearing testimony of its truth after fifty-
two years of personal experience among that
people, frequently saying she would be glad to 
return to her mountain home among the
Saints.
	Now let the bawling Jarman, or any other
defamer of Utah's women, forever hold his
peace; for here is a living rebuttal to his foul
aspersions in regard to them.
	No freer women live on the earth than the
women of Utah, and nowhere else are they
placed on such equality with men; nor is her
virtue more carefully and jealously guarded
than among the Mormons, as evidenced by the
testimony of Sister Horne. 
Your brother, etc.,
GEO G. HALES.
—Millennial Star.


DESCRIPTIVE LETTER. 

	MURRAY, Idado, Sept. 5, 1888.
*	*	We had to stop over at Umatilla
twenty-three hours at the most miserable hotel
one could imagine. All the folks, excepting
myself, strolled down to the banks of the
Columbia River, that runs not more than a
block or two from the hotel.
	Papa met us at Spokane; we stayed not more
than fifteen minutes, and what we could see of
the town from the depot, it looked like a very
pretty, as well as a very enterprising city. We
took the U.P. to the Couer-d'-alene Lake; it is
about an hour's ride. It was real early when
we landed; about half past five a.m. We had
breakfast and dinner on the steamer; it was
my first experience of the kind, and I enjoyed
it immensely, as did all the folks of our party.
How I wished every minnte you were there to
enjoy it with me.
	Oh, I never was on such a lovely lake—I
shall not go back on dear old Salt Lake, don't
for a moment think it, but this was so different.
The water is so very deep that it looks a dark
green color, and on every side the mountains
are covered with dense groves of pine, fir, cedar
and spruce. The captain of the steamer was
so very nice; took us all over it, and showed us
all the points of interest. After we leave the
lovely lake, we go up the Couer-d'-lene River,
which is just as beautiful as the lake in its way.
It bends and winds around the grand old moun-
tains like some great snake. When we were
about half way up the river, the captain
pointed out a large mountain as Medicine
Mountain; they say the Indians worship it, and
whenever they are sick they come here and
pray to it, thinking it will cure them. A very
strange idea, isn't it? but then, I think it is
quite reasonable, for Indians.
	Along the river's banks there are great piles
of wood, all chopped and split. The Indians
up here are quite industrious; they chop wood
all the time, and sell it to the white men.	*
	I must tell you, dear grandma, about the
old father that traveled with us from Spokane
to the old mission; he was the first white man
that ever came here; he is about eighty-four
years old; and came here fifty years ago. I
cannot at present recall his name, but he is
such a dear old man; the mission have done a
noble work with the red-men in this part of
the country. After we land we take the train
to Osborn, and about a mile or so from where


we start we come to the old mission; they say
the old mission house that was built when they
first came here has not a single nail in it; 'tis
made with wooden pins; I have seen many like
it illustrated in the Harper of the old mission
in Southern Cal. The old father's name is
Father Joset, it just now occurred to me.
	At Osborn we saw from a distance our future
home; that is, the building spot. It is thickly
covered with great tall pines, spruce and firs,
some over a hundred feet high, and at the back,
not many rods away, runs the river. There are
not many buildings there as yet, but as soon
as all build that have lots over there, we will
have quite a town. If you were well I'm sure
you would enjoy the trip over the mountains
from Osborn to Murray, which is about eighteen
miles. You wind first around one mountain
and then another, until it makes one almost
dizzy. Oh, the mountains, the mountsins, they
are so beautiful; our mountains at home are
grand, but these are really beautiful, with their
dense groves of trees and underbrush.
Murray has two streets only; Main St. runs
East and West; the other runs South into Main.
Murray is built in a small gulch, half a mile
across in the widest place. We have two daily
papers and one weekly; quite an enterprising
little place.
	I'm sure you would enjoy sitting out on our
porch, where you could hear the water rushing
down day and night; 'tis so cold at morn-
ing and evening, that one has to wear heavy
clothes.
	Very lovingly,
		VERONA.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
	LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL
		  BY M. J. TANNER.
		      [Continued.]
	Ithaca, Monday, May 25th, 1885. Went to
a Presbyterian meeting with my cousin yester-
day, and improved the time making acquaint-
ances among them. There are four sisters and
a niece, all maiden ladies, and not a member of
this branch of the family to transmit the name.
Uncle John Bessac's family will all be gone when
they pass away. They tell me many, many
things of historical interest, and give me the
likeness of my great uncle, Henry Bessac, and
related anecdotes of Col. Nichols, who was
their great-grandfather, and mine by one more
removal. During the Revolutionary War, in
which he took an active part, Grandfather
Bessac was at the house of his father-in-law,
Col. Nichols, when it was attacked by tories.
A shot came through the window, grazing his
head; failing to kill him, they entered and beat
him over his head, the scars of which he
carried the rest of his life. They showed me
a letter, of which I kept a copy, written a
short time before his death.
	Wednesday, May 27. Weather pleasant.
Get another letter from home; all is well.
Answer it, and go with Cousin Calista to see
the Cornell University, which is on the hill
just above the town. The stage takes us up
the hill, and we walk to the conservatory. The
grounds are very pleasant. We go through
the building, and admire the flowers, the loveli-
est I ever saw. Go over to the main building,
and see the museum. Such a variety of stuffed
animals in glass cases. The first we notice is a
Bengal tiger; measures twelve feet from his
nose to the tip of the tail; he is a handsome
animal, but I admire him just where he is. In
the same case is a wild cat and several speci-
mens of house cat, another tiger and some
skeletons that I cannot name. In another case
is a noble looking deer, a camel and calf camel,
some skeletons, and a double calf, two heads,
two tails, two sets of legs and one body. There
are monkeys, snakes and birds, and a very
[Column 2]
handsome black bear, with a history; he never
killed anybody. The tiger had a history, too;
he was a man eater. Bones of a mastadon and
fac similes of skeletons, without labels; two
enormous turtles—a man might crawl inside
them. In the next story we see curiosities
from the sea; many of them are fac similes
made of colored glass, and show us the beau-
ties of the ocean; sponge and coral in great
variety, and a great variety of birds, some of
them very beautiful. It is time to close, and
we have to leave without going to the next
story.
	Thursday, 29th. Visit the farm or country
seat at Brookside, on the bank of Geauga Lake.
The rocks here are slate, and the lake is very
clear and pretty. The youngest sister lives
here during the summer, and their brother's
widow is with her. I am much interested in
them, and have a pleasant visit. We stay all
night and come home on the 11 a.m. train.
Take dinner with Calista.
	Saturday, May 30. This is Decoration
Day, and is a holiday. It rained all the fore-
noon, but in the afternoon we see a great many
people going with flowers to the cemetery. To-
wards evening I go out for a ride with a friend
of my cousin's. I am looking for a letter from
my aunt at Lockport. I do not know the
name of my cousin's husband, I only know her
as Cousin Emily, and the address, which is
No. 111, Vine St., as I have addressed all
my letters to my aunt.
	Monday, June 1. I intended going to Lock-
port to-day, bot have received no letter. I
have looked among my papers, but find no
name, so I conclude to wait another day.
	Tuesday, June 2. Concluded to travəl on
and take the chance of finding Cousin Emily.
I left Ithaca on the 6 a.m. train, arrived at
Lockport at 12 m. Asked the hackman to 
take me to 111, Vine Street. "What name?"
said the man. "I do not know the name,"
said I, "I only know the number. The lady
I am to meet is Mrs. Bond, but I do not know
the name of the gentleman with whom she is
stopping, I only have the post office address,
which is No. 111, Vine St." "Upper Town or
Lower Town?" "I do not know." "What is
the gentleman's occupation?" "He is a rail-
road man." I gave him the check for my
trunk; he came back, saying it was all right; it
was Robert Duff, and he was looking for me.
I felt quite relieved, for it placəd me in such a
foolish situation. When I drove up Aunt
Permelia came out to meet me. She was very
glad to see me, and my cousin welcomed me
very kindly, and I soon got acquainted. After
tea Cousin Emily and her daughter took me
for a walk. We go to the bridge near the locks.
The freight train, which came in soon after I
did, tipped some of their cars off the bridge,
which is over a very deep ravine or gulf,
through which the canal passes. Fortunately
they were near the bank, and the damage is
not as much as it might otherwise have been.
It was caused through the breaking of a wheel.
No lives were lost, but it is a great deal of
work to get the cars back on the track again,
and is an inconvenience to other trains that
wish to pass. We went down on the locks and
watched a boat go through. It is certainly a
wonderful piece of engineering skill. It is
built with immense stones, and looks as if it
might defy the wear of ages. They are made
to carry boats up and down hill. They form
boxes in which the boat enters, when a gate is
shut down, and the water fills in until the boat
raises on a level with the next box or lock. It
is drawn in by means of a span of mules on
the tow path. Another gate is closed behind
it, which floats it to a level with the next lock.
So it goes to the top of the hill. The boats
come down on the other side by the same pro-
cess. One channel is to go up and the other
to go down. Between the channels is filled
[Column 3]
with solid masonry steps, leading from one level
to another. Stone steps lead down the bank,
and everything in the evening air is "stone
cold."
	We return to the house, stopping on the
way at a grocery store to make a few pur-
chases. The proprieter says he keeps free
lunch: " A glass of water and cold shoulder."


A PLEASANT PAYTY.

	On Saturday, Sept. 8th, 1888, a surprise
party was given by the 1st Ward Primary
Association of Richfield, in respect to Bishop
Poulson and Bro. Peterson, who were to start
for Provo the following Monday to stand their
trials for unlawful cohabitation.
	At 2  o'clock p.m. the Presidency and mem-
bers of the Association, their parents, together
with the leading brethren and sisters of the
city (making a total of 160), gathered at the
Social Hall, and awaited the arrival of the
brethren. After they came, the meeting was
opened with singing and prayer. There was
an appropriate programme, consisting of songs,
recitations, essays; etc., rondered by the child-
ren; also some interesting remarks, made by
Bishop Joseph S. Horne, Bro. N. C. B. Orrock,
Simon Christenson and W. G. Baker. Bishop
Poulson and Bro. Peterson expressed their
gratitude for the respect shown to them, and
thanked the people for the same; said they
were greatly surprised. They were presented
with a few dollars as a token of remembrance
from the Association.
	The tables were then spread with delicious
and various kinds of picnic, and  blessed by
Bro. Victor E. Bean. The people then par-
took of the refreshments.
	There was a special meeting called the same
afternoon, for the purpose of reorganizing the
Association. Stake Prest. Lucy Heppler pre-
sided. Sister Nancy M. Nebeker, who had
been laboring as President in the Association
for the past six years, and having had to con-
tend with very poor health for three years,
read her resignation, which was accepted, and
she was honorably released, having done a
very noble work, and being very much beloved
by the children.
	Sister Minnie A. Nebeker was put in as
President, with Ellen Oberg and Olena Olsen
as her Counselors.
	Sister Heppler gave instructions to the new
officers and children; thought Sister Nebeker
and her Counselors had done a very noble
work, and had been interested in the welfare of
the Association, and in teaching the children
true principles.			
	Bishop Poulson arose and remarked, that
President Nancy M. Nebeker and Coun. Ruth
Hayward had labored faithfully in the Prim-
ary, and had done a noble work, and would
receive a great reward for their labors.
	The officers were set apart to their different
offices, and the meeting was dismissed. Every
body returned to their homes feeling that they
had spent an agreeable and enjoyable after-
noon.
	On Sept. 22nd a surprise was given by the
new President and the Association to President
Nancy M. Nebeker and Coun. Ruth Hayward,
in honor of their faithful labors in the Primary.
Sister Nebeker was presented with a rug, and
Sister Hayward with a quilt. Other presents
were given to them, the rug and quilt being
made by the children. Picnic was partaken of,
and a nice programme rendered.
	Very respectfully,
		A. N.


He who comes up to his own idea of great-
ness must always have had a very low standard
of it in his mind.—Hazlilt.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


TO MRS. E. L. SAXON AND MRS.
CLARA B. COLBY.

	Sister Workers, in the cause
	Of God's mighty Truth and laws,
	May His Spirit guide and bless,
	Leading you to sure success.

	And it will, for Truth is strong,
	Right is right, and wrong is wrong;
	Error lifted ne'er so high,
	Must be crushed, must fall and die.

	Temperance, Purity and Love,
	Truth's own children, born above,
	Sent with Unity to earth,
	Hold the power of all true worth.

	And gainst these the world now fights,
	While in evil it delights,
	But Intelligence must win
	Power o'er ignorance and sin.

	Vile Intemperance must be bound,
	Liberty again be crowned,
	Freedom's enemies be slain,
	Truth and Justice rise and reign.

	Temperance Unity and Love;
	Virtue's offspring born above.
	These, the pure are fighting for,
	In the world's great moral war.

	Lo! the pathway must be paved,
	And our favored land be saved,
	Sister Workers, do your best
	GOD and Israel for the rest.

		LULA.

	In behalf of the ladies of the Latter-day Saints, Salt
Lake Assembly Hall, Tuesday, Sept. 25th, 1888.


[Column 1]

THE OLD GARRET.

	In the old-fashioned garret! Methinks, I
hear some one say rubbish! that's all there is
in a garret. Not so fast, gentle reader; allow me
to tell you that in the garret there is inspira-
tion for the muse, and for me there is romance
in the old New England garrets, which haunts
me still, and

		"Oft in the stilly night,
		Ere slumber's chain hath bound me, 
	Fond memory brings the light,
		Of other days around me."

And I find myself among the "old lumber" in
the selfsame garret where I played so much
when a child and I wrote my first poor verses,
at eight years old, and where I was continually
making new discoveries of old lace and rib-
bons for my dolls. There is as much poetry
in it as there is in the parlor, and more. Par-
lors are very prosy, prim affairs, and one is
always afraid of doing the wrong thing in one
of those old-fashioned ones, that are never
opened except on grand occasions; and never,
one might almost say, for children. I remem-
ber parlor chairs, with their straight, upright
backs, and funny, tall legs, used to seem to
stare at me, when a little girl I crept in softly
to take a peep around; and I said then, and I
maintain it still, that I would never shut up the
best room in the house and make it gloomy
and dark, to be used only for company.
	But if there is anything more inspiring than
a musty old garret, it is spring time, groves,
meadows brooks, waterfalls, woods, orchards
and the like, nature's haunts; these are all sug-
gestive and poetical, and especially when the
birds are singing their sweetest notes in the
tree tops, and the boughs nodding and swaying,
keeping a sort of irregular time to the un-
written music of the songsters. Ah, yes, apple-
blossoms, they come with the early spring, and
if I am going to talk of garrets, and old books,
and antiquities generally, and grandmothers,
and old maids, and love letters, I must shut
out the scent of lilacs; the violets and the pink

[Column 2]

and white apple-blossoms, and their dainty,
subtle perfume, or I shall wander off into
dreamland. I must confess that garrets are not
so exquisite or charming as these, but they have
a history, some of them at least.
	I do not know if there are such old houses
standing now, as there were in the country
towns in Massachusetts, thirty-five years ago,
but the pictures of them, outside and in, are
fresh in my mind to-day. I am very much
afraid most of them are remodeled, for this is
a stylish age we are living in; but when I was
a little girl there were many such romantic
places, and each one of them had a garret.
	In one of these, "long, long ago," a little
girl was playing; her dolls were finely arranged
in dainty bits of beautiful things, and she her-
self in such a funny gown (not dresses then,
but gowns), she had found in an old, old
dresser. It was gored in long, narrow strips,
and withal would scarcely fit her little figure,
but it was red and blue changeable silk, bright
and gay, heavy enough to stand alone, and the
short waist, and shorter sleeves, were trimmed
with very yellow lace, just ready to drop to
pieces with age. The child was a quaint one,
and the dress was queerer still—but a spell
seemed upon her, she was always thoughtful,
and at this particular time she was very pen-
sive. I don't mind telling you a few of her
thoughts. She might almost be called vision-
ary, for she seemed to see the future all in a
panorama, spread out before her, very much as
we who have lived long, look back over the
days that are past. She had been rummaging
more than usual, and trying to rake up some-
thing fresh; and she had succeeded, for away in
the furthermost corner of the garret, from un-
der the very eaves of the house, she dragged
out a budget of old papers, tied in bundles. So
sitting upon a broken stool, in her silk and
lace, she began to investigate these old papers.
First of all, she solilquized: "I like old things
that are pretty and have a history, like old
pictures and silks and laces, and beads, and
jewelry, that I hear the big girls talk so much
about. and I dearly love to play here in the
garret, and make believe I am a woman and
all that, but old papers like these are useless.
I can't see what people save them for, unless
they are deeds, or records, or valuables of that
description, and those are always locked up in
desks, and some man carries the key. Yes,
some man, I wonder why women don't? They
have the keys of the parlor and cellar and
pantry, and the big chest of drawers, where
valuable linen is kept, but then I suppose
women have nothing to do with papers, and
documents, and so on."
	But I am afraid I am telling you too much;
I musn't tell you all she thought and felt, but
merely say that she became so fascinated with
the contents of the papers, that she forgot the
curious way in which she was rigged out, and
kept on reading and reading until it began to
grow dim in the strange place, and all the
while through the open window came the
dainty perfume of the apple-blossoms, for it
was springtime then; O so many years ago, I
was about to tell you how many, but I think I
wont, or you might guess how old that little
girl is now, and that would be too bad, you
know, for women, I've heard, seldom like to
have their correct age known. By and by her
sister came to look for her, and made her take
off all her finery, and scolded her too for get-
ting herself so dusty and musty with the old
papers, and told her they were only rubbish
that ought to be burnt, but—(Well, we'll call
her Rosabel) didn't think so, and she went
back day after day, until it was a byword in
the old home, that—Rosabel lived in the gar-
ret. She didn't go there without a purpose,
though, she left off playing with dolls all of a
sudden, and forgot to dress herself up any
more, but she remembered to leave the window

[Column 3]

open to let in the sunshine and the sweet scent
of the lilacs and apple-blossoms, and hour
after hour she would sit so quietly, that the
stillness was almost painful, and yet no one
must disturb her.
	A light had dawned upon her in that out of
the way place, she had found out that women
sometimes put their thoughts upon paper, and
she conceived the idea of making rhymes, or
jingle; that was why she wanted to be so much
alone, and needed the fresh perfume from out-
side; it rested her, and helped clothe her
thoughts. A little at a time the secret leaked
out that Rosabel actually thought she could
write a book. Was this very absurd? Folks
seemed to think so, and her mother, who was
very practical, tried to convince her that little
girls didn't write books all at once, and there
was much good hard work aud study to be
done by way of preparation. That even her
grandmother had studied hard, and had a good
education before she attempted to write those
old love letters, which had so enchanted poor
Rosabel. "You are getting strange notions
into your head," she said to the child, "you
must go away to school and write compositions,
and they must be corrected, and after a long
time it may be that you can write something
readable." Poor Rosabel! how crest fallen!
all her glorious visions of fame were about to
be dispelled, for I will tell you another secret
now, her mother was strong-minded, and wanted
her daughter to be a woman, and not a senti-
mental wishy-washy novel writer. I can't tell
you all Rosabel's life history now, but some
day I am almost certain some little girl or
woman, poking about in a musty old garret will
find  it in manuscript.
		AUNT EM.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	If you want to lift me, you must be above
me.—Emerson.
	It is in length of patience and endurance
and forbearance, that so much of what is good
in mankind and womankind is shown—Arthur
Helps.
	There is a better thing to ask than exemp-
tion or escape from sorrows, even grace to bear
them rightly.—Dr. Maclaren.
	We often excuse our want of philanthropy
by giving the name of fanaticism to the more
ardent zeal of others.—Longfellow.
	You will find the mere resolve not to be use-
less, and the honest desire to help other people,
will, in the quickest and delicatest ways, im-
prove yourself.—Ruskin.
	The true Christian is like the sun, which
pursues its noiseless track and everywhere
leaves the effect of his beams in blessing upon
the world around him.—Luther.
	As men often declare that women are the
"salt of the earth," the quicker some of this
preserving element is used to purify politics the
better.—Geo. R. Scott.
	The government needs woman because she is
different from man, and views things from a
different standpoint, and only by the combined
counsel of the two can the best results be at-
tained.—Mrs. C. A. Dunham.
	Justice requires that women should vote in
"a government of the people, by the people,
and for the people," deriving its just powers
from the "consent of the governed." One-
half of the people cannot justly deprive the
other half of suffrage, and at the same time
tax them. Taxation without representation is
anti-American and unjust—Judge James
Baker.
