SALT LAKE CITY,	-	-	OCTOBER 1, 1888


VISIT OF MRS. SAXON AND MRS.
			COLBY.
	On the 23rd of September, Mrs. Elizabeth Lisle
Saxon, of Memphis, Tenn., and Mrs. Clara B.
Colby, of Beatrice, Neb., arrived in this city from
the North-West, where they had been for some
weeks traveling and lecturing, and withal, examin-
ing into the subject of woman suffrage in Wash-
ington Ter., and the disfranchisement of the
women citizens by the decision of the Supreme
Court of that Territory, which, as is now well
known, decided that the Territorial Legislature
had not the right to confer suffrage upon women.
In company with Dr. Pratt we met the ladies in
Ogden on the arrival of the Utah Northern, and
accompanied them to the city.  Mrs. Zina D. H.
Young and Mrs. Jane S. Richards met the party
of four at the depot with a carriage, and together
we drove to the residence of Hon. F. S. Richards,
where a delicious lunch was served in the best
style by the genial hostess, Mrs. Emily T. Rich-
ards, to the invited guests.  Mrs. Arthur Brown
was one of the party, and engaged the ladies,
newly arrived, in an animated discussion upon
their favorite topic, woman suffrage.  Hon. F. S. 
Richards was the only gentleman at table, but
bore the honors with becoming grace and dignity,
as is his custom, although it is always more or less
embarrassing to be the only gentleman among so
many ladies, and more especially when a majority
of them are accounted "blue stockings."
	In the afternoon Mrs. Saxon and Mrs. Colby
attended service in the Tabernacle; Elder John
Nicholson was the speaker, and his sermon was
one well calculated to impress the mind of the
stranger with the important truths so forcibly
enunciated.
	The same evening, at eight o'clock, as per pre-
vious announcement, these distinguished public
speakers addressed a good sized audience, in the
Salt Lake Theatre. Mrs. Saxon was the first
speaker, and occupied most of the time. Her
subject, taking it all in all, was social reform,
though one might say temperance, poiltics and
suffrage for women. The lady told much of the
condition and corruption of modern society, and
urged all to come forward and lend their aid in
suppressing vice, in every form, and in whatever
guise. Her appeal was eloquent and touching;
she has an excellent voice and a great command
of language, and expresses her thoughts and 
views clearly and emphatically.
	Mrs. Colby followed her, but in a somewhat
more logical vein; her manner is entirely different
from that of Mrs. Saxon, which makes a most 
pleasing variety, yet the two blend admirably.
Mrs. Saxon has the warm blood of the South in
her veins, which rises to fever heat when dwelling
upon exciting topics, and Mrs. Colby has the cool
and calm manners of the North. She reasons
more and is less emotional. Both are pleasing
[column 2]
in voice and manner, and it would be difficult to
choose which interests most, as they are both
equally earnest, sincere, and in love with their 
profession, besides being eminently qualified to
fill a position upon the public platform.
	Monday morning they were out viewing the
principal points of interest in the city, and ex-
pressed themselves much gratified with the loca-
tion of the city and its surroundings, considering
it, as most strangers do, a very charming valley.
Mrs Saxon had visited Salt Lake in the fall of
1882, and spent several weeks here, and had
cherished (so she stated) many pleasant remem-
brances af Utah, and the people whom she met
while sojourning for a little season among the 
Mormons.
	In the afternoon a reception was tendered the
ladies at the Gardo House, by a committee of
ladies here, in behalf of the women of Zion, and
as an appreciation of the labors and efforts of
these noble workers for the advancement of
women of all lands and climes, and the cause of
temperance, suffrage and social reform, of which
they are such able exponents. Between five and 
six hundred people had been invited, including
His Excellency Governor West, the Hon. Secre-
tary, Judges and U.S. officials, officers at Camp
Douglas and other prominent gentlemen and
ladies, non-Mormon, the authorities of the Church,
the Mayor and City Council, and others, in fact as
many as it was thought possible to introduce during
the three hours allotted to the reception, from two
to five p.m. The affair proved to be pleasant, so
far as one can judge, and certainly there was
nothing wanting on the part of the committee to
make it all that a public reception could be when
hurriedly arranged. It gave the lady visitors an
opportunity to exchange greetings with a larger
number of people than would have otherwise been
possible, and it is hoped that they carried away
some pleasing recollections of their sojourn here,
though it was very brief.
	About half-past five the doors were closed, and
the ladies in whose honor the reception had been
given, with a few invited guests, assembled in the
dining room and partook of refreshments pre-
pared by the committee. It was somewhat in-
formal, yet withal very pleasant; the conversation
was general, and an hour or two was spent in a 
most agreeably social way, chatting of the day,
the people and the impressions made.
	It is, perhaps, out of the usual order to write up
a matter of this kind, and not mention the ladies'
elaborate toilettes, entering into the details of
color, style, and other feminine specialties, but it
must be remembered that this was a reception
given to two very strong-minded representative
women, and, therefore, the writer begs to be ex-
cused for not telling the public who were the most
handsomely dressed ladies, what they wore, and
so on, but we will say that there were a large
number of very interesting elderly ladies, really
matronly, beautiful younger women, and charming
young girls.
	The flowers with which the parlors and ante-
rooms were decorated were a pretty feature of the
gathering, and added, as flowers always do, to the
beauty and grace of the house and its appoint-
ments. There was some music on the piano by
one or more of the young ladies—Misses Dwyer
and Folsom, who excel in the divine art—and a 
song or two by Mrs. Nellie Druce Pugsley, who
has a rare, sweet voice, and one that touches the
tender emotions of the human soul. Her selec-
tions are always choice and in good taste, and
her style of rendering her music is as winning as
her voice is melodious and sympathetic.
	In the evening at eight o'clock the speakers
were again on the platform in the theatre, with
rather a larger audience before them than on the
evening previous. Mrs. Colby was the first
speaker; she gave an interesting account of the
[column 3]
way in which the ballot had been unjustly wrested
from the women in Washington Territory, and
attributed it on the whole to the whiskey element.
She made some very pertinent and strong points
in favor of the rights and privileges of the women 
in that Territory, that would apply with equal, if
not greater force, to the women of Utah. Her
discourse was full of good, sound arguments from
beginning to end, and no one, it would seem,
could listen to such sentiments set forth, without
feeling that the world of women was awakening to
a higher appreciation of woman's latent powers,
and the possibilities of a more glorious future,
when men and women should stand side by side
with a better comprehension of all the great un-
derlying questions that must be solved, before
the establishment of a "government for the peo-
ple and by the people," Mrs. Saxon's discourse
was of woman in the past, going back to the days
of Scripture, and the days of Rome and Greece,
and giving many incidents in the history of na-
tions and kingdoms, in which women figured
prominently. She dwelt much upon the sacrifices
and heroism of women, and especially of mothers,
and the injustice and ingratitude of men, yet sum-
ming up the whole matter, she threw the great
responsibility of much that is wrong in modern
society upon the women "who have all the rights
they want," and dwell in fine houses and happy
homes, indifferent to the wrongs that cry so loudly
for the help and redress, that might come if all
such women would put forth a helping hand, in
the great moral warfare that has begun, instead of 
sinking down in helpless weakness, content be-
cause their own life has been "cast in pleasant
lines."
	Tuesday morning, at ten o'clock there was a
meeting for women only, in the Assembly Hall,
the subject, "Social Purity," the very topic on
which Mrs. Saxon succeeded in making a sensa-
tion in Washington, during the International
Council. The main body of the building was
pretty well filled though the house would cer-
tainly have been crowded, had there been more
time to circulate the notice. Mrs. Saxon had
several times expressed a wish to hear the hymn,
"O Zion," sung, so the meeting opened with the
hymn, "O ye mountains high," etc, chorus, "O
Zion." A very earnest prayer was offered by
Sister Zina D. H. Young, after which Mrs.
Saxon addressed the ladies on the subjetc an-
nounced for her lecture.
	It would be utterly impossible to give even a
brief synopsis of her remarks here, but may here-
after give some items from it; however it was a 
most fitting theme for the mothers and daughters
gathered there, and was presented in a way that
will be sure to produce earnest thought in the
minds of those, who have not given much attention
to this important subject, and at any rate Mrs.
Saxon described pathetically the vices and crimes
that exist to such an alarming extent in the out-
side world, which must have been new to most of
the young women present. Mrs. Colby spoke
briefly but not with the enthusiasm manifested
in the former speaker. As she is a much younger
woman and with less experience in the evils that
are prevalent in great cities, she could not be ex-
pected to have the same zeal and fervor in pre-
senting the picture, as Mrs. Saxon, who has had
thirty years experience in dealing with this and
other weighty matters, that touch the vital forces
of the hearts of every true mother in the land.
Mrs. Colby is very practical and reasons admira-
bly; she likes to get at the bottom of things, and
weigh and measure them. She spoke to the 
ladies about her paper, the Woman's Tribune,
of which she is the editor and manager, and a very
able and efficient one she is too. During the time
of the International Council and Convention
of the National Woman Suffrage Association last
Spring, she published her paper daily in Washing-
ton, D. C., giving everything pertaining to


that remarkable event and the speeches of the
ladies and papers read in full.
	The Woman's Tribune is the official organ
of the National Woman Suffrage Association and
is reliable authority on all questions connected
with this movement. Mrs. Colby has good 
reason to be proud of her paper, and as it is a
national affair it is of course well supported.
She succeeded in obtaining quite a number of
subcribers in this city and also in Ogden when
she spoke there after leaving Salt Sake.
	A point inadvertently touched upon by Mrs.
Saxon in her discourse aroused the fire of Mor-
monism in the hearts of many Mormon women,
and seemed to call for expression in reply,
therefore when Mrs Colby resumed her seat. Sis-
ter Zina in her dignified motherly way rose and
bore a powerful testimony to the truth of the Gos-
pel, and the divinity of the mission of Joseph
Smith.  Her language was eloquent and her emo-
tions were so intensified that she poured forth her
words like a volume.  It was impromptu, but it was
all the more electrifying, and it told upon the audi-
ence in tones not to be mistaken.  Mrs. Saxon
was evidently deeply moved, and requested the
privilege of closing the meeting with prayer.
Mrs. Charlotte Ives Kirby occupied a few mo-
ments, but the spell of Sister Zina's simple and
powerful eloquence was so effective that the in-
fluence could not be easily dispelled.  The Dox-
ology  was sung and Mrs. Saxon offered in
language most sublime and eloquent a fervent and
heartfelt prayer.  In our next issue we will give
the trip to Salt Lake, on Tuesday, afternoon of a 
party of ladies and gentlemen, and the visit to the
Industrial Home on Wednesday morning prior to
the departure of the ladies on Wednesday after-
noon.
	An amusing instance of the power of the im-
agination is related in a New Orleans paper as an
experiment of a Dr. Durand.  The physician
wished to test the practical effect of mind-disease
and gave a hundred patients a dose of sweetened
water.  "Fifteen minutes after, entering apparent-
ly in great excitement, he announced that he had
by mistake given a powerful emetic, and prepara-
tions must be made accordingly.  Eighty out of
the hundred patients became thoroughly ill, and
exhibited the usual result of an emetic: twenty
were unaffected.  The curious part of it is, that,
with very few exceptions, the eighty 'emeticized'
subjects were men, while the strong-minded few,
who were not to be caught with chaff, were
women."—Ex.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


"LIFE OF JOSEPH SMITH."
	The new work, entitled, the "Life of Joseph
Smith," has just recently been published at the
office of the Juvenile Instructor.  Apostle Geo.
Q. Cannon is the author, and one need hardly say
it is exceptioually well written, when the author's
name is given.  His ability as a journalist, editor
and author, is well established, and the Latter-day
Saints, who so love the memory of Joseph, their
beloved martyr-prophet, and his brother, Hyrum,
the martyr-patriarch, will, we are sure, have great
pleasure and satisfaction in reading and possess-
ing a book containing their history, recorded by
one whose accuracy in all matters of historical
data can be safely relied upon.  We give below
the author's preface and it speaks for itself in
tones not to be mistaken of the genuineness and 
truthfulness of the history.  No writer could have
a more sublime theme than the man (Joseph Smith
the Prophet), and the work established by him in
the dispensation of the fulness of times.
			AUTHOR'S PREFACE.
	"Joseph and Hyrum are now dead; but, like the
first martyr, they yet speak.  Their united voice
is one of testimony, admonition and warning to
the world.


"They lived men of God. They died pure and
holy, sealing their testimony with their blood.
	"No men ever suffered greater persecution than
they; no men were ever less understood by their
generation.
	"It is in the hope that the Saints may find joy in
reading of their beloved Prophet and Patriarch,
and that the world may judge more fairly of these
benefactors of mankind, that this book is writ-
ten.
	"To the Author its preparation has been a lov-
ing duty. In the midst of a somewhat busy and
laborious life, he has found comfort in the con-
templation of this great subject. The closing
chapters, detailing the final sufferings upon earth
of the Prophet of God and his ever-constant
brother, were finished in a prison for adherence
to the principles which they taught, and for this,
the Life is invested with a dearer regard. To
send the work away now is like being torn from
a beloved companion when most the solace of his
friendly presence is needed.
	"In some respects this volume may be imperfect;
the circumstances which surrounded its prepara-
tion were not favorable to the collection and
arrangement of materials. But it is believed to be
truthful and just.
	"To many friends the Author is indebted for
information here embodied; and he takes this oc-
casion to thank them, hoping to live yet to meet
them and express his gratitude in the flesh.
	"That the sublime example and inspired teach-
ings of Joseph, the Prophet of the Last Dispensa-
tion, may be of eternal benefit to all who read this
Life, is the heart-felt wish of
		"THE AUTHOR.
	Utah Penitentiary, Oct. 1, 1888.


EDITORIAL NOTES.
	SISTER ZINA D. H. YOUNG, President of the Re-
lief Society, has returned recently from her visit to
the North-west. Sister Zina left the city last
Spring, April 24, and on her trip via. Portland
visited her son Lieut. W. Young remaining there
a few days, then proceeding to Alberta, which she
reached on the 7th of May, after a pleasant and
happy season spent with her beloved daughter,
Zina Y. Card, who, by the by, has a little girl,
born while "Aunt Zina" was there, and named Zina.
and other friends, she bade good-bye for a little
time, and returned home by way of Montana,
reaching this city on the 16th inst. in excellent
health and spirits to renew her labors here in the
important mission to which she has been called
and assigned.
	ON Tuesday, 18th of September, in company
with Sisters M. I. Horne and B. W. Smith we at-
tended the Annual meeting at Sandy, Mrs. W.
Olsen, presiding. The report of the last year and
minutes of the last yearly meeting were read and
showed their affairs to be in good condition. At
noon a sumptuous dinner was served in the So-
cial Hall of which about a hundred people par-
took. At the afternoon meeting the officers of
the stake and ward were presented and sustained.
A good spirit prevailed and much good instruc-
tion was imparted during the day. Several breth-
ren were present and those who spoke gave the
sisters every encouragement possible.
	THE Fair, which will open in this city on the
third of this month (October), will be quite an
 event in the history of Utah, as it gives promise
of being of much importance to those interested
in building up the Territory, and to display its in-
dustries, manufacturies and specimens of its re-
sources also. The building is a substantial one
of handsome brick, and well designed for its
purpose. As the time of the opening of the Fair
draws near, much enthusiasm on the subject is
perceptible. The directors are certainly energetic
and persevering, and are methodically arranging
[ COLUMN 2]
and carrying out the desired purpose. Success
to the Fair and to those who are doing the head
work, and so forth.
	ON the 25th of August, in company with Sisters
P. L. Kimball and E. S. Taylor, we attended the
annual meeting of the Relief Society of Union.
Mrs. E. Richards is the venerable President, and
Mrs. M. A. Smart a most able and efficient Secre-
tary. The annual report and minutes of last
annual meeting were read, and some other busi-
ness transacted. The reports showed the Society
to be in a flourishing condition. Much good in-
struction was given. At noon  there was a dinner
prepared for a number of the guests at the residence
of Coun. M. H. Brady. Another meeting was
held in the afternoon, at which much encourage-
ment was given by Coun. E. S. Taylor and other
visitors, and many testimonies were borne by the
local sisters. The Bishop was present at the
meeting, and addressed the sisters in the after-
noon, instructing them in the performance of their
duties, and encouraging them in the good work of
relieving the poor and comforting the downcast,
etc. Sister Kimball, in her remarks dwelt much
upon the early days, when Joseph the Prophet
was among the people, his teachings to her and
others.
	ON Monday, the 17th of September, Apostle
Geo. Q. Cannon was sent to the Penitentiary,
having voluntarily given himself up, knowing that
an indictment had been found against him for un-
lawful cohabitation, long before, on which account
he had been a long time in exile. This is the
second time an Apostle has been sentenced under
the Edmunds law. The first of the Apostles
to suffer imprisonment was Bro. Lorenzo Snow,
a veteran in the ranks of the Twelve. It seems a
remarkable thing, that in free and enlightened
America, men should be punished as criminals
because of the exercise of their religious convic-
tions; men with whom no fault could be found ex-
cept carrying out a principle given as a command-
ment from the Almighty. Yet such are the facts
before the world. Geo. Q. Cannon in the Con-
gress of the United States, when a Delegate from
Utah, was recognized there by members and
others, ladies and gentlemen of culture and re-
finement, and of the highest social standing, as a
man free from every appearance of evil; exem-
plary in word and deed, and in deportment.
Every person who approached him or associated
with him, felt this, and instinctively knew him to
be an upright, conscientious man. It was the
good fortune of the writer to hear such testimony
from a number of people in the city of Washington,
both from men and women, when they spoke of
the Utah Delegate. Now, instead of a seat in
the house of Congress, he occupies a prison cell,
but his heart is as pure, and his life as blameless
as when he mingled with the nation's statesmen,
and the love of the people will be increased. The
prayers of the Latter-day Saints, and their child-
ren, the Sunday School children of Zion, are
offered daily in his behalf to the God of Israel,
and He will answer them in blessing upon his
head, and his prison life will be cheered, and the
time seem short to him, by reason of the mighty
faith exercised in his behalf. Be of good cheer,
for "the Lord will comfort Zion."


PAST, PRESENT AND FUTURE.

"The path of duty is the only path of safety."

	I speak of the past with my heart filled with
emotion and gratitude, that our Heavenly Father
permitted us in this dispensation to be blest with
Sister Eliza R. Snow Smith to dwell with us to a
good old age; she who has ever been faithful and
true even to the end, diligent in seeking to establish
the cause of truth, and ever encouraging her sis-
ters to persevere and press forward in the work.


How nobly she bore her trials! knowing He in
whom she trusted, would do all things well. Her
blessed presence in person is now remembered
with the past, but we have her grand example with
us still.
	At the present time we have nine of her books,
and oft we have in words the fragrance of the
silent emotions of her soul when alone commun-
ing with her Maker, and sipping from the fountain
of life, to bless and cheer others while struggling
along, and to lead them on to victory.
	The present day is of eternal worth to us all,
having had the privilege of having time allotted
to us to make our record of obedience, like our
Elder Brother, to the laws of life eternal, that
we may know by contrast a fullness of glory.
	For the future, my dear sisters, I trust our re-
cord may grow brighter, although trembling with
weakness, as experience gives us a knowledge of
the power there is in love and union, and seeking
the best good of all, in attending to our daily
duties.
	And that nothing may hinder our attaining to a
fulness of celestial glory, is the prayer of,
		ZINA D. H. YOUNG,
	President of all the Relief Society.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


ANNIVERSARY.

	Salt Lake City, October 1st, 1888. This is the
anniversary of my father, Wm. Huntington, and
family of seven leaving their home in Watertown,
New York, to gather to Kirtland with the Saints.
There are still three of the number remaining in
the Church, living in Utah. Fifty years to-day
since we went into Adam-ondi-Ahman. Also the
same day of the month, in 1855, I moved into the
Lion House.
	Many are the incidents this day brings to mind,
but many more joys than sorrows on this our up-
ward march.
		Z. D. H. Y.


VISIT TO FARMINGTON PRIMARY.

	In company with Sister Ellen C. Clawson, on
Saturday, Sept. 29, I went to Farmington to attend
the Primary Conference and Fair.
	In the morning we passed through the Court
House. It was surprising to see what the ingen-
uity and industry of the children had accom-
plished in the useful and ornamental articles they
had spread there for gratified parents and friends
to look upon. One room was devoted especially
to the offerings to the Deseret Hospital from the
Davis County children; the many cans fiiled
yearly, dried fruit, etc., and a nice cheese, made 
by a little boy, for which please accept thanks on
behalf of the board.
	Sister Aurelia Rogers, the County Prest. of P.
A., must have been highly gratified with her able
assistants and the kindly presence of the Bishop,
Counselors and parents. A good spirit prevailed,
and we had a very enjoyable time.
	 	Z. D. H. Y.


TERRITORIAL CONVENTION.

	A Territorial Convention of the People's Party
s hereby called to convene at the City Hall, Salt
Lake City, on Monday, the 8th day of October,
A. D., 1888, at 12 o'clock m., for the purpose of
nominating a candidate for delegate to the Fifty-
first Congress.
	The number of delegates to compose the Con-
vention had been apportioned to the several coun-
ties as follows, to wit:
	Beaver County, 2; Box Elder, 3; Cache, 6;
Davis, 3; Emery, 1; Garfield, 1; Iron, 2; Juab, 2;
Kane, 2; Millard, 2; Morgan, 1; Piute, 1; Rich 1;
Salt Lake 16; San Juan, 1; Sanpete, 6; Sevier, 2;


Summit, 2; Tooele, 2; Uintah, 1; Utah, 9;
Wasatch, 1; Washington, 2; Weber, 6. Total,
75.
	The County Central Committees are requested
to take immediate steps in their respective coun-
ties for the election of delegates to the Territorial
Convention.
	By order of the People's Territorial Central
Committee.
		ELIAS SMITH, Secretary.

immediately either with Dr. E. R. Shipp, 16 Main

CLIFTON, IDAHO.

	Minutes of Relief Society meeting, held
May 3rd, 1888, for the purpose of reorganizing
our Society. Our worthy Prest., Jane M.
Howell, having resigned on account of her
having removed from the ward for a short
time. The visitors present were Stake Prest.
Elizabeth Fox, of Franklin, Sister Lewis and
Sister Crawshaw, of Oxford; local officers,
Bishop W. F. Garner, Counselors John S.
Bingham and Martin Hendersen, Prest. Jane
M. Howell and Counselors.
	After Prest. Howell had resigned her office,
the Society was reorganized, with D. E. Dudley,
President; S. E. Bingham and Janette Davis,
Counselors; M. A. Garner, Secretary; E. M.
Henderson, Ass't Secretary; Amelia J. Dixon,
Treasurer.
	We had an enjoyable time, and much good
and timely instruction was given by all the
speakers present.
		M. A. GARNER, Secretary.

BEAR LAKE STAKE.

	The Relief Society of the Bear Lake Stake
held their Twentieth Semi-annual Conference
at St. Charles, July 7th and 8th, 1888, Prest.
Julia P. Lindsay presiding. Saturday 10 a.m.
Singing, "Come, ye children of the Lord."
Prayer by W. M. Allred. Singing. Roll
called. Minutes of previous conference read
and approved.
	Prest. Julia P. Lindsay made a few opening
remarks. Reported the Societies as being in a
good condition, as far as she knew.
	The presidents of the different branches of
the Society gave in their verbal reports, which
were favorable.
	Coun. E. Collings made a few remarks, in
which she plainly set forth some of the duties
pertaining to the Latter day Saints, and showed
the good results of obedience to God's laws.
	Annie Laker, Stake Prest. of the Primary
Association, referred to some of the duties
resting upon the members of the Relief So-
ciety.
	Closing remarks were made by Mrs. Lewis
and W. M. Allred. Singing, "Love at home."
Benediction by Elder Noall.
	Sunday, 2. p.m.: Singing, "When trials
shall be no more," etc. Prayer by Prest. Geo.
Osmond. Singing, "I know that my Redeemer
lives." Sacrament was then administered.
	Prest. Julia P. Lindsay addressed the con-
gregation a short time. In her remarks she
spoke of the duties that rest upon mothers in
regard to teaching their children the principles
of the Gospel. Desired the sisters to be dili
gent in their labors to assist in building the
Tabernacle; also in storing grain.
	Prest. Geo. Osmond made a few very appro-
priate remarks.

[Column 2]

	Prest. James H. Hart said, "We are all
members of the Church of God, and there is a
labor for every one to perform." Spoke of
the progress the Saints are making, of the
beautiful cities and towns they have built, and
of the work there is still to be done. 
Choir sang an Anthem. Conference ad-
journed until the 2nd Saturday and Sunday in
January. Benediction by Bishop John Hunt.
		LOTTIE PRICE, Cor. Sec.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


EDITORIAL PARTY.
	On the 12th of September a party of editors
from Dakota, accompanied by a number of
ladies, while on a flying visit to our city, visited,
among other points of interest, the Tabernacle,
and listened with surprise and rapture to the
melody of the grand organ, produced by the
skillful playing of Joseph J. Daynes; after
which they took  the afternoon train for Gar-
field Beach, to enjoy the Lake and its attrac-
tions. A short ride of questioning expectancy, 
and then with eager haste they secured their
dressing rooms, and soon after were in the water,
some advancing with noisy splashing and ven-
turesome dives, while others proceeded with
more cautious efforts; but all fear soon vanished
in the persuasive luxury of the softly lapping
waves.
	A gladsome picture is presented by the
groups of happy faces, the bright and laughing
eyes of the pleasure seekers, as they dip and
float on the bosom of the buoyant lake; peals
of laughter and gleeful cries of delight float
out on the air, until the onlooker above in the
pavilion merrily smiles from very sympathy.
	When at length the delicious pastime has
been sufficiently indulged in, the pavilion
afforded the next attraction, and the graceful
waltzers kept time to the enticing strains of
music from the uniformed band, which accom-
panied the party; others, too, engaged in con-
versation, the "Saints" and their city being
the main topics. Through the courtesy of Mr.
Davis, manager of the excursion party, we re-
ceived introductions to quite a number, and an
animated colloquy followed; curious but cour-
teous were the visitors in regard to the "very
peculiar" religion of the Latter-day Saints.
When "all aboard" was announced, regret
was expressed that time would not permit of
further conversation, and a cordial invitation
was given, and accepted, to meet again in the
evening, when the broken thread of afternoon
converse was taken up and pleasantly spun
around the room. A moonlight drive with
three of the party, was not the least pleasant
feature of the day's diversion.
	Among those whose brief acquaintance we
shall pleasantly remember is, Mrs. Irene
Adams, a literary lady, who desired to see a
copy of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT, and received
a promise to have some sent to her address;
also Mr. Davis, Prest. of the Dakota Press
Association, Mr. Bliss and wife, Prest. of the
Dakota Board of Agriculture, Mr. and Mrs.
Alonzo Wardell, Mr. and Mrs. Bronsen, and
others, all of editorial repute, while not least
among them was Reverend Mr. Murray, the
"Cow Boy Preacher" of Dakota.


Withal; it is surprising the mistaken and
improbable notions that many people visit us
with. Surely a wide missionary field is open
to give the truth to those who come in our
midst, as well as those in whose midst we go, 
and thereby remove much prejudice, that
many people carry with them after visiting
our beautiful and prosperous city.
					AUNT POLLY.


HISTORICAL PICTURE.

TO MY FRIENDS:
	Some years ago; while reading the autobiog-
raphy of that noble apostle, Parley P. Pratt,
I was deeply impressed with the scene described
in the paragraphs I will hereafter transcribe,
and at different times, amid my many duties
and cares, my spirit has held communion with
itself, holding up to the eyes of my soul the
picture upon that page of his existence.
	Its pathos and grandeur have lingered when
I have striven to put them aside, and have fol-
lowed me when I have turned to other things.
	Often has that spirit picture seemed to lean
across the space and silence of the years that
have intervened since it transpired, and reach-
ing to the present time, seemed to say; "Who
will embody this midnight's story with shape,
that shall stand forth in power, that by its own
eloquence and truth shall live again and eter-
nally in the hearts of our children, and all
who hereafter shall learn the story of Joseph,
the Prophet!"
	An author's words, however glorious in their
power and beauty, often miss reaching the
heart of the general reader, as gems may be
overlooked among commoner stones; but sculp-
ture and pictures have reached the souls of
even those who cannot read. Even the rabble
in great cities know much of history by monu-
ments alone.
	Among the many scenes of Joseph's life,
more wonderful than fiction, this one, in which
he used the majesty and might of his priest-
hood's authority (almost God-like in the hu-
man), stands perhaps without a rival.
	During the past winter I began work upon
this subject, and after an interruption of three
weeks (caused by a broken wrist), resumed and
completed a pencil drawing. This I took to
Mr. Dan Weggeland, and engaged him to re-
produce the same in a painting, from which
large photographs will be taken.
	While working upon it, Mr. Weggeland
said: "It is the finest thing that has ever been
brought before this people." And he has
spared none of his own genius to do justice to
the subject.
	The picture is the prison scene, lighted by a
lantern. The ruffian guards occupy one side;
Joseph, and Parley, who slept beside him, are
the central figures. Among the other impris-
oned Saints will be recognized Hyrum, Amasa,
Sydney Rigdon and his daughter, Mrs. Robin-
son, with her infant, who was permitted to
nurse and care for her father and husband.
	I have endeavored to portray the moment
when Joseph could no longer endure the taunts
of those vile men, and in the kingly majesty of
his priesthood rebuked them. I have sought
carefully to have the picture one of historical
fidelity, and to faithfully represent the spirit
and emotions of the weary and suffering Saints,
also the terror of the Missouri guards, as de-
scribed by Parley: "In one of those tedious
nights we had lain in sleep until the hour of
midnight had passed. I had listened till I
had become so disgusted, shocked, horrified,
and so filled with the spirit of indignant justice
that I could scarcely refrain from rising upon
my feet and rebuking the guards, but had said
nothing to Joseph or anyone else, although I
lay next to him and knew he was awake. On
a sudden he arose to his feet, and spoke in a
voice of thunder, or as the roaring lion, utter-


ing, as near as I can remember, the following
words:
	" 'SILENCE, ye fiends of the infernal pit.
In the name of Jesus Christ I rebuke you and
command you to be still.  I will not live an-
other minute and hear such language.  Cease
such talk, or you or I die THIS INSTANT!'
	"He ceased to speak.  He stood erect in
terrible majesty. Chained, and without a 
weapon; calm, unruffled and dignified as an
angel, he looked upon the quailing guards,
whose weapons were lowered or dropped to the
ground, whose knees smote together, and who,
shrinking into a corner or crouching at his
feet, begged his pardon, remaining quiet until
a change of guards.
	"I have seen the ministers of justice clothed
in magisterial robes and criminals arraigned
before them, while life was suspended upon a
thread, in the Courts of England; I have wit-
nessed a Congress in solemn assembly to give
laws to nations; I have tried to conceive of
kings, of royal courts, of thrones and crowns
and emperors assembled to decide the fate of
kingdoms; but dignity and majesty have I seen
but once, as it stood in chains, at midnight, in a
dungeon in an obscure village of Missouri.—
Autobiography of Parley P. Pratt, page 229.
	The title of my picture, for which I have
duly made entry for copyright is:
	JOSEPH REBUKING THE GUARD.
	At Richmond Jail, Ray Co., Missouri.
	Should you honor me with your subscription
and the work hereafter fail to meet your ap-
probation, I will return the amount you ad-
vance to assist me.
	Respectfully,
	AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.
	South Bountiful, Davis Co., Utah,
	August 25, 1888.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


A SPLENDID OFFER.

	The Salt Lake Herald is adopting a novel
and praiseworthy method of securing to its
several editions subscribers. Arrangements
have been made to furnish all those who take
either the Daily, Semi-Weekly or Weekly or
Sunday editions of the Herald with a sewing
machine at one-half to one third the usual
prices. To assure the public that no fear may
be entertained of this machine, it has been
tested by W. H. Rowe, Esq., Superintendent
of the Z. C. M. I. shoe factory, with leather,
heavy cloth and fine goods, and has proven
thoroughly satisfactory. Each machine has
been thoroughly tested, and is supplied with
a complete set of attachments. Four styles
are carried. The cheapest, including the
Daily Herald for one year, is $27.50; including
the Semi-Weekly Herald, $20.50; including the
Sunday Herald, $20.00; including the Weekly
Herald, $19.50.
	If $6.50 more be added to each of these
figures, it will show the value of the dearest
machine, including the papers.
	Full particulars can be had on application
for information.
	It also appears that the Herald Company
have arranged for an extensive drawing of
valuable premiums in connection with the
Semi-Weekly during the coming week. Over
1,000 premiums are promised. The Herald is
evidently up to the times.


[Column 1]
		NOTES AND NEWS.
	
	There are four Siamese ladies studying in
England.
	New York city has ten reformatories for the
rescue and help of fallen women. Fifty years
ago there was not one.

[Column 2]
	Princess Eugene of Sweden has sacrificed
her family jewels to build a  hospital for crip-
ples upon an island off the coast.
	The Empress of Japan is rapidly becoming
one of the best-informed women of her time.
She is a diligent student of German, Russian,
French, and Italian.
	The Directors of the Western Pennysylvania
Medical College in Pittsburg, Pa., have de-
clared against the admission of women as stu-
dents, and refused the applications of six young
women.
	The Association for the Advancement of
Women, better known as the Woman's Con-
gress, will hold its annual meeting November
14, 15 and 16, with preliminary meeting on
the evening of the 13th, at Detroit, Mich.
	Mrs. Elizabeth Cady Stanton has come out
for the Prohibition Party. She says: "In a
critical review of the different party platforms,
I find more promise of social reorganization in
the Prohibition platform than in either of the
others."
	Another woman has passed her examination
and will soon be admitted as a member of the
Suffolk County (Boston) bar. When admitted,
Miss Mary A. Green will be the second Massa-
chusetts woman lawyer, Miss Lelia J. Robin-
son being the first.
	"The Prevention of Prostitution" was the
theme of an able and suggestive paper, pre-
sented by Dr. Rebecca C. Hallowell at the
thirteenth annual meeting of the Alumnae As-
sociation of the Woman's Medical College of
Pennsylvania.
	Cremation of  garbage is to be practiced in
Minneapolis, a furnace for the purpose hav-
ing been authorized by the council. This is
certainly the right way and the only sensible
way to dispose of substances which, uncon-
sumed, pollute the air and water of cities.—Ex.
	An informal reception was given Mrs. Eliza-
beth Cady Stanton by her Chicago friends, at
the Sherman House, on the afternoon of Sept.
2. Addresses were made by Mrs. Stanton, the
Baroness Gripenberg, who was in the city on
her return from the Pacific Coast, and Judge
Waite.
	Clara Barton has given $1.000 out of her
own pocket to the yellow fever sufferers. As
President of the American Association of the
Red Cross, she has been appealed to by the
Jacksonville Board of health to establish a 
headquarters there, and she has given the
necessary orders to the organization throughout
the country to come to the aid of the stricken
city.
	The Chicago Central W. C. T. U. carries on
two day-nurseries (where an aggregate of over
14,000 children were cared for last year), two
kindergartens, two industrial schools, two medi-
cal dispensaries, a waif's Sabbath School, a
nightly gospel Temperance meeting, a free
lodging house for men, where 60,000 were
lodged during the year, and a lunch-room just
opened.
	The poet Whittier is a sweet-souled Quaker,
but even Quakers decline to be slandered. Re-
garding the recent charge of a Southern news-
paper, that Mr. Whittier was an enemy to the
South, the venerable poet recently wrote a
friend: "I was never an enemy to the South or
the holders of slaves. I inherited from my
Quaker ancestry hatred of slavery, but not of
slave-holders. To every call of suffering or
distress in the South I have promptly responded
to the extent of my ability. I was one of the
very first to recognize the rare gift of the
Carolinian poet, Timrod, and I was the intimate

[Column 3]
friend of the lamented Paul H. Hayne, though
both wrote fiery lyrics against the North. I
am sure no one rejoices more heartily than I
do at the prosperity of the Southern States."—
Ex.

	The highest exercise of charity is charity
toward the uncharitable.—Buckminster.


OBITUARY.

	DIED, at Harrisville, August 20th, 1888, Nancy be-
loved wife of Daniel B. Rawson. Deceased was born in
Davenson Co, N. C., March 26 1829. Embraced the
Gospel 1858, and gathered with the Saints in Nauvoo 1842,
in connection with her mother, brothers and sisters; her
father being dead she was obliged to work and help her
mother, untli in 1849 they arrived in Salt Lake Valley; the
same Fall she was married to Brother Rawson, with
whom she had 10 children of whom 2 are dead. She was
a kind, honest and affectionate mother, always a friend of
the poor, and always on hand to assist the Relief Society
whatever help was needed.
	Sister Rawson labored many years as Coun. to the Re-
lief Society in the first organiazation in the Harrisville Ward,
 which office she filled with honor; she also obeyed the ce-
lestial law of marriage, and one can truly say her mission
on earth is filled. As our Heavenly Father has seen fit to
call from us our beloved sister and co-laborer, we, the
members of the Relief Society of West Harrisville Ward,
deeply mourn the loss of onr sister and heartily sympa-
thize with the husband and family in their great bereave-
ment, and ask God our Eternal Father to comfort and
strengthen them aud help us all to be faithful that we may
meet Sister Rawson when our work on earth is done.
		MARY HEGSTED, Prest.
		CHRISTENA HEGSTED, Sec.
	Harrisville, Sept. 27, 1888.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL. 17.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, OCTOBER 15, 1888.	NO. 10.


[Column 1]
			AN UNSEEN ANGEL.
		    NANCY PATTON M'LEAN.
Soft fell the tender shades of eve, the coming night fore-
	telling; 
Afar and near rose sweet and clear the organ's song up-
	welling;
To other ears a wordless hymn; to mine, attuned to
	sadness.
Each note was full and running o'er with loving praise and
	gladness.
With weary hearts and tired feet I crossed the sacred 
	portal. 
Weary of sin and tired of toil, the common lot of mortal.
Down throunb the aisles the music rolled from out the 
	organ ringing, 
The angel met me at the door, a joyous message bring-
	ing—
"Give thanks, the Lord our God is good, His mercy faileth
	never;
Be glad, O heaven, and sing, O earth, our God is God for-
	ever!"
"I am too tired to sing," I said; "I have no thanks to 
	render.
Life's path is cold and hard to tread, my feet are sore and 
	tender. 
Where friendship's torch should constant burn, it shows 
	but fitful flashes; 
The gold I grasp to rust will turn, the fruit is only ashes. 
They have the less of care to bear whose life is soonest 
ended." 
Sweet with the organ's solemn roll the angel's answer
	blended—
"Envy thou not the quiet dead; nor covet thou their
	sleeping.
Bear bravely thy appointed lot, thy faith and honor keep-
	ing;
And hold this blessed truth in mind, forget its beauty
	never,
Whether Life smiles or darkly frowns, our God is God for-
	ever!"
"I am so sick of sin," I said, "and sin is all surrounding. 
The good I would do, do I not, because of sin abounding. 
I reach toward the higher life, my soul for freedom long-
	ing, 
My feet are struggling in the mire where base desires are
	thronging.
What need is there of sin to be? Why spurn I not its
	proffers?
The very bitterness of death is in the cup it offers." 
Still underneath and far above the organ's solemn pealing 
The angel's tender melody into my heart came stealing- 
"Behold the cross! O tired soul; lay down thy heavy bur-
	den! 
Seek here the strength oft promised as true faith's unfailing
	guerdon."
"I have no faith," I said with tears. Why should I cease 
	from crying? 
Within the clasp of these sad years my darling dead are
	lying.
Why are they gone while I am here? My grief is just and
	holy."
The angel sang still sweet and clear, still lovingly and
	lowly—
"Thy dead are safe in God's own care; O, sad soul, cease 
	thy wailing. 
Earth hath no guardian anywhere so loving, so unfailing.
The Lord is very pitiful unto His children's sorrow; 
This grief is for thine earthly day— what joy the heavenly 
	morrow!
Oh, sing the Father's grace to thee, whose mercy failing
	never,
Gives unto thy beloved sleep- then sinless life forever."
I bowed my head in bitter pain that strove with deep con-
	trition. 
"Father," I cried, and with the word my heart broke in
	submission.
I saw the love, I saw the grace, I saw the tender beauty 
That shineth from the Father's face along the path of duty.
[Column 2]
The angel's voice was hushed awhile and all the people
	singing
Sent up to God a noble hymn with thankful praises ring-
	ing. 
And when the solemn words of prayer rose over heads all
	bended,
The doubts, the fears, the cares, the tears and weariness 
	were ended; 
And all the happy paths of peace within my heart rang 
	ever,
"Give thanks! Our Father is our God, whose mercy fail-
	eth never." 
							    -Hearth and Hall.


[Column 1]

SOME SERIOUS REFLECTIONS.

	I dreamed, a few nights ago, of being led by
circumstances into a room, or office, where I
found myself in the presence of a number of
our aged brethren, some Apostles, and one
brother, who occupied a chair near the centre
of the room and attracted my attention most,
was from the spirit world. Though I did not
know his name, I kept my eyes upon him,
while listening with deep interest to the con-
versation, which was upon common topics, or
 the scenes which most deeply concern the Lat-
ter-day Saints. After a little he spoke as fol-
lows, emphasizing every word so that it im-
pressed itself the stronger upon my memory:
"Why," said he, "we are around here all the
time, and know everything that is transpiring,"
meaning among our own people and the Gen-
tiles, and that there were many more beside
himself, who were similarly engaged.
	This I believe to be verily true. It brought
to my mind the testimony of many who were
present at the dedication of the Manti Temple,
whose eyes were opened, and they bore witness
of many glorious visions, of personages who
 appeared to them with the heavenly music and
singing, which was heard at different times, all
proving that we are not separated from those
who were co-workers with us here, and are still
engaged in the interest of those with whom
they labored for years in establishing the
principles of this Gospel, and planning for the
holy Temples, in which the work is continuing
on for the living and for the dead.
	I am often led to reflect upon these glorious
things, and compare them with the vanities of
the world, the mammon of unrighteousness,
which has such a hold upon the human mind,
that it can take those richer and everlasting
treasures in the hearts of men and women who
have once feasted upon the smne, but through
slothfuluess and a gradual neglect of their
duties, have come to love these pleasures more
than the things gf God. I can realize some-
thing of the solemnity that rested upon the
minds of some of our fathers who bore the
weight of these things for years, before they
were laid to rest under the silent sod of mother
earth. I have felt to bemoan the many sad
mistakes that have been made by brethren and
sisters once so valiant for the truth, which
they professed to have a testimony of, and
have wondered how they could have a face to
deny the "Mormonism, which they had once
known the truth of, and declared to be the
work of God, then deny it, and say that it is
false, and turn to persecuting those who remain
true to their convictions. Apostacy from the
Church of Christ is a thing more terrible and
more to be lamented than, all the calamities,
that have ever befallen the Latter-day Saints.
At different periods within my recollection

[Column 2]

these things have been witnessed, and the mid-
night darkness into which they sank, their
very countenances betraying it to their con-
demnation before their brethren and sisters.
They had lost the light of the true Gospel by
degrees, and were not able to discern between
men chosen by the Almighty, and those in-
spired by the wicked one. The trials of many
has made shipwreck of their faith, it having
been pinned to the actions of men. I cannot
see what the mistakes, or failures of men has
to do with the Gospel revealed from on high
for the eternal salvation of the human family,
nor how any principle, or doctrine connected
therewith can be effected. The Church has
continued to grow and increase in strength and
power, and will still roll farward, and no power
can hinder the accomplishment of the pur-
poses for which it was revealed and established
in this dispensation, no matter how many may
slip or weaken at the sight of danger and
yield to cowardice, or to some other weakness
of poor humanity—beset as we are by the
powers of evil, not being prepared or suffici-
ently fortified to contend against them. It
shows how necessary it is for every individual
to know for themselves whether or not this
work is of God or of man, and not depend up-
on the testimony of another. This has been
taught from the beginning, and the Prophet
Joseph frequently remarked, that his word
was no more than any other man's, except
when he said, "Thus saith the Lord."
	It was in this spirit that he taught the princi-
ple of celestial marriage, and some of his
wives have testified that he admonished them
to go to the Lord and inquire of Him concern-
ing this principle, with the promise that they
 should have his faith; and this was his counsel
to many others to whom he revealed the
principle, and none who obeyed his counsel
failed to receive the promised testimony, be-
sides receiving a great blessing. And it was
this-alone that enabled them to endure their
bitter trials and privations, as well as the scorn
and reproaches of many a one calling them-
selves Latter-day Saints. Had the Prophet
designed evil, or to lead captive the daughters
of Zion, he would hardly have given this kind
 of counsel. Here is some of his earliest teach-
ings from Lecture Sixth in Doctrine and
 Covenants, treating of the "knowledge which
persons must have, that the course of life
which they pursue is according to the will of
God, in order that they may be enabled to
exercise faith in Him unto life and salva-
tion."
	"An actual knowledge to every person, that
the course of life which he pursues is according
to the will of God, is essentially necessary to
enable him to have that confidence in God,
without which no person can obtain eternal
life. It was this that enabled the ancient
 Saints to endure all their afflictions and perse-
cutions, and take joyfully the spoiling of their
goods, knowing (not believing merely), that
they had a more enduring substance. Hebrews
x, 34.
	"A religion," he says, "that does not require
the sacrifice of all things never has power
sufficient to produce the faith necessary unto the
enjoyment of life and salvation, for from the
first existence of man, the faith necessary unto
the enjoyment of life and salvation, never
could be obtained without the sacrifice of all 
earthly things. It was through this sacrifice,
and this only, that God has ordained that men


should enjoy life, and it is through the medium
of sacrifice of all earthly things, that men do 
actually know that they are doing the things
that are well pleasing in the sight of God."
	This is plain and to the point. The sacrifices
which the Saints have made at various times, 
and the adversities which they have had to 
meet, seem so very similar to those endured by
the ancient saints, that this alone should be a 
testimony of this being the true Church. 
Those who have imagined that a smooth, un-
ruffled life is proof of their upright and Godly 
course; have not considered that of the Savior, 
nor of His disciples. But those who are estab-
lished in the faith can easily believe that op-
position, persecution, tribulation and sacrifice 
is the alloted portion of every faithful follower 
of Jesus Christ.
	The persecutions and unlawful proceedings
that are being carried on by men inspired by 
the evil one, we have been led to expect, and
also for a falling away, or apostacy from the 
Church of God. This was among the events 
that were to take place before "the redemption
of Zion," which "must needs come by power;
therefore, I will raise up unto my people a 
man who shall lead them like as Moses led the 
children Israel. 	*	*	And as your
fathers were led at the first, even so shall the 
redemption of Zion be."
	The true Latter-day Saint will be measur-
ably prepared for whatever may transpire.
The great and mighty revolutions, and the dis-
tress of the nations at the present time, are 
things that we have been looking for. 	We 
know that they will continue until the earth is 
cleansed of its wickedness; and it stands us in 
hand to set a double watch over ourselves, 
that we do not grieve and lose the Holy Spirit, 
and drift away into unbelief, but prepare our-
selves for what is laying before us, which are 
days of tribulation, and try to avoid that awful
whirlpool into which so many have been led, 
either by a spirit of doubt and unbelief, or 
because of transgression, and have sank to rise 
no more. Those who bear these things in
 mind, and are watching the signs of the times
will be able to discern between truth and error,
and accept their trials as needful lessons, that 
will add to their store of experience, which in 
a future day will be of the greatest worth.
Besides, it will test the faith of every soul, 
provingwho are the pure in heart, who can
 lay claim to the promise that they shall see 
God and walk in the light of His countenance. 
The promise that the old men should dream
dreams, and the young men should see visions
—meaning women as well—we claim has been 
realized by many, and to a great extent; and 
would that there were more who had on the 
armor of righteousness, and realized the honor
which the Lord has conferred upon them, and 
the conditions upon which they have received
the promise of an eternal exaltation in His 
kingdom, that whatever comes they would be 
found among that number, who will stand firm 
and immovable as the rock of ages to every
principle of the everlasting Gospel, trusting
not in flesh, but in the Almighty, who stands
at the helm, and will honor their integrity aud
bear them off victorious. 
			HELEN MAR WHITNEY.
	October 3, 1888.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

LINCOLN PARK.

	Lincoln Park, in the North-Eastern part
of Chicago, borders on the lake point, and is
very extensive and attractive, as was the man
from  whom it derived its name.
	As in other parks, there are rustic seats,
shaded by large trees, and the ground is every-
where carpeted with beautiful lawns and
flower beds, intermingled with artificial ponds
and channels, for the convenience of boaters.
	But its chief attractions are those of its hot

[Column 2]

﻿house and its extensive exhibition of wild
animals. In the hot house can be found in-
numerable tropical plants and flowers, so
arranged, according to class and form, as to
produce at the same time the most pleasing and
instructive appearance.
	One room of the house is dedicated to the
feathered songsters, of which there is a great
variety, but we will leave the birds and pass on
to the beasts.
	As you enter the Park from the street car, a
beautifully bridged channel is crossed, then im-
mediately to the right is a small pond, in the
 centre of which is a rock island, bearing an in-
scription something like this; "Any person
found throwing any thing into the water, or
at the sea lions, will be promptly arrested."
On the other side is the name of the donor,
and at the time of the printing there were two
sea lions, since when one has had the misfor-
tune to die, and if it is possible for the lower
animals to feel a loss of any kind, the remain-
ing seal surely mourns for its lost companion.
It now seldom creates any disturbance, while in
the past discord seemed to be its chief amuse-
ment.
	A little farther on to the left is a very large
wire cage, and as has already been surmised, is
occupied by birds. The owl sits upon a high
perch, just large enough for himself, with his
eyes wide open, and apparently watching the
 moving forms of human spectators, while the
eagles fly from perch to perch, and though un-
able to attract attention from the owl, their pre-
sence is at least perceptible to the slowly moving
alligators and mud-turtles, as they crawl in and
out of a sluggish little pond beneath. The rabbits
have a separate apartment in the same cage,
and enjoy themselves, as the more intellectual
rabbits should, i. e.: in games that are not dis-
tasteful to the refined rabbit. A little further
on is another large bird cage, containing a
great many varieties of birds and small
animals, such as the blue jay, quails, porcu-
pines, squirrels, etc. How quick the latter as-
cends the tree, but how much quicker they de-
scend if induced to jump for peanuts. Across
the walk from the bird cage is a small box
faced with very fine wire, entombing white, or
 albino rats. They make a very beautiful pic-
ture, with spotless coats and pink eyes. The
young are placed in a small wire nest, and they
lie so closely together, that they very much
resemble a little roll of pure white cotton.
	The ground to the right of the "rats" is the
home of the prairie dog; true to his habits of
uncivilized life, he gathers all the peanuts
offered into his under-ground retreat, and pro-
vides in every way possible for the long, cold
winter, which he seems to realize is inevitable.
Whether they understand the geological
changes of the seasons or not, could only be
learned by conversing with one of their profes
sors, or attending one of the "dog" schools.
	At any rate, they provide for the coming
season, and in all seem so fearless. It is a
characteristic of the prairie dog to be timid,
but here we see them coming to the edge of
 the wire railing, and standing on two legs, hold
out the other two as if professional beggars.
(For the latter there is no place like Chicago.)
 	The other animals will be described at a
later date.		X.


PRICE PRIMARY FAIR.
	
	On the 21st of September a Primary Fair
was held at Price, Emery Co. Doors were 
opened at 10 o'clock. There was an exhibi-
tion, of needle and crochet work, home-made
woolen yarn, carpenter work, marbles, vege-
tables and corn, by children from four to six
years old.
	Meeting was called to order by the Prest.,
Jane Eldredge. Singing. Prayer was offered
by Bro. E. W. McIntire. Singing, after which


there were songs, recitations and dialogues, by
the Price and Spring Glenn Primaries.
	Bros. Hardman and McIntire spoke a few
encouraging words to the children.
	Singing. Benediction by Bro. F. M. Ewell.
						E. E. BRANCH.


MISCELLANEOUS.
	The brave man is an inspiration to the weak,
and compels them to follow him.

	As it is the characteristic of great wits to
say much in few words, so it is of small wits
to talk much and say nothing.—Rochefoucault.

	Obscurity in writing is commonly an argu-
ment of darkness in the mind. The greatest
learning is to be seen in the greatest plainness.
—Bishop Wilkins.

	Although men are accused of not knowing
their own weakness, yet perhaps as few know
their own strength. It is in men as in soils,
where sometimes there is a vein of gold, which
the owner knows not of.—Swift.

	There is no knowledge for which so great a
price is paid as the knowledge of the world;
and no one ever became an adept in it except
at the expense of a hardened and wounded
heart.—Countess of Blessington.


Miss Mary A. Green is the second Massa-
chusetts woman lawyer. She will be a mem-
ber of Suffolk County Bar.


OBITUARIES.
	Mary Ann, the beloved wife of Joshua Jarvis, departed
this life at Bloomington, Sept. 24th, 1888.  She was born 
in Bethlingreen, London, March 10th, 1840; emigrated to
Salt Lake City in 1861; moved to Cottonwood, where she 
resided a short time; from there went to Logan, and in
June, 1865, came to Bear Lake Valley, where she resided 
until her death, proving herself a devoted wife, loving
mother and constant friend.  She was faithful to her 
religion, and filled the offices of Secretary and Second
Counselor in the Relief Society; the latter office she held
at the time of her death. There is not one but will miss
her cheering smile and loving counsel.  Among singers she
was the sweetest, but her melodious voice we shall hear no 
more, until we hear it in the choirs of heaven. 
		Sister, rest your weary body,
			Now  your work on earth is done,
		But your memory will be sacred
			In the heart of every one
		Who has joined you in the duties
			Resting on our little band;
		May we still enjoy your presence
			In the blest and holy land.
									E. C. P.
	It is with feelings of sorrow we record the death of our 
beloved sister, Elsie Lant, who died Aug. 6th, 1888, aged
thirty-eight years, eleven months and fourteen days. 
			RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT
	Whereas, our Heavenly Father has seen fit to take from
our midst our beloved sister,
	Resolved, That we, her associates and co-workers in the
Third District of the Payson Relief Society, of which she 
was presiding teacher, do mourn her loss, that we cherish
her memory, for we can truly say she manifested that true 
charity which flows from a sense of duty and hope in God. 
	Resolved, That we sympathize with the bereaved family
and friends of our departed sister, praying that the comfort-
ing influence of our Heavenly Father may rest upon them
and that her children may be inspired to walk in her foot
steps.
	Resolved, That we present a copy of these Resolutions to 
the bereaved family; also that a copy be sent to the EX-
PONENT for publication, and that they be placed upon the 
district record. 
								MARTHA SIMONS,
								MARY A. NEBEKER,
								PHEBE HANCOCK.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THOUGHTS OF LIFE AND NATURE. 

	The poet's mind has wandered 
		O'er the path of Mortal life,
	One side seems strewed with roses, 
		The other, thorns and strife.		
	God sent those blooming flowers
		To cheer the weary day,
	But the thorns will often prick us
		As we saunter by the way.

	Life's composed of clouds and sunshine,
		Of the evil ways and good,
	But the story of the future
		Is not easy understood;
	The calm, bright days of summer	
		Pass by us like a dream, 	
	And the icy path of winter
		Soon melts into a stream.

	The flowers by the roadside
		Bloom but once, and die;
	Their green leaves fall and wither—
		Oh, some one tell us why?
	Why does the brightest sunshine
		Become darkened by the night?
	Because we are only mortals,
		And it can't be always bright.

	Why is the smile of mother,
		Often broken with a sigh?
	And in her gloomy chamber 
		Do we often hear her cry?
	Why do her wavy ringlets
		Become so thin and white?
	And the eyes that shone like morning
		Lose their lustre and their light?

	Because it's human nature,
		And the gayest things have changed;
	God's hands have painted nature, 
		And His colors He's arranged,
	Life's path is always threatened
		By some approaching storm,
	And the peal of distant thunder
		Comes to warn us of the harm.

	Why do the apple-blossoms,
		On the branches white and tall,
	Only come to show their beauty,
		Then with the breezes fall? 
	Because Dame Nature calls them
		To the earth's [-]ntidy floor,
	For they've lived their time appointed,
		And their blooming time is o'er.

	Oh! why are we but mortals,
		Just sent to stay awhile,
	Un[-]il Thy heavenly angels
		O'er our whitened brows will smile.
	The gentle summar breezes
		All whisper in a breath,
	That life is always subject
		To weakness and to death.

			From M. A. F.


TO AUNT POLLY.

Dear Aunt Polly:
	Your pleasantly worded letter has roused
me from the partial apathy which has fallen
over "things mental" of late, and I am going
to steal these few minutes, while my dinner 
is boiling to try, at least, to answer you.
	You have alluded in so truthful and vivid
a way to my uneventful life at Laie, whose
chief variation consists in wash-day, and whose
only excitement is mail-day, that I shall simply
say, "It is even so!"
	I sometimes wish that speaking was done in 
something the same way as writing; for when
one is angry, one seldom takes time to write
down the angry thoughts, nor do we often
write out our troubles, or gossip to our friends.
But our tongues go away at a tangent, and 
things are often said for which we feel sorrow:
	Since I have been here, I have but once
or twice broken through a rule I made when
I came, and that was, no matter what occurred
[Column 2]
here of a disagreeable nature, not to put it on
paper for others to read. But I have, indeed,
as you so thoughtfully suggest, been shadowed
by heavy clouds of sorrow, and have pricked 
my feet over many thorny obstacles in my 
path. Well, so we do everywhere; therefore, 
let me be grateful for all I have enjoyed and 
endured. The one is a pleasant remembrance,
the other needed lessons in life.
	At present we are very happily situated.
President King's wife and Sister Nellie Beesley
live over at the Mission House, and take turns
at the kitchen work; our family being the only
other at present in Laui Huli. We three sis-
ters have many cosy times together, and in no
place have I seen a better, more united spirit
than exists here. But for many months we
have had no evening services on Sunday, be-
cause there were so few of us here. I have 
missed them.
	Your kind and sympathetic words have
touched me strangely; perhaps because I have 
grown very tender-hearted here so far away
from home; perhaps because it is so rarely we 
are made conscious that our humble efforts are
realized or noticed by any one. I earnestly
echo your wish for that nearer acquaintance-
ship.
	Now let me give you a little Laie news. You 
will remember the "big room," or general sit-
ting-room at the Mission House? Then, if you 
do, you will at the same time recall its generally
comfortless aspect. No one, I am assured; has 
been to blame for this, as eating in it three
times a day, and the almost constant presence
of the natives, have kept it in a comparatively
untidy condition.
	Of late, I am pleased to report, Sisters King
and Nellie Beesley have taken the matter into 
their own hands, and the result is really a 
genuine surprise, as well as an innovation.
Lace curtains, with heavily fringed velvet lam-
berquins drape the windows, hanging from rich
looking cornices.  A very good striped mat-
ting covers the floor.  The dining-table has
been banished, and in its stead a large center
table, covered with a bright cloth, and strewn
with books, occupies the center of the floor.
The asthmatic organ, the ancient sideboard, and,
indeed, every piece of furniture in the room
has received a generous coat of varnish.  New
pictures, donated in some instances by the
Elders, aid in the wall adornment.  The old
sofa has been stuffed till it looks almost 
apoplectic, then covered with dark, handsome
creton, and cushions added to it; the big chair
has been treated in the same manner, while the
cane rocking chair is gorgeous in two fine
crazy cushions, all of silk and velvet pieces.
The rough deal shelves, which assisted the few
walnut ones in forming our modest library,
have been brightened by embroidered velvet
valances or curtains, and a large, fine office
clock ticks on the wall. The pictures of our
beloved leaders hang in the honored places, 
while Daniel Webster frowns down upon us
from a corner; Anderson's oil painting hangs
above the door, and the pictures of our ex-
presidents, Pack, Cluff and Partridge, and Sis-
ter Partridge, all occupy prominent positions.
Dear "Maggie" Cluff, as the natives love to
call her, still smiles upon us near her husband,
the only thing we have left of her sweet 
womanliness. We have no picture of Sister
Speight, for which we often express our regrets,
as we have a magnificent one of her companion.
Bright colored mats lie under vases and lamps,
pretty tidies add the finishing touches to lounge,
chairs and tables. The whole room has an air
of comfort and elegance, and inspires every one
with a desire to behave one's self at one's very
best.
	All this sounds extravagant, but outside of
the white-washing and painting (new coats of 
which are being added inside and out over the
whole house), the deft and tasty fingers of the
[Column 3]
sisters have done every bit of it all. In point
of dollars, the cost has been small, but as a 
labor of love and good-will the account runs
up large.
	I must add that the dining-table is now set
in the middle room, fitted up for the purpose,
between the dining-room and kitchen.
	Perhaps this letter is long enough to satisfy
you and bother "Aunt Em," so I will say no 
more just now, only that I sincerely send my
"aloha mui" to the Editor and readers of our 
EXPONENT, and beg you, Aunt Polly, to keep
a very generous share for yourself.
						HOMESPUN.	
	N. B. I suspect who you are, notwithstand-
ing your reserve.					H. 
	Laie, Sept. 23, 1888.


NOTES AND NEWS. 

	Elizabeth Cady Stanton has come out for the
Prohibition Party.
	The gamblers and criminal classes of Wash-
ington Territory lit bonfires and rang bells and
hurrahed in the streets when the Federal
Judges violated their oaths and refused to
enforce the woman's suffrage law. According
to these Judges one blackleg ruffian has more
power at the polls than all the wives and
mothers.—Ex.
	The Baroness Gripenberg of Finland had a
reception given her, week before last, in Sara-
toga, at tbe house of Mrs. Harriet Whipple
Wallace. Among those present were Mrs.
Lucy Stone of the Boston Woman's Journal,
Miss Anthony, from Rochester; Miss Caroline
A. Huling, editor of Chicago Justitia; Mr.
Albert O. Wilcox, from New York, and many
residents of Saratoga. Several addresses were
made, and the Baroness's account of women's
affairs in Finland was heard with much inter-
est. She is a member of the Board of Educa-
tion of Helsingfors, and was a delegate to the
International Council of women.


"Denis Kearney was "finished" by Sara An-
drews Spencer. At the Chicago Labor Con-
vention of 1880 he interrupted her, roaring:
"My wife told me if I voted for the women she
would greet me with a flatiron! Women don't
want to vote!" Mrs. Spencer answered,
"Thank you much for telling me which is the
head of the Kearney family." That was the
end of Denis. "The daughter of Zion had
laughed him to scorn." He could not get the
convention to hear him, and has not been heard
from since."

	"Your name ain't Tree, is it?" cried Ange-
line's father down the stairs to Angeline's
young man as the clock struck 12.
	"N n-no sir; m-m-my name's R-r-r-r-obert
W-w-wilkins, sir, stammered the young man.
	"Oh, excuse me, Mr. R-r-r-r-obert W-w-wil-
kins sir," retorted the old man courteously.
"I might a known your name wasn't Tree, be-
cause you never seem to leave."
	And then Mr. Robert Wilkin's limbs
sprouted, and the pitter-patter of his feet
down the broad walk told that he was leaving
as fast as he knew how.

	They were discussing the magazines. "That
was a fine portrait of Lester Wallack in Scrib-
ner's, Miss Brown," said he.
	"Indeed, yes. And how interesting the
Century 'Bric-a brac' was last month," said
she.
	"Wasn't it. And how good the last chap-
ters of 'Annie Kilburn' are?" said he.
	"I haven't read them," said she.
	"Aren't you fond of serials?" said he.
	"Not very; I don't mind rice, but I despise
oatmeal. Why do you ask?" said she.
	And he said, "Good evening Miss Brown."
