SALT LAKE CITY,	-	-	JANUARY 1, 1889.


THE NEW YEAR 1889.

	The EXPONENT wishes its readers A Happy
New Year, in every land and clime, wherever
they may be, from the Rocky Mountain region,
to the borders of the Mediterranean, and thence
to the shores of Hindostan, or upon the islands
of the Pacific sea. To one and all it extends
sincere and cordial congratulations, upon the ad-
vent of the New Year; hoping it may prove a year
of prosperity, in the acquisition of knowledge, in
the growth of intelligence, in the success of
science, and in the extension of "life, liberty and
the pursuit of happiness" to all mankind, irre-
spective of creed or party; and although it was
seemingly unpropitiously ushered in with an
eclipse of the sun, who knows but it may be an
augury for good, and the sun shine out far more
brilliantly after being darkened by the lesser light
of the moon, and the new year mark an era in
the history of the world, that the people will be
proud to look back upon as the turning point in
the road toward a higher civilization, that shall
envelop humanity with its curtains of strength
and love, and raise the banner of freedom aloft
for all to gaze upon wonderingly.
	That a glorious time is coming has long been
predicted, and why not now? The present is auspi-
cious with pale stars of hope, that grow brighter
and brighter—the midnight hour of ignorance
is, doubtless, past, for light has come into the
world, and truth has burst forth in rich effulgence
and ere long it shall assuredly triumph over error
and sin, and the reign of righteousness begin.
Speed the time when strife and enmity shall cease
and the world rejoice in a reign of peace.


[Column 1]

EDITORIAL NOTES.

	"SALT LAKE CITY ILLUSTRATED" is a hand-
some work by D. C. Dunbar & Co., issued recent-
ly in Omaha. It contains, over fifty views of the
city from  various points, very fine cuts of business
and other public buildings and large and commo-
dious residences, churches, etc., taken correctly
from photographs. In addition to this there is an
accurate description of the city, its rise and pro-
gress, by R. W. Sloan. The book contains forty
pages, is of heavy paper, and the type and me-
chanical work in the very best style. It gives
the stranger a very good idea of Salt Lake City
and its environs, and will be a good thing to carry
a general impression of the beauty and outside
appearance of the town. The work shows com-
mendable enterprise, as well as possessing con-
siderable merit on its own account. For sale at
Dwyer's Book Store, Main St, 40 cts per copy.
	The Deseret News Company has just issued a 
paper entitled, "The Deseret Weekly."  It is in

[Column 2]

pamphlet form, a very suitable size for handling
or for binding; larger outside than Harper,s
Monthly, with three column pages, but only con-
taining thirty-two pages of reading matter. The
paper is intended to combine the current news as
a news paper and magazine, and may be properly
styled a journal. This first number is filled with
original articles from writers well known to the
community, and upon themes of interest to the
people here, and well worth preserving to be read
in the future as well as to send abroad to the
world. The opening of the volume is a poem
"Thought's Martyrdom" by the gifted poet of
Zion, O. F. Whitney, followed by "Ideas of
Government," by C. W. P., a subject upon
which much may be said educational to the peo-
ple. A story follows, opening with the saying
"Truth is stranger than fiction," signifying of
course that it is a true story and will therefore be
of greater interest to those who read it. "The
Relations of Life," by S. W. R., "Among the
Mexicans," by J. E. Talmage, "Curing a Cold,"
C. W. Stayner. "Spectacles a Necessity," by
Romania B. Pratt. M. D., "Holiday Changes," a
poem by Lula, characteristic of the times; another
by Mrs. Woodmansee, "Stand Your Ground,"
possessing the genuine ring of Mormonism. "A
Happy New Year," by Godfrey Williston Chris-
tine, contains much information on the subject of
New Year's in other ages and centuries etc.
"Martyrs to the Truth," Josiah Burrows. "A
Peculiar Race," B. F. C. Jr., "The Late Fair,"
Essay Caigh. "The Resurrection," a lecture by
J. E. Taylor, and other articles and miscellaneous
matter make up a very interesting number. The 
price per year, payable in advance, is $2.50, or
half year $1.50. Twenty six numbers will com-
plete a volume, as the fifty-two numbers would
not be a suitable size for binding. Success to
the new enterprise.

     "THE STORY OF THE BOOK OF MORMON," just
issued by Elder Geo. Reynolds, is unique in its
originality, and will certainly enhance the value
of that wonderful book, which forms the founda-
tion of the work; a book that seems to have
troubled the enlightened world so much, because
of the manner of its coming forth, as also its pe-
culiar style, and the general character of the
work itself, and its striking similarity to the
Hebrew Bible. The "Book of Mormon" has
been a source of discomfort and of anxiety, so to
speak, from the time when it was first announced
that Joseph Smith had discovered some gold
plates in a hill near Palmyra, until the present
day.  It has been one of the most important fac-
tors in the history of this Church, and wherever
the name "Mormon" is heard the story of that
wonderful book is told in one form or another;
generally so sensational and highly-colored and
so far wide of the truth that it seems strange
that people do not know that the tale is false.
However, at last, after sixty years, a story is
actually told on the subject that can be relied
upon. The familiarity of the writer with his
theme is well known to the people here, and he is
certainly able to present the subjects he describes
in a manner that will be attractive and entertaining
to all who read for the sake of obtaining true
knowledge. The book is handsomely illustrated.
The first picture is a copy of the painting, "Jesus
Appearing to the Nephites," by Wm. T. Armi-
tage, in the Logan Temple; "The Vision of
Nephi," "The First Sacrifice On The Promised
Land," "Babylon," "Nazareth," "Discovery of the
Records of the Jaredites," "The Vision of
Limhi," and other illustrations descriptive of the
book, forty-two in all, must intensify the interest
of the literary work. We have not yet had time
to read the book sufficiently careful to be able to
review it properly, but feel much interested in the
subject matter of the work. It is handsomely
bound and certainly presents an attractive exterior.
The book contains nearly 500 pages, and the

[Column 3]

typographical part of the work is excellent. After
having perused the book we shall give a more ex-
tended notice, and in the meantime we trust it
will have, as it certainly ought, a wide circula-
tion. Price, cloth binding, $3.00, leather, $4.50.


NEW YEAR'S GREETIEG.

TO MY SISTERS:
	I wish you A Merry Christmas and A Happy
New Year!  In all our varied circumstances as
Saints we have a right to acknowledge the hand
of God in all things.  This encourages and
strengthens us in our labors in the Relief Society
at this time; it makes the hearts of the poor re-
joice through the diligence and thoughtfulness of
those who minister to them in doing good, being
in harmony with our worthy bishops; the teachers,
both male and female, find the needy and see that
none lack for the comforts of life, and thanksgiv-
ing ascends to the Father, instead of the cries of
suffering.  We have had no paupers formerly, and
the absence of saloons and places of refuge that
follow intemperance, which belong not to Latter-
day Saints.  We are compelled by law now to
submit to some of these.  The United States
think the precepts of the Bible too good for Mor-
mons to practice, they must only believe; these
things make us sorry; although over eight hun-
dred true wives married for time and all eternity
(for death ends all matrimonial covenants in this
world at present), we will wait and call upon our
Heavenly Father for aid, for strength (for the
means accumulated for the support of the family
must go for fines), to rear the children in the path
of honesty and uprightness, and to teach them to
honor our God-given constitution.
	Knowing it is truly a religious conviction that
prompted the acts of fathers and mothers to take
upon themselves the heavy responsibilities of
large families, and the knowledge that we have
received the Holy Ghost through obedience, as
the Bible directs, let us not grow weary, but be
faithful unto the end, in doing good and loving
our sisters "as ourselves."  Mothers, our beauti-
ful, healthy, loving children, cherish them, for
they are gifts from God.  Under these peculiar
and trying conditions, fonder are the affections for
the darlings, and as the family circle is the center
of bliss and sunshine in time and eternity, let us
enjoy the home.
	As all, even those who persecute us, are our
Father's children, we can pity all mistakes; those
that come through misrepresentation, and all, even
 our worst enemies, we pray for, and while the
merry Christmas rung through all Christendom, 
how little did the world think of the loving hearts
that are wrung in prison, innocent of wrong doing,
and of the desolate hearts and hearthstones in
Utah.
	Here women are organized and have power to
do much good; it will show some joy and honor
too.  May we ever be true to the trust and confi-
dence bestowed upon us; bridle the tongue; by
ceasing to speak detrimentally of any, for all
have a portion of infirmities, and there is ever
room for improvement, and by extending mercy,
it is promised to us.  Read a little each day, my
dear sisters, if but ten minutes, and we will lay up
treasures of knowledge, besides mental and
spiritual strength for performing our daily duties,
and these are the first consideration.  The monthly
visits of our ever faithful teachers are a great bless-
ing to the Saints; these are our special representa-
tivesin the kingdom of God, with the sister teachers.
May they ever be endowed wtth wisdom, discern-
ment, and a goodly portion of the Holy Spirit,
that none may suffer, and may they ever have
wise counsel to comfort the afflicted.
	The counsel given us from the brethren of the
Apostles a few days since was for the sisters to
continue their good works, as Sister Eliza and her
Counselors, with others, had done and had taught


in the past; we ever hope to improve, until we are
perfect in every good word and work in the sphere
we are called to act in. Sisters, continue in sav-
ing wheat; if it needs renewing it can be loaned
to the presiding Bishop, or by the Bishop of the 
ward being responsible in writing.
	Wherever the brethren can take time to visit
and encourage the sisters there is a great differ-
ence in their prosperity and growth.  We did not
call ourselves to these labors of love, but we are
willing and desirous to do what is required; we
wish to hold our meetings and conferences regu-
larly as heretofore, that we may improve.  Can
our time and means be more profitably spent than
in becoming better acquainted with each other, 
by strengthening confidence, encouraging faith,
and purifying our thoughts?  And may our joys
increase as each year unfolds its pages to write
upon; may they never be blotted with unkindness
or regrets.
	That the bright sunshine of peace may ever be
and abide with us, is the sincere prayer of,
		Your sister and co-laborer,
						Z. D. H. YOUNG.
	P. S. Again to my dear sisters, A Merry Christ-
mas and A Happy New Year.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


A ﻿TRAINING SCHOOL FOR NURSES.

	The need of competent and well-trained nurses
has long been felt in this community; and it is in
itself a department of labor that requires prepara-
tion. Training schools for nurses have been
established in all the large cities of the east, and
the practical knowledge on this subject thus ob-
tained has been fraught with the best results pos-
sible in this line of work.
	It is therefore with great satisfaction that we
announce the opening of such a school in Salt
Lake City, believing it will be beneficial in its re-
sults here as elsewhere.
	Dr. Mattie P. Hughes, whose name is well-
known in the community as a successful medical
practitioner, has decided to open a Training
School for Nurses, on the 20th of the present
month, January, 1889. The Dr. has, while travel-
ing, in the United States and also abroad in
Great Britain and on the continent, visited the
principal training schools in large cities, and has
taken great pains to look carefully into the plans
and methods upon which such institutions are
conducted. She proposes to give lectures similar
to those of the Boston Training School for nurses,
and is thoroughly qualified for the teaching, and
has provided herself with the needful apparatus,
etc, the best charts, books and skeleton. All
who are acquainted with Dr. Hughes will feel as
we do, that she is competent by education and
training to give the necessary instruction, and has
the high moral courage and independence neces-
sary for the work she is about to undertake, which
will, if it succeeds, prove a blessing to the sick,
and perhaps save not only much anxiety, but
many lives. Doctors depend greatly upon their
nurses, and we all know what a comfort it is to be
carefully and skillfully tended, when there is sick-
ness, and the nursing is very frequently all that is
necessary, without medicine or the attendance of
a physician. Communications in regard to terms
and other matters should be addressed to Dr.
Mattie P. Hughes, corner First East and South
Temple St. or, P. O. Box 575, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


[Column 1]

SEVENTIETH ANNIVERSARY.

	On the 29th of December last, 1888, Sister
Sarah M. Kimball reached the seventieth anniver-
sary of her birth. Sister Kimball has in many
respects been a remarkable woman, and at the
present writing is as brilliant in mind, and as
capable of mental activity as she was at forty.
She has had some experiences calculated to de-

[Column 2]

velop her best faculties for thinking, planning,
and suggesting; but aside from this development
she has much originality which has been a marked
characteristic of her career in life.
	It was at Sister Kimball's home in Nauvoo that
a few sisters belonging to her own neighborhood
met together at her suggestion, to consult as to a
plan for combining their means and efforts to
form a Ladies' Society, with a view to assist in
the building of the Nauvoo Temple. Out of this
little nucleus thus called together in Sister Kim-
ball's parlor has grown the organizations of
women that exist in the Church to-day.  It was in
this wise. The ladies invited, met and decided
to organize; Sister Kimball was delegated to call
on Sister Eliza R. Snow, who was then engaged
in teaching in the family of the Prophet, and ask
her to write a constitution and by-laws, and
submit them to President Joseph Smith prior to
the next meeting appointed for the following
Thursday. Sister Eliza cheerfully responded, and
when she read them to him he replied that the
constitution and by-laws were the best he had
ever seen. "But," he said, "this is not what you
want. Tell the sisters their offering is accepted of
the Lord, and He has something better for them
than a written constitution. Invite them all to
meet me and a few of the brethren in the Masonic
Hall over my store, next Thursday afternoon and
I will organize the sisters under the priesthood
after the pattern of the priesthood." He further
said, "The Church was never perfectly organized
until the women were thus organized."
	Most of our sisters already know the result of
this organization, and are familliar, by reading,
with the teachings of Joseph Smith to the Relief
Society in those early days.
	When the Relief Society was organized in the
Fifteenth Ward in Salt Lake City, Feb. 7th 1857,
Sister Kimball was elected President and has re-
tained the position ever since. Under her wise
and judicious management, the Society in that
Ward has flourished temporally, and much posi-
tive good has been accomplished by it in many
directions. The record of the work upon the
books, and the written reports from time to time,
tell this story, but they do not tell all. The com-
forting of the sorrowful and afflicted, the minister-
ations to the sick and dying, and the many deeds
of loving tenderness wrought by the sisters in
this and other organizations are not to be seen of
others, but they are doubtless recorded on high.
	Sister Kimball has been a widow for over
twenty-five years. Her husband was drowned in
the Pacific Ocean by the wreck of the steamer
Ida Hancock, off the coast of the San Pedro on
his way to the Sandwich Islands.
	Sister Kimball has the honor of being the first
woman to lay the corner-stone of the first Relief
Society building erected in this dispensation.
She thus describes it:—
	November 13, 1868 a silver trowel and mallet, were
furnished me, and, assisted by a Master Mason,
and surrounded by an assemblage of people, I
had the honor of laying the corner-stone of the
first Relief Society building erected in this dis-
pensation.
	At the time of the organization of the Central
Board of the Relief Society, Sister Eliza R. 
Snow the President-elect chose Sister Sarah M.
Kimball as the Secretary of that Board, and she
was set apart to act in that capacity by President
John Taylor, July, 17th, 1880, at the same time
that Sister Eliza R. Snow Smith was set apart to
preside over all the Relief Society in the Church
of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. The two
Counselors to the President were also set apart
at the same time, Sister Zina D. H. Young and
Sister Elizabeth Ann Whitney. After this was
done Sister Kimball was set apart as secretary
and received the following blessing from Presi-
dent John Taylor.
	"Sister Sarah Melissa Granger Kimball, in the

[Column 3]

name of the Lord Jesus Christ, and by virtue of
the Holy Priesthood I set thee apart to the office
of Secretary of the Relief Societies in Zion; to
record important events and keep a faithful re-
cord. Thou shalt be quick to discern and a ready
writer. Seek more earnestly for the Spirit of the
Lord, and thou shalt be blest more than thou
hast ever had power to conceive of. As thou
hast been preserved in life, thou shalt continue to
be preserved. I seal upon thee thy former bless-
ings, and thou shalt be honored in time and in
eternity, and have a seat in the celestial kingdom
of God, in the name of Jesus, Amen."
	Sister Kimball has faithfully fulfilled the obliga-
tions thus placed upon her in keeping the records
of all important matters and events pertaining to
the organization, and still continues to labor in
the performance of the duties of the calling to
which she was set apart; and though delicate in
her physical organization, she seems likely to re-
tain her powers of activity, and to be able to fill
the responsible positions with which she has been
honored many years to come. That she may
be preserved in the full possession and enjoy-
ment of health and powers of endurance in body
and mind, to advise, assist and instruct, those who
have had less experience in the sphere in which
women are now called to act, is sincerely to be
hoped for by all who favor Zion. The writer ex-
tends her most hearty congratulations to Sister
Kimball, wishing her length of days, peace and
prosperity.
		E. B. W.


TO MRS. Z. D. H. YOUNG.

			SANDWICH ISLANDS.

TO THE PRESIDENT OF THE RELIEF SOCIETY
	OF THE CHURCH OF JESUS CHRIST OF
	LATTER-DAY SAINTS:
	Dear Sister:—I have just finished the report
of the Relief Societies of these islands to for-
ward to you, but, having had no previous cor-
respondence, I do not know where to address.
I have not received the EXPONENT for some
time, and, as I think that, perhaps you have
received a letter for the EXPONENT from the
islands ere this, concerning the work of the
Relief Society I will not write to it at present.
But I will say, we held our Fair at the time of
our conference at Laie, Oct. 6th, which was
attended with marked success in the ability of
our native sisters.  Seven handsome quilts
were on exhibition, a large variety of babies
hoods, little shoes and stockings, lace tidies,
underwear, dresses, aprons, etc. etc., All
kinds of useful and ornamental work; over
two hundred articles in number, and reflecting
much credit on the ability of our dusky sisters.
This work was done by the four Societies of
this island only.
	They take great interest in this work, and
it is an industry that should be continued
among them, as it will tend to help them to
renounce their slothful and indolent habits,
and make them more fit for the association of
white people.
	Our Hawaiian sisters wish to send their
alolia nui to yourself and the societies in
Zion.  Ever praying for the advancement of
the work of God, I have the honor to be,
	Your Sister in the Gospel.
						LIBBIB NOALL.


R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS.

			WEBER	STAKE.
	Synopsis of the minutes of the quarterly
conference of the Relief Society of Weber
Stake, held in the tabernacle in Ogden City,
Dec. 13th, 1888, Mrs. Jane S. Richards pre-
siding.  Meeting opened by singing, "Come let


[Column 1]

us anew." Prayer by Bro. Gale. Singing,
"May we who know the joyful sound."
Minutes of the preceding conference, were
read by the secretary and unanimously adopted.
	Pres. J. S. Richards was thankful for the
pleasure of meeting again in conference.
Was glad to see so many present; had visited
quite a number of the branches of the Relief
Society throughout the county, and had en-
joyed a goodly portion of the spirit of God,
and listened to many faithful testimonies from
the sisters. Prayed for the Spirit of the Lord
to be with us to-day, to build up and
strengthen us in our holy faith.
	Pres. Zina D. Young felt we had met for a
renewal of the Spirit of God and prayed we
might receive it. Had noticed the same hum-
ble, kindly, Godly spirit filled the brethren in
prison, that did those who were laboring in the
Temples, and it was a proof that God was
well pleased with them and comforted them
abundantly with his Holy Spirit. Warned
the sisters against harboring the spirit of fault-
finding. Compared our trials to traversing a
canon which seemed that therewas no way out,
by, by faith and perseverence came through all
right; she had never lost faith in the silk business
in Utah; hoped to see it revived and prosper
under the management of the Saints. She
knew that faith in God and this Gospel was
daily increasing in the hearts of the Saints;
prayed it would continue.
	Sister Martha Bingham had been absent for
six months, but thanked God for the blessing
of returning. Had felt it quite a trial to have
our husbands imprisoned, or exiled, but knew
if we lived right, God would be with them and
with their families, and bless them abundantly
with His Spirit.
	Bishop R. McQuarrie was anxious for all
who came to meeting to hear the voices of the
speakers distinctly, that their hearts might be
filled and warmed and renewed with the Spirit
of God. We should seek to cultivate the
same spirit that inspired and animated our
brother Jesus Christ. Exhorted all to attend
to their prayers continually. Spoke of the
great good the Relief Society was doing; ad-
vised the sisters to work more earnestly in
saving wheat, and obeying that counsel given
to them on this subject by Pres. Young and
others.
	Sister Sarah Richards had a strong testi-
mony of the truth of this Gospel, and had
enjoyed it for many years. Was glad to see
Sister Young and others of our sisters, here to
advise and talk with us, and hoped each one
would go home filled according to their hearts
desire. Had never doubted that Pres. Brigham
Young was the inspired successor to Joseph the
Prophet, for she heard his voice and saw his
countenance reflected in him.
	Sister E. B. Wells felt that this was the
best day we had ever seen, notwithstanding the
clouds which often hover over us. She was
pleased to hear the brethren and sisters encour
age the saving of wheat, for there was no
danger of our storing away to much, for we
are told to seek out all the poor and needy no
matter what creed or color. Said we should
cultivate a spirit of charity and faith, especially
in our children; and made other remarks calcu-
lated to encourage the sisters in their labors of
love.
	Sister Emily Shurtliff felt it was necessary
for us to arouse ourselves from our seeming
neglect in regard to storing grain; was glad to
hear the subject revived and dwelt upon by
the brethren and sisters.
	Singing, "Come all ye sons of God." Prayer
by Bro. Dye.
	2 p.m.  Meetnig commenced by singing,
"Hail to the brightness." Prayer by Bishop
McKye. Singing, "Praise to the man who
communed with Jehovah." Opening remarks

[Column 2]

by the President, Mrs. Richards. Reports
made.
	Sister Eliza A. Tracy reported the Society
in Huntsville in a prosperous condition.
	Sister Adeline Belnap gave a favorable re-
port of the Hooper Society, said they had been
doing all in their power to assist in erecting a
meeting house in that place; had also visited
many sick, etc.
	Sister Jeannette Bingham of Wilson Ward,
bore a faithful testimony, and gave an encour-
aging report of their Society.
	Sister E. B. Wells, said it seemed to fall to
her lot to speak upon more practical matters, though
she desired more to be spiritually minded
thought it our duty to do all in our power to
allay the prejudice against us in the world;
explained a blank which had been received
from the Woman's National Association for the
signature of all those who believe that women
should vote on equal terms with men in all
political affairs. Gave some very good instruc-
tions on various subjects of importance to this
people. Spoke of the many benefits to the
suffering, obtained by upholding and sustaining
the Deseret Hospital.
	Sister Herrick spoke a short time expressing
her pleasure at meeting in the conference, and
bore a good testimony.
	Pres. Zina D. H. Young said she hoped the
sisters would work in unison with our brethren
in the priesthood. Advised mothers to observe
the Sabbath and teach their children to do the
same; "take special care in teaching the youth
good manners, self sacrifice and humility.
Let us show the world we are noble, true
women in every respect. When we become
pure and good enough God will open th way,
and the world will know that they have not
judged us any more justly than the Jews did
the Savior."
	Coun. C. F. Middleton, said he loved the
Latter-day Saints throughout the earth, and
desired to see them grow in purity and grace
before God.
	Pres. Jane S. Richards loved to listen to the
brethren and sisters on all occasions. "The
days of our conference are not long enough,
for we would like to hear from all if there
was but time." Urged upon the sisters the
necessity of laying up wheat, and of providing
means for the Hospital, also subscribing for
our own paper, the EXPONENT.
	After singing, "The Spirit of God like a fire
is burning," benediction was pronounced by,
Bishop Allred.
	MATTIE B. CANNON Stake Sec. R. S.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


NOTES OF TRAVEL.

EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	DEAR SISTER:—After a long silence I thought
a few lines from this remote corner of Utah
would not come amiss.
	We are having very peculiar weather,
stormy, sunshine, then storming again. Good
health prevails, except colds; peace and pros-
perity everywhere apparent.		
	As I have been absent most of the summer
I will give a short sketch of our journey. We
left home July 13th, 1888, traveled by team
to Springville, where my sister resides, and
spent the Twenty-fourth at that place. It was
very nice, indeed, but the dinner for the old
folks was the most tempting part to see, the
sumptuous table seated with our aged brethren
and sisters was a beautiful sight to behold;
their cheerful faces and dim eyes shone with
delight and sparkled as the viands were passed
by pleasant and tidy young waiters, eager to
do their best for the aged; although not one of
the number, I was all the more delighted to
have the privilege of looking on. After the re-
past and a recess came the fun of the enter-
tainment, which was creditable; also speeches

[column 2]
and toasts, with singing, some appropriate bal-
lads.
	On the 28th we took the afternoon train on
the D. & R. G. for Price, which place we
reached at half past six o’clock, and then a
ride of 25 or 30 miles to Huntington, where
our daughter resides, to visit her. The scenery
through Spanish Fork Cañon was beautifully
grand. The station at Price was not as large
as I expected to find it. We arrived at Hunt-
ington at half past 11 o’clock; all our friends
were well and pleased to see us. We prolonged
our visit here 12 days, but unfortunately it
rained most of the time, which put a stop to
our rambling about much. Huntington is a
flourishing town, and bids fair to be a large
city in the future.
	On the morning of the 8th we again returned
to Price station, and took the 8 o’clock
train for Pueblo; it being night we had no
view of the country, but as day dawned we
were astir to see the country we were whirling
through. Some parts looked rather barren; as
we neared the streams it became more fertile,
and as we climbed the mountains to Marshal
Pass it was grand to behold, and a wonder how
a road could be made for the iron horse to
climb such a mountain, and the beautiful little
stream, Crystal Creek, how sparkling it was
on its course. All through Grand Cañon it is
wonderful, and there are many nice little spots
inhabited by busy people. On we are carried,
until we reach Pueblo, at half-past 8 at night;
here we have to lay over till 4:20 in the morn-
ing, when we take the Atchison Topeka & Santa
Fe route for Deming. We pass over the
barren plains of Colorado, through cañons,
over trestles that span the wash out; they look
rather toppling for such a heavy, long train to
cross; here we are surprised to see the native
Mexicans and their homes scattered along; the
train was divided to climb the mountains of
Colorado; at the top was a long tunnel; when 
we passed through it we were in New Mexico.
Here we met another train waiting for ours to
come through. A stop of twenty minutes
here for dinner, and away we go down; much
faster than we came up. We begin to come to
grassy plains and meadows and herds of stock;
they looked much better than the cattle in
Colorado. The Mexicans become numerous;
also their huts, for such they appear to us;
some beautiful country, nice streams and fields
of corn and beans, and occasionally an onion
and pepper patch. We pass through Alber-
querque at midnight. The stations are small
all through this country, or towns, as they
should be called. We follow the Rio Grande
a long way; it is a shallow, muddy stream; the
bed seems to be mostly quicksand; fine large
cottonwood trees grow along its banks.
	At Rincon we change cars. The passengers
are a motley crew, from the American to the
negro; in one car I observed five different
nationalities, and such a clatter; a keen ob-
server of the physological would have an ex-
cellent opportunity to read character in the
faces.
	We reach Deming at 3:30 p.m.  The
weather is delightful for the 11th of August
in the south, with a nice breeze to cool the air.
This is quite a busy little place, noted for its
wind mills, as that is the only means of ob-
taining water; some fine buildings, dwellings
and stores; the American custom house is here,
but few gardens or trees; everything brought
here by rail, and very reasonable prices. At
this place we were met with carriages and
teams to take us to Diaz, 95 miles.
	On the 13th we start in the evening; the
roads are level and hard, the twilight most
delightful, and evening air cool and grass in
abundance; splendid country, with many
strange sights, and afar off are the grand old
mountains. We pass several  wind mills, that
are used to get water for the herds of cattle


[Column 1]

that range over the wide plains. We camped
here at night; had a shower, not much, but re-
freshing; had supper and a good night's rest;
up early, and travel again after the usual
round of breakfast and putting things straight
for the day. Now we are amongst the moskeet
bushes, also the cactus of various kinds, and
beautiful flowers of lovely hues. We now
come to the Casas Grande River and noon; we
are delighted with the many varieties of
foliage, and gather some wild grapes, while the
birds make a terrible chattering, for fear we
might take all; the curious turtle is seen
here. We meet the first Mexicans traveling,
an old wagon, two yoke of cattle, one horse,
the whole family of a dozen; and then to drive
the team, two on one side and one on the other,
several children in the rear following. We
soon come to the guide; he accompanies us the
remainder of the way; camped with us. Next
morning, Aug. 16, we halted opposite Diaz.
Our son, the Bishop, came and took me to
Diaz, while the rest went to La Ascension to
pass through the custom house, all arriving at
12 o'clock in the little settlement of Diaz,
where we were kindly greeted by friends and
relations, and to rest after our long, tedious
journey.
	After a week in Diaz, I will try and describe
the secluded settlement a few Latter Day
Saints away in this far off land.
	The streets are laid out as our cities are
usually. Main Street runs North and South,
with shade trees of cottonwood on either side,
and a water ditch on each street. The garden
abounds in all kinds of vegetables; including
the delicous water melon in abundance; also
fruit trees and flowers of gay gorgeous hues,
that make the paths look lovely with an eye
to home adornment. The houses are built of
adobies, very large ones, also the fences and
out-houses mostly. The weather was not so
warm as we expected to find it; the nights
were cool and pleasant. We took a trip to the
upper settlement G—— W—— among the
mountains; three days travel over beauti-
ful country, passed several Mexican towns very
comically built and laid out. Their churches,
their mode of worship was explained to us, all
of which seemed very singular and simple. In
the town is a fine building built for our
dear departed brother, Apostle E. Snow, and
also many nice houses, fields, gardens, and
orchards, with the beautiful river Casas Grande
running through it; we spent six days there
with warm friends, and feasted on sweet potatoes
and melons, and returned home through as de-
lightful a valley as I ever saw, and passed the
first place the Saints settled and had to leave.
We caught fish and duck; and had the pleasure
of seeing the majestic century plant growing
in its native state and beauty. We felt well
paid for our trip around. I must say some-
thing of the ruins; we passed one town all
in ruins, it looked so lonely; also some very
large ruins which appear to have been a temple
centuries ago, and many mounds on being
opened are found cooking vessels of an extinct
people, curious shapes and various sizes. On
our return we were surprised to note the growth
of the garden and shade trees. Our stay in
Mexico was seven weeks; when we left our
dear ones to return, children and grand-children,
our sons and others came to Deming with us,
a journey of four days; we came a different
route back home, we had the pleasure of seeing
more country. We returned from Deming
as we went; the weather was stormy part of
the way. At Alberquerque we saw the Pueblo
Indians, and bought grapes, peaches, and
apples of them. We reached Salt Lake City
Oct. 15th, made a short visit there, and left on
the 24th of Oct. for home; arrived Nov. 9th,
well satisfied with our journey and visit to old
Mexico. At each settlement I was pleased to
see that the little paper EXPONENT was highly

[Column 2]

prized by the sisters. There is a Relief Society,
Primary and Y. L. M. I. Association in each
settlement, and I was told that they were
well attended, and all seemed to be alive to
their duty; health seemed to be apparent every-
where; molasses making, and haying, all
seemed to be full of business, as always is the
case with the much abused "Mormons."
	With kind regards and success for the
always welcome little valuable EXPONENT, I
remain,
	Your sister in the Gospel,
		J. C. JOHNSON.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

A FEW STRICTURES BY MR. STEN-
HOUSE, EX-M. P. P.

TO THE EDITOR OF THE VANCOUVER WORLD:

	Sir:—I have first of all to congratulate you
on the success of your new venture in journal-
istic enterprise, a sample of which I am de-
lighted to receive. It is true your politics
would seem to have undergone some modifica-
tion—but what matters? Consistency, in these
days, is no proof of sincerity, while its oppo-
site is often redeemed by the noblest attributes
of patriotism. You must allow me, however,
to take exception to an article which appears,
under the head of "Horrors of Mormonism,"
in your weekly edition of November 1, though
I hope I am not expected to notice at any
length the strangely obscene talk of Mrs. A.
Newman, before an assembly of ladies in New
York. No wonder it elicited, as we are told,
"exclamations of horror from all parts of the
house." Such language and such allusions are
never heard among the Latter Day Saints, but
are common enough probably in the more
modern quarters of Salt Lake City, where the
"Missionaries to Utah," hook their fish and
call them "Mormons." To find such Mormons
it is not necessary for any one but a missionary
to go so far as Utah. We have some very
good fellows in the Northwest, who had earned
the playful prefix of "Mormon" long before
the arrival of the genuine stock. In Utah the
"Jack" or mock Mormon has a commercial
value, only to be appreciated by the despairing
missionary when his report is overdue. If the
putrid utterances of Mrs. Newman had one
grain of solid truth, I would assuredly quit the
priesthood and the communion of the Mormon
Church before the advent of another day. It
is the only church in Christendom, as far as I
know, where immorality of the laity, as well
as of the clergy, is punished by excommunica-
tion.
	Your assumption that I preside over the
Latter Day Saints in the Northwest is quite:
erroneous. Our President is Mr. C. O. Card,
a son-in-law of the late Brigham Young and
the first Mormon, as it happens, that I was
privileged to meet. Our church has no lack
of giants both in mind and stature, many of
whom are the product of our much decried
polygamy, and it would have been the most
ridiculous thing in the world had the local
presidency been assigned to an obscure monog-
amous born individual like the late member for
Comox.
	In another article you quote, with amusing
complacency, the last report of the anti-Mor-
mon Governor of Utah, in which he says:
"This priesthood not only rules the church but
governs the state, &c, &c." Well, and what
then? Will it surprise your readers to learn
that not a priest, bishop or president can be or-
dained or continued in office without the
assent of the people expressed by vote in open
meeting? Or will it stun you to hear that on
all such questions the ladies—the poor down-
trodden women of Utah—have an equal voice
with the men? If there is any church in Canada
where the ladies and the laity have more power
over their clergy, I shall be glad to hear of it

[Column 2]

	I would refer the candid inquirer to a book
of travels lately published by Phil. Robinson,
a Gentile, and a man of the world, entitled
"Sinners and Saints," (Boston: Roberts Bro-
thers).  No one can read the book without
feeling that the author is indeed a "seeker
after truth." He flatters neither sinner nor
saint.  Had he catered for either, his book
would have sold better.
	One word as to polygamy; and here I wish
to be understood that I am now speaking for
myself alone—not as a Mormon, but as a man.
Everybody knows that the criminal element in
bigamy is the deception which is practiced on
one, and sometimes two, of the three parties
interested. Among the Latter Day Saints,
polygamy can only be contracted by all who
are concerned in it. I have still to graduate
in mohogamy; but when I have once attained
such eminence, it is not the action or opinion
of intermeddling governments that will deter
me from seeking higher honors in matrimony.
As a free-born Briton, I shall certainly claim
equal rights with the Indian, Mahommedan,
and other subjects of Her Majesty. You will,
therefore, understand once for all, and quite
apart from any doctrine supposed to be taught
by Mormons, that monogamy, though consecra-
ted by centuries of triumphant error, is yet
very far from defining the creed or prescribing
the practice of your very faithful servant,
		A. MAITLAND STENHOUSE.
	Cardston, Alberta, Canada. Nov. 16, 1888.


THE ANNUAL WASHINGTON CON-
			VENTION.

	The National Woman Suffrage Association
will meet in Washington, D. C., January 21st,
22nd, and 23rd, 1889, at the Congregational
Church, corner of G. and 10th streets.
	Delegates from all the states are invited to be
present. The question of uniting the two so-
cieties will be again under consideration, as the
resolutions passed at the recent annual meeting
of the American society, held at Cincinnati,
demand some action on the part of the Na-
tional Association. For this and other reasons
a large gathering of the friends of this move-
ment is desirable.
	For twenty years in succession these conven-
tions have been held at the national capital,
with hearings before committees of the Senate
and House judiciary, and able minority reports
thereon, which have been extensively circulated
throughout the nation, and thus a great educa-
tional work has been accomplished. We see
the effect in the extension of the suffrage to 
woman, and a growing interest on her part in
public affairs.
	In the presidential canvass just ended, women
have taken a more active part than ever be-
fore, and shown that they are by no means a
unit on political questions. For the first time 
large delegations of women have marched with
banners, in various processions, and spoken on
the platforms of different political parties.
	Neither among politicians, nor women them-
selves, is this in any sense a party movement.
While the prohibition party incorporated
woman suffrage in its platform, the Republicans
of Kansas made it a fact by extending munici-
pal suffrage to the woman of that state. The
Democrats of Connecticut on several occasions
voted for woman suffrage while Republicans
voted against it. In the New York Legislature
Republicans and Democrats have alike advoca-
ted and voted for the measure. In Congress
the last vote in the House was eighty Repub-
licans for woman suffrage, and four Democrats
against it, and not a single Democrat voted in
favor of it on the floor of the Senate. Both 
the Labor and Greenback parties have uni-
formly recognized woman suffrage in their
platforms. 
	There are movements now in the political


horizon pointing to the formation of a national
party in the near future, combining the interests
of labor, woman, temperance and other reforms.
Our strength for future action lies in the fact
that woman suffrage has some advocates in all
parties, and that we, as a party, are pledged to
none.
	The denial of the ballot to woman is the
great political crime of the century, before
which tariff, finance; land monopoly, temper-
ance, labor, and all economic questions sink into
insignificance, for the right of suffrage involves
all questions of personal and property rights.
	While each party in power has refused to
enfranchise woman, being sceptical as to her
moral influence in government; yet with strange
inconsistency they alike seek the aid of her
voice and pen in all important political
struggles, as shown in the late presidential
campaign. While not morally bound to obey
the laws made without her consent, yet we find
woman the most law-abiding class of citizens
in the community. While not recognized as a
competent part of the government, she is most
active in all great movements for education,
religion, philanthropy and reform.
	The magnificent convocation of women from
the world over, held at Washington last March,
a council more important than any since the
Diet of Worms, was proof of woman's marvel-
ous power of organization; and her clear com-
prehension of the underlying principles of all
questions of government. With such evidence
of her keen insight and executive ability, we
invite all interested in good government to give
us the inspiration of their presence in the com-
ing convention.
		ELIZABETH CADY STANTON, Pres.
		SUSAN B. ANTHONY, Vice Pres.
		MATILDA JOSLYN GAGE, Ch. Ex. Com.
		RACHEL FOSTER AVERY, Cor. Sec.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

NOTES AND NEWS.

	Mrs. Harriet Beecher Stowe is said to be in
better health now than she has been for years,
and walks quite readily with the aid of a
cane.
	Lady Jane Henrietta Swinburne has en-
tered her ninetieth year. She is mother of
the poet and critic, Algernon Charles Swin-
burne.
	The Evelina de Rothschild School for Girls
at Jerusalem has been opened under the di-
rection of Mlle. Fortuee Behar. It has more
than four hundred pupils.
	The Governor of Massachusets has ap-
pointed Mrs. Kate Gannett Wells to the place
on the State Board of Education left vacant
by the death of Miss Abby W. May.
	Miss Abby W. May left $5,000 to the Home
for Aged Colored Women, $3,000 to the
Gwynne Temporary Home, and $1,000 to the
New England Hospital for Women and Child-
ren.
	At the regular monthly meeting of the ex-
ecutive committee of the Massachusetts
Woman Suffrage Association Dec. 7th, the
following was unanimously adopted:
	"In the death of our friend, Abby W. May,
woman suffrage has lost a wise and faithful
advocate, and higher education an experienced
and devoted friend. She gave twenty years
of heroic effort for the slave, five years for the
soldiers in camp and hospital, more than
twenty years for the legal and political equality
of woman. Her example will be to us an in-
centive; her memory an inspiration.—Ed.
	Talk about the trials of genius in endeavor-
ing to make itself known to a cold world! The
truth is that nowadays in these United States
there is hospitality to new writers which often

[Column 2]

becomes ludicrous in its eagerness. Authors
of common place abilities spring into sudden
prominence through no fault of their own;
and there is no danger that any writer who
possesses the least spark of originality will be
overlooked. There is no Chatterbox business
going on now. What is most needed is a
higher standard of literary taste and less spas-
modic enthusiasm.—Ex.
	A horrible occurrence is related as having
taken place at Erie, Pa. Mrs. William
Savory and Miss Lucy Stinson were bosom
friends. Miss Stinson died, and Mrs. Savory
insisted upon impressing a last kiss upon the
lips of her friend. She did so. But the un-
dertaker had just treated the face with a pre-
paration for preserving the color. Mrs.
Savory was infected, and subsequently died
from blood poisoning—a most remarkable in-
stance of the folly of a custom that has noth-
ing to recommend it but sentimentality, and
everything against it in the light of sanitary
laws and the doctrine of common sense.—Ex.
	Mrs. Cleveland has succeeded in raising one
hundred thousand dollars in order to rebuild
Wells college, the female institution where
she was graduated. It is situated a few miles
from Ithaca, N. Y. It was destroyed by fire a
few months ago. The trustees had given up
the prospect of rebuilding as impossible, when
Mrs. Cleveland came forward and gave the dis-
tressed institution her personal attention. She
not only contributed a large sum of money
herself, but she circulated a paper and induced
others to subscribe, until money enough has
been raised to rebuild the college in better 
style than it was originally.—Ex.
	"God and the Women in American Public
Affairs" was the suggestive heading of a ser-
mon preached Sunday, Dec. 16, in Tremont
Temple, Boston, by Rev. Emory J. Haynes. 
He said:  It was the influence of the woman
in the home, by the breakfast table, over the
father and brother, that was the one factor in
the last municipal election which did more
than any other to win the victory. We have
our publicists and politicians, but it is to the
women we must look for the future of the
country. That is what did it—the influence of
home. These same home influences are going
to continue at all the municipal elections. That
is the great power of women. Woman, I be-
lieve in you! The records show that the
criminals are five men to one woman. To be
pure and self-sacrificing comes to women more
naturally than to men. We believe in you
because you have led men in the Christian
church. I hope you will help us to right a
thousand wrongs.—Ex.


Women have more strength in their looks
than we have in our laws, and more power by
their tears than we have by our arguments.—
Savile.
	In a truly good character we look, first of
all, for integrity, or an unbending regard to
recitude; then for independence, or the habitual
determination to be governed by an enlight-
ened conviction of truth and duty; then for
benevolence, or the spirit of kindness and
good will to men; and last, but not least, for
piety toward God, or an affectionate, reverent
regard for the will and glory of the great
Jehovah.—Hawes.


OBITUARIES.

	DIED, at Union, Salt Lake County, Dec. 6th, 1888,
Mary Ann Alberta Cunningham, the beloved daughter of
Hyrum H. and Della A. Brady, of membranous croup,
aged one year, ten months and eight days.

	I laid my darling down with a sigh,
		In grief, for sorrow was too deep;


My anguished heart could only cry,
		"O, God in heaven, my darling keep."

	I know my darling's form will wait
		Beyond this world, where grief's dark night
	Enshroud my saddened life, at heaven's gate
		I'll meet my child where all is light.
			M. A. SMART.

	DIED, at East Bountiful, Davis Co., Utah, after an ill-
ness of two months, Sept. 23rd, 1888, Emma Maria, be-
loved wife of Daniel Wood. Deceased was born July 12,
1834, in Plymouth, England. Baptized Feb. 25, 1851, by
Charles Phelps. She embraced the order of plural mar-
riage, was the mother of six children and twenty-three
grandchildren. Sister Wood was a faithful member in the
Relief Society, and labored in the capacity of a teacher
for many years; was always prompt to her meetings and
fulfilling her duties honorably, and died in full faith of the
Gospel, and in the hope of a glorious resurrection.
	And as teaohers of the Relief Society we feel to offer to
her memory the following:

RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whereas, in the Providence of our Heavenly Father He
has seen fit to take from our Society our sister, Emma
Maria Wood, and,
	Whereas, our departed friend and sister was worthy of
our warmest affection, we feel that proper recognition of
her goodness and virtue be had among us. Therefore,
be it
	Resolved, That we, as members of the Relief Society of
East Bountiful, deeply mourn the loss of our sister and
hold her name in affectionate remembrance.
	Resolved, That we extend our sympathy to her family,
and that we try to live that we may be prepared to meet
her, when we have finished our work here below.
	Resolved, that these Resolutions be spread on our record,
also a copy sent to the WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
		ANN DUSTIN,
		JANE HOLT, Committee.
		ESTHER SESSIONS,

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL. 17.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JANUARY 15, 1889.	NO. 16.


TREASURES.

"Will you keep my new knife, mother?
	I might dull the shining blade;
Please to keep it ready for me,
	Or I'll lose it, I'm afraid."
"Yes, I'll keep it for you, darling,"
	And I kiss the rosy lips,
While upon his golden ringlets,
	Softly rest my finger tips,

"Please to keep my marbles, muzzer,
	So zey won't get rolled away;"
"And my pretty bead and buttons;"
	And my top string, while I play."
Four dear boys thus bring their treasures,
	I must keep them all in reach;
Four loved forms to watch and care for,
	Four bright infant minds to teach.

As to me they bring their treasures,
	So to Thee, oh, God above!
I entrust them, ever praying,
	Keep them safely in Thy love.
And, lest I should spoil or lose them,
	'Mid earth's busy cares and strife,
Teach me, Father, how to bring them,
	Back to Thee with Endless Life!

		LULA


REVERIE.

We laugh when our souls are the saddest,
	We shroud all our griefs in a smile;
Our voices may warble their gladdest,
	And our souls mourn in anguish the while.

And our eyes wear a summer's bright glory,
	When winter is wailing beneath;
And we tell not the world the sad story
	Of the thorn hidden back of the wreath.

Ah! fast flow the moments of laughter,
	And bright as the brook to the sea;
But ah! the dark hours that come after
	Of moaning for you and for me.

Yea, swift as the sunshine, and fleeting
	As birds, fly the moments of glee!
And we smile; and mayhap grief is sleeting
	Its ice upon you and on me.

And the clouds of the tempest are shifting
	O'er the heart, tho' the face may be bright;
And the snows of woe's winter are drifting
	Our souls; and each day hides a night.
 
For ah! when our souls are enjoying
	The mirth which our faces reveal,
There is something—a something—alloying
	The sweetness of joy that we feel.

Life's loveliest sky hides the thunder,
	Whose bolt in a moment may fall,
And out path may be flowery; but under
	The flower there are thorns for us all.

Ah! 'tis hard when our beautiful dreamings.
	That flash down the valley of night,
Wave their wing when the gloom hides their gleaming,
	And leave us, like eagles in flight;

And fly far away nnreturning,
	And leave us in terror and tears,
While vain is the spirit's wild yearning
	That they may come back in the years.

Come back! did I say it? but never
	Do eagles come back to the cage:
They have gone—they have gone—and forever!
	Does youth come back ever to age?

No! a joy that has left us in sorrow
	Smiles never again on our way;


But we meet in the farthest to-morrow
	The face of the grief of to-day.

The brightness whose tremulous glimmer
	Has faded we cannot recall;
And the light that grows dimmer and dimmer—
	When gone—'tis forever and all.

Not a ray of it anywhere lingers,
	Not a gleam of it gilds the vast gloom;
Youth's roses perfume not the fingers
	Of age groping nigh to the tomb.

For the "memory of joy is a sadness"—
	The dim twilight after the day;
And the grave where we bury a gladness
	Sends a grief, like a ghost, on our way.

No day shall return that has faded,
	The dead come not back from the tomb;
The vale of each life must be shaded,
	That we may see best from the gloom.

The height of the home of our glory
	All radiant with splendors of light;
That we may read clearly life's story—
	"The dark is the dawn of the bright."

		A. J. R.


WOMAN SUFFRAGE MEETING.

AN ASSOCIATION FOR UTAH.

	An official call having been previously
issued, signed by influential women of the
Territory, for a meeting of suffragists, to be
held in the Salt Lake Assembly Hall, Thurs-
day, January 10, 1889, accordingly, in re-
sponse there was a large number of ladies in
attendance, residents of Salt Lake City and
vicinity, and representative women from other
parts of the Territory. Upon the platform
were several prominent ladies—Mrs. Zina D.
H. Young and Mrs. Margaret P. Young, Mrs.
Emily S. Richards, Mrs. Lena Wells, Mrs.
Nellie Webber, Mrs. Lydia D. Alder, Mrs.
Charlotte I. Kirby, Mrs. Maria Y. Dougall,
Mrs. Birdie Cummings, Mrs. L. Ardelle Cum-
mings, Mrs. Phebe Y. Beatie, Mrs. Julia C.
Taylor, Mrs. Edith Knowlton and others. At
the reporters' table were seated Mrs. E. B.
Wells, Editor of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT,
and Mrs. R. B. Pratt, M. D. Opposite the
platform were Mrs. M. I. Horne, Mrs. Sarah
M. Kimball, Mrs. Jane S. Richards, Mrs. B.
W. Smith, Mrs. Mary Pitchforth,Mrs. Elizabeth
Howard, Mrs. Nellie Little and other distin-
guished ladies.
	Mrs. Zina D. H. Young stated the object of 
the meeting, and that Mrs. Emily S. Richards
had been duly authorized to preside and to
organize a Woman Suffrage Association for
Utah Territory, having been appointed Chair-
man of the Executive Committee for Utah at
the last Annual Convention of the N. W. S. A.
in Washington, D. C., April, 1888. Mrs.
Richards then took the chair and said, "Will
some one nominate a Secretary?" Mrs. M. Y.
Dougall noninated Mrs. L. D. Alder as Secre-
tary pro tem. Mrs. Ardelle Cummings seconded
the motion. Carried.
	The meeting opened with singing by the con-
gregation, "Know this, that every soul is free;"
and an earnest prayer was offered by Mrs. M.
I. Horne.
	The Chairman then requested the Secretary
to read the Call, which was responded to, and
the call was read.
	The ladies of Utah Territory who are inter-

[Column 2]

ested in the subject of woman's suffrage con-
template organizing a Woman's Suffrage Asso-
ciation.
	For this purpose a meeting of suffragists
will be held in the Assembly Hall, Salt Lake
City, on Thursday, January 10, 1889, com-
mencing at 2 p.m.
	All ladies interested in the cause of woman's 
enfranchisement and political advancement are
cordially invited to be present and take part
in the meeting.
			Respectfully,
		EMILY S. RICHARDS,
Chairman Ex. Com. N. W. S. A. for Utah.
		ZINA D. H. YOUNG,
	Prest. Cen. Board Relief Society.
		JANE S. RICHARDS,
		BATHSHEBA W. SMTIH,
Vice-Prest's Cen. Board Relief Society.
		JOSEPHINE R. WEST,
	Prest. P. A. Associations Weber Co.
		DR. ELLEN B. FERGUSON,
		ELIZABETH HOWARD,
		ROMANIA B. PRATT, M. D.,
		E. B. WELLS,
	Ex-Vice-Prest. N. W. S. A. for Utah.
	Members National Woman's Suffrage Asso-
ciation.
	Chairman said, "You have heard the Call
and understand its object; we will now proceed
to organize."
	Mrs. M. Y. Dougall nominated Mrs. Margaret
Caine, President of the Woman Suffrage Asso-
ciation of Utah; the nomination was seconded,
and the motion put by the Chairman and
carried unanimously. Chairman suggested
that some one nominate three Vice-Presidents.
Mrs. C. I. Kirby nominated Mrs. Lydia D.
Alder as First Vice-President; seconded by 
the ladies on the platform; Chairman put the 
motion; carried. Mrs. L. D. Alder nominated
Mrs. Nellie Webber, Second Vice-President;
seconded by several of the ladies on the plat-
form; carried. Mrs. Ardelle Cummings nomi-
nated Mrs. Priscilla Jennings Riter, Third
Vice-President; seconded and carried. Mrs.
Birdie Cummings nominated Mrs. Cornelia
H. Clayton, Secretary of the Association; mo-
tion seconded and carried, and Mrs. C. I.
Kirby Corresponding Secretary; carried. Mrs.
Birdie Cummings nominated Miss Margie
Dwyer, Treasurer of the Association; carried.
Chairman said, "The appointing of an Execu-
tive Committe is now in order." Mrs. Alder
nominated Mrs. M. Y. Dougall, Chairman of 
Executive Committee, and the names of Mrs.
Nettie Y. Snell, Mrs. Ann E. Groesbeck, Mrs.
Phebe Y. Beatie and Mrs. Jennie Rowe were
then presented as members of such committee;
voted upon and carried. Chairman asked the
secretary to please read the Constitution of the
National Woman Suffrage Association. Secre-
tary read as desired.

CONSTITUTION.
	ART. 1. This organization shall be called
the National Woman Suffrage Association.
	ART. 2. The object of this Association shall
be to secure national protection for women in
the exercise of their citizen's right to vote.
	ART. 3. All citizens of the United States
subscribing to this Constitution, and contribut-
ing not less than one dollar annually, shall be
considered members of this Association.
	ART. 4. The officers of this Association


[Column 1]

﻿shall be a president, vice-president from each
of the states and territories, corresponding and
recording secretaries, a treasurer, and an exe-
cutive committee of not less than five.
	ART. 5. A quorum of the executive com-
mittee shall consist of nine, and all officers of
this Association shall be exofficio members of
this committee, with power to vote.
	ART. 6. All woman suffrage societies
throughout the country shall be welcomed as
auxiliaries, and their accredited officers or duly
appointed representatives shall be recognized as
members of the National Association.
	Chairman: "You have heard the Constitution
of the N. W. S. A., what is the pleasure of
the meeting?" Mrs. Alder: "I move it be
adopted." This motion was seconded by a large
number of ladies present, and the  Constitu-
tion was adopted.
	Chairman suggested that some one nominate a
Committee on By-Laws. Mrs. Dougall moved
that the Chairman appoint the Committee.
Chairman appointed Mrs. Lottie Young, Mrs.
Julia C. Taylor, Edith Knowlton, Committee
on By-Laws. Committee retired.
	While the Committee were out Mrs. C. I.
Kirby addressed the audience. She spoke of
the advantages to be derived from woman
suffrage, and presented some of her views and
the information she had obtained at home and
abroad in regard to woman's work and influence.
When Mrs. Kirby concluded, Mrs. Jane S.
Richards was called for, but declined; and Mrs.
Z. D. H. Young responded to her name, and
addressed the meeting. Her speech was elo-
quent and pathetic; she appealed to the heart:
her subject was, "Woman and Motherhood,"
and her ideas were so gracefully conveyed, that
every heart was touched in a tender place.
	The Committee came in and reported, and
Mrs. Julia C. Taylor read the "By-Laws and
Regulations." After the reading some one
moved that they be read and voted upon separ-
ately. The Secretary read as requested, and
the By-Laws and Regulations were adopted
separately. The following is a copy:

BY-LAWS AND REGULATIONS
			
ARTICLE I.
	This Association shall be called the Woman
Suffrage Association of Utah.

ARTICLE II. 
	The object of the Association shall be to
work for the enfranchisement, of all women,
without regard to party, sect, or creed, and to
take such steps from time to time as shall seem
to be wise and expedient for the political ad-
vancement of the women of this Territory.

ARTICLE III.
	The officers of this Association shall be a
President, three Vice-Presidents, a Secretary,
Corresponding Secretary and Treasurer, with
an Executive Committee of not less than five.

ARTICLE IV.
	All persons wishing to become members of
this Association must pay an initiation fee of
25 cts. annually, and must give or send their
names with the money to the Secretary, who
will present them in the order of business to
the next regular meeting for admission.

ARTICLE V.
	The officers of this Association, with the
Executive Committee, shall have power to
change or amend the By-Laws and Regula-
tions at any Executive session, and shall
then submit them to the regular meeting, where
they may be adopted by a majority vote.

ARTICLE VI.
	The Executive Committee shall devise plans
of work suited to the development and progress

[Column 2]

of the members of the Association, and co-
operate together with the members in encourag-
ing young people to speak, write essays and
discuss questions upon political matters and
civil government, and such other subjects as
relate thereto; and invite those not already in-
terested in suffrage, and encourage them to
become acquainted with the objects of the
organization.

ARTICLE VII.
	The names of delegates to the Annual Con-
vention of the N. W. S. A. at Washington
shall be presented and voted upon at the an-
nual meeting of the Association, previous to
said Convention, or a special meeting called
for the purpose. The Credentials of the dele-
gates shall be prepared by the Executive Com-
mittee and properly signed by the Officers of
the Association.

ARTICLE VIII.
	All official papers and documents shall be
signed by the President and Secretary, or, in
the absence of the President, Vice-President
next in order, and all official correspondence
must be signed by the Chairman of the Execu-
tive Committee and the Corresponding Secre-
tary.
 
ARTICLE IX.
	Regular meetings of this Association shall
be held once a month, and such business tran-
sacted as shall be brought to the attention of
the meeting by the Executive Committee or
the Secretary, or other officers, after which
miscellaneous business or discussion will be in
order.

ARTICLE X.
	A punctual attendance of the members is
particularly requested, in order to further the
interests of the whole, and to promote harmony
in all plans and methods of organized work.

ARTICLE XI.
	All local suffrage associations that may be
organized throughout the Territory, by adopt-
ing these rules, or similar ones, and the Con-
stitution of the N. W. S. A., shall be considered
auxiliaries, and their representatives or dele-
gates will be expected to attend a Territorial
Suffrage Convention should one be called, and
entitled to the privileges pertaining thereto.

	A suggestion was made in reference to dele-
gates to the National Woman Suffrage Conven-
tion to be held in Washington, D. C., Jan. 21,
22 and 23, and Mrs. Kirby nominated Mrs.
Emily S. Richards as one of the delegates,
which was seconded and sustained by unani-
mous vote. Mrs. Margaret Caine was then
nominated by Mrs. Alder, and the nomination
seconded she was chosen unanimously. The
Credentials for the delegates were alluded to,
and the officers were reminded that the Execu-
tive Committee were authorized to prepare the
Credentials. The ladies came forward and
gave their names to the Secretary, and about
one hundred were enrolled members of the
Association.
	An adjournment was moved, as there was
no further business, and the motion being
seconded the meeting was adjourned for one
month, notice of time and place of meeting to be
published by the Secretary.

OFFICERS.
		EMILY S. RICHARDS,
	Ch. Ex. Com. N. W. S. A. for Utah.
WOMAN SUFFRAGE ASSOCIATION OF UTAH:

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.﻿


PLEASANT PARTY.

	A number of the officers of the Primary
Associations of Davis Stake met at the resi-
dence of Sister Aurelia S. Rogers, at Farming-
ton, Dec. 28,1888, to manifest their apprecia-
tion of her past labors, in the form of a sur-
prise, she having been confined to her bed for
nearly six weeks.
	Her First Counselor, Sister Julia Hess, was
unanimously chosen to take charge of the
exercises, which consisted of remarks from all
the sisters present, and songs from the Prim-
ary Hymn Book; also an original poem was
rendered by the author, Counselor Lucy A.
Clark.
	After listening to many expressions of affec-
tion and words of encouragement from the sis-
ters, Sister Rogers sat up in her bed and though,
very weak, said words could not express her
gratitude to the sisters who had come to see
her, for she would never forget it; though there
were some of the presidents absent, yet she
thought of them all, and all seemed as dear to
her as her own sisters. She cherished the love
and good wishes of the sisters more than gold
or silver. During her sickness she had been
near the “other side,” but thought that if God
would spare her longer she would serve Him
better. Feared that we allowed our minds to
dwell more on the things of this world than on
the things of God, which is not right; knew she
would be better. She blessed the sisters in all
their outgoings and incomings, and invoked
the blessings of God upon all His chosen ones.
The Primary hymn, “Nay, speak no ill,” was
sang, after which her Counselors presented her
with a beautiful plush wrap, bought by the
Primaries of the Stake, which was received
with gratitude unspeakable. The sisters then
sung, “The Spirit of God like a fire is burn-
ing,” and then the tables were spread, and
about twenty-five persons, including the Presi-
dency of the Stake, the Bishop and Counselors
and a number of brethren who were invited in
after the priesthood meeting, partook of a
bounteous repast, after which the brethren ad-
ministered to Sister Rogers, and then dispersed
to their several homes, feeling that it was good
to have been there.
		ANNA S. ROCKWOOD,
		KATE M. CHASE,
		ELIZABETH G. FORD, Committee.
		WEALTHY R. CLARK, Secretary.


Another meeting was held last week to pro-
mote the return of women as county council-
lors. The devoted ladies who are going to
make victims of themselves and test the eligi-
bility of women as candidates, are Margaret
Lady Sandhurst and Miss Jane Cobden, for
London, and Mrs. Massingberd, for the Lind-
say division of Lincolnshire. Both the two
first named ladies addressed the meeting at St.
James’s Hall on Saturday. The audience
was largely composed of ladies. The chair-
man, Mr. Channing, M. P., advised all lady
candidates to persist in their candidature, as
he believed that the first rejected nomination
would be the surest way of getting rid of the
barrier that some said existed.— Westminster
and Lambeth Gazette.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WHITE CHRYSANTHEMUMS.

	Born of the clouds and the darkness,
		Of the frosts and the early snow;
	When the summer blooms have faded,
		The beautiful Christ-flowers blow.
	All through the budding springtime,
		All through the summer's heat,
	All through the autumn's glory,
		They hide their blossoms sweet.
	But when the earth is lonely,
		And the bitter north winds blow,
	With a smile of cheer for the dear old year,
		The Christmas blossoms blow.

	Sweet as the dream of summer,
		White as the drifting snow;
	When our hearts are filled with grieving,
		The beautiful Christ-flowers blow.
	Not all the south-wind's wooing
		Opens their secret heart;
	Slender they grow and stately,
		Guarding their life apart.
	But when the earth is dreary,
		And heavy clouds hang low,
	With their tender cheer for the way-worn year,
		The Christmas blossoms blow.

	Sweetest of all consolers!
		Fairest of flowers that grow !
	When hopes and flowers have faded,
		The beautiful Christ-flowers blow.
	Bright in the cottage window,
		Sweet in the darkened room,
	Fair in the shortened sunlight,
		Cheering the dusky gloom.
	Oh, when our hearts are lonely,
		And clouds of care hang low,
	What blessed cheer for our dying year,
		The Christmas blossoms blow !
									—Selected.


AT THE RANCHE.

I write you as one in my Christmas letter,
As in life you have been for worse or for better;
God grant that the worst has all gone bye,
That "the better 'twill be in the year drawing nigh.
'Tis the birthday of Jesus, sweet peace to the soul
He can give, though dark billows may over us roll,
Let us thank Him for all of His mercy and love,
And pray He may guide us still onward above.
The past year brought shadow, but, too, it brought light,
The gleams of the morning still follow the night.
'Twas hard when another was called from the fold,
When the eold earth claimed our Mattie to hold;
But we all know she's called back unto God,
'Tis only the casket that sleeps 'neath the sod.
Close, close by her mother, whose last earthly care,
Was for the frail Mattie who needed her care,
Again can I hear her wild, pitiful cry,
"Oh, God! I can't live if my mother should die."
Don't think I am sorrowful, no, when I think
Of the joining again of that severed link,
I forget our own loss, our sorrow and pain,
When I know unto them 'tis an eternal gain;
I will not turn back the leaves of the past.
The light and the shadow over them cast,
For we have shared them all together,
The stormy and the pleasant weather.
I am thinking now of the children all,
With a loving wish for both large and small;
Johnie, the first of our precious band,
Born upon Zion's favored land,
Unto her colors be firm and true,
And the crown of the victor awaiteth you.
I cannot speak of them all apart,
But each hath a place in my inmost heart;
There's Belle and Nettie, and Minnie dear,
Have helped to brighten my pathway here.
Soon again may we have our Christmas tree
All together, and—let me see—
Let us appoint our committee here,
To take the lead in our reunion cheer—
Mamie and Susie, Jen and Fan
(You may find a better one if you can),
With the two Johns and Vaughan, that's seven,
Or had we not better have eleven?
For Georgie and Lucy, Alex and Sam


Will have to look out for a Juvenile Jam,
For there'll be a troop of them, little and big;
All tumbling and jumping or dancing a jig.
There's all of Sam's, with young Sam at the head,
Propitious all of the "trundle bed,"
With Belle's and Nettie's, and how many more
Are coming along from the heavenly shore?
And all the homeless, you know are mine,
There'll be quite a few of them to dine!
Minnie and Belle the two shall be
To receive our guests to the Christmas tree;
And Nettie shall sing,
	"Hush, my dear, lie still and slumber,
		Holy angels guard thy bed;
	Heavenly blessings without number,
		Gently falling on thy head."
			S. E. R.
Dec. 25th, 1888.


CHRISTMAS TIME IN MEXICO.

EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	Well! here I am, away up in the mountains
of Mexico, and nearly Christmas, the time of
"Peace on earth, good will to men." How
strange the changes which occur, and we notice
them more when Christmas time comes, as we
look back from year to year, conning over the
gay and merry time of childhood, and the none
the less enjoyable occasions of more mature
years; such a train of thought is awakened that
it becomes a task to write, unless one had a gift
for dotting down one's thoughts as they come,
and making them more readable than I feel 
capable of doing.
	Where are the playmates of childhood? the
companions of youth? Time has no doubt
brought changes to them as great as to us.
How are the absent loved ones, and how will
they spend the holidays, are queries often put
by the children, bless them! Christmas has a
charm for happy, innocent childhood, no matter
how simple the preparations, how limited the
presents, the fact that they are made by loving
hands, makes them, I sometimes think, more
appreciated, than would be more costly ones.
	And after all a view of the fine display and
magnificent array of Christmas goods in the
show windows of Salt Lake, and that delight
of childhood, the Christmas tree, with its
weight of good things, cannot alone bring
happiness, even to our little ones. By the way
there is no lack of Christmas trees here, if they
do lack the trimming, for this is a land of
pines; we certainly have some magnificent
scenery here, and when viewed either in the
daytime by the light of the glorious sunshine,
or by the light of the full round moon, it seems
to me it would be hard to excel the crags and
peaks of the majestic mountains, the tall,
straight pines casting their shadows eastward,
and the evergreen oaks with their thick, dark
foliage, and over all the clear blue sky, the
twinkling stars, and mellow light of the moon,
the queen of night, forms a scene which is
indescribably lovely, and has a charm for me
which I cannot explain.
	I have been for a lovely walk with the
children, at the earnest solicitation of my
boys, and have enjoyed it very much; we
climbed their favorite peak, almost solid rock
seemingly, and yet with soil enough for a com-
paratively thick growth of trees, and from
which we can trace the winding of the river,
the "Peadris Verde," and across that, perhaps
a mile or two distant, a large group of curious
looking rocks, perfectly bare of vegetation,
which look like so many old castles of every
kind and description. I felt well paid for the
climbing, and returned with a bouquet of dry
grass, oak leaves, etc., and several varieties of
beautiful moss from the trees and rocks.
	On our way here a few weeks ago we passed
one peak of this range of mountains, which
was different to anything I ever saw before,
towering perhaps five hundred feet above us at


the side of the road.  It took but little stretch
of the imagination to convert the rocks at its
summit into multitudes of people, some gaily
and others grotesquely attired; one figure in
particular attracted my attention, that of an
old and venerable monk seated in an old-
fashioned, straight backed arm chair, just as I
have seen in pictures; some elegantly dressed
ladies just out on a shopping expedition.  But
I shall tire your readers, so adieu for the
present.					KATE.
	Sierra Madre Mountains, Mexico, Dec. 23,
1888.


SIMPLE SIMON.
     It is very ridiculous and altogether unbe-
coming for men who have come into this com-
munity as business men, and been patronized
by the people to turn round and berate those
who have never done aught against them,
merely, it would appear, for the sake of popu-
larity.  What other motive could prompt men
whose business interests are here, to go east
and voluntarily try to suggest a “holy crusade”
against a practice already legislated upon
severely by the Congress of the United States.
Ill-timed and in very bad taste are all such
speeches.  Whoever tries to climb up by pull-
ing another down, will surely meet his just
deserts.  It is in keeping with some others,
however, who wield the pen more than the
needle, who, living among the “Mormons,”
receiving their patronage, associating with
them in neighborhoods socially, and depending
upon them for friendly aid and assistance in
times of sickness, are constrained from their
very love of popularity, to misrepresent them
through the press and in public assemblies, be-
cause it is the fashion.  Now one can see
plainly that milliners must be a la mode, and
therefore perhaps should be excused, but what
grievance had this man to present to the Presi-
dent-elect?  had Gen. Harrison inquired “What
have you to complain of in Utah?” he would
doubtles have been forced to acknowledge, like
the other simple Simon, “Indeed I have not
any.”
	"Simple Simon met a pieman
		Going to the Fair;
	Said Simple Simon to the pieman
		Let me taste your ware;
	Said the pieman to Simple Simon,
		Show me first your penny;
	Said Simple Simon to the pieman,
		Indeed I have not any."
							MILLINER.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	No man can answer for his own valor or
courage till he has been in danger—Rochefou-
cault.
	Drinking water neither makes a man sick,
nor in debt, nor his wife a widow.—Spanish
Maxim.
	There seem to be but three ways for a nation
to acquire wealth: the first is by war, as the
Romans did, in plundering their conquered
neighbors—this is robbery; the second by com-
merce, which is generally cheating; the third
by agriculture—the only honest way wherein a
man receives a real increase of the seed thrown
into the ground, is in a kind of continual miracle,
wrought by the hand of God in his favor, as a
reward for his innocent life and his virtuous
industry.—Franklin.


The State University of Oregon has 15
women students.
     Mrs. Ednah D. Cheney is to write the life of
Louisa M. Alcott.
