		EDITORIAL NOTES.
	
	THE Secretaries of the Relief Society in this
Stake of Zion are hereby particularly requested
to prepare correct semi-annual reports, and for-
ward them not later than the 25th of February,
to the Stake Secretary, Mrs. Elizabeth Howard,
No. 60, Fourth South St., Salt Lake City, in order
that a full and complete report may be made in
time for the Stake Conference. By order of Mrs.
M. Isabella Horne, President Relief Society,
Salt Lake Stake.
	THE Secretary of the Central Board of the Y.
L. M. I. Associations is particularly anxious to
obtain correct semi-annual reports from each
Stake of Zion. These should be forwarded so as
to reach her not later than March 10th, so as to
enable her to compile them in time for General
Conference on April 6th. The secretaries of the
several Stakes are therefore desired to send
promptly to Mary E. Cook, 323 East, Third South
Street, Salt Lake City, Utah.
	THE Secretary of the Central Board of the
Primary Associations is desirous of obtaining a
full report from each Stake of Zion for the com-
﻿plete report to be prepared in time for the April
Conference, and this semi-annual report should
be ready ten days before, or about the 25th of
March. Consequently the reports from all points
ought to be in the hands of the Secretary as
early as the 10th of March at the latest, which
would give her then only two weeks time to pre-
pare them. It is a very important thing, and the
Presidents of Primary Associations in the several
Stakes of Zion, should see that this matter is
properly attended to, both as to correct figures
and statements and date of sending. Forward
the Stake reports to Mrs. Cornelia H. Clayton,
No. 115 Third Street, Salt Lake City.
	"FREEDOM'S CONQUESTS" is a wonderful show-
ing of facts and figures in relation to the spread
of Woman Suffrage in America, Africa, Europe,
Asia and Oceanica.. The book is by Hamilton
Wilxoc, New York City, an able worker in the

[Column 2]
suffrage cause, and one who has always spoken
without prejudice of the suffrage in Utah. The
story is briefly told, and one might say the whole
is alphabetically and mathematically arranged,
for the writer has given the number of square
miles of islands and countries, and the number of
inhabitants, and so forth. Mr. Wilcox says,
"The cold facts are as usual beyond the greatest
achievements of fancy." "The figures are all
from the latest officials returns and estimates."
We shall be pleased to make some extracts from
the pamphlet at some future date.
	THE annual meeting of the Young Ladies' Mu-
tual Improvement Association of the Seventeenth
Ward was held in the upper hall of the ward
meeting house, Tuesday evening, January 29th,
Mrs. Julia C. Howe presiding with ease and
dignity. There were quite a number of young
ladies present and several visitors from other
wards; and the Bishop's Counselor, Alonzo Hyde,
was on the stand. After the usual opening
exercises minutes of the last annual meeting were
read and accepted, and the President announced
the reading of a manuscript paper, entitled, "The
Quarterly Review." Miss Lucy Woodruff stepped
forward and read in an easy and graceful manner,
the editorial, "Stray Thoughts," and other con
tents of the paper, "The Gifts of the Gospel,"
Sketch of Gen. Geo. Washington; also of the life of
Jared from the Book of Ether, and a Correspond-
ence between Mrs. Maria Y. Dougall and Miss
Ella Dallas, in which there was much food for
reflection and comment. During the intervals
between the reading of each article short speeches
were made by the sisters present—Sisters E. S.
Tayor, M. I. Horne, R. B. Pratt, and E.
B. Wells. After these exercieses were concluded,
Coun. Hyde occupied a few minutes in speaking
to the young ladies. Programme for the next
meeting was read by the Secretary, and the meet-
ing closed with singing and benediction, which
was pronounced by Sister B. W. Smith. This
Association we consider a very progressive one,
and a pattern in many respects for others to
follow. The young ladies of the ward have ac-
quitted themselves creditably on several occasions
in special meetings and entertainments, and have
shown that they were really engaged in the
cause of improvement, mentally, morally and
spiritually.


[Column 1]
IN MEMORIAM.
	Nancy Broadbent Folsom, wife of Hyrum P.
Folsom, after suffering thirty-one days with pleuro
pneumonia, died at 1:50 p.m. January 12, 1889, in
this city, at her residence in the Nineteenth Ward,
near the Warm Springs; aged 46 years, 7 months
and 24 days. Deceased was born in Hursh, Ash-
ton, Underline, Lancashire, England, May 18, 1842. 
She was a devoted wife and mother; a friend to
the poor, and a faithful Latter-day Saint, and 
many will mourn her loss. She leaves a family of
six children, four sons and two daughters, the
youngest four years old. 
	On January 15th, as previously announced, the
remains left the family residence, arriving at the
Nineteenth Ward meeting house at 11 a.m., which 
was crowded on the occasion. Appropriate and 
consoling remarks were made by Elders Joseph
H. Felt, Edwin Frost, Martin Lenzi, Bishop Geo.
Romney and Robert Smith. The funeral cortege
was one block and a half long. 
	In connection with her demise we have received
the following statement, which is somewhat re-
markable: 
	"As Sister Broadbent was getting in the car-
riage to go and see the remains of Mrs. H. P.
Folsom, her daughter, she received a telegram
from Terra Haute, Indiana, that another daughter
of hers, Mrs. C. M. Daggett, expired at 7:45 p.m.
There was thus only about three hours difference 
[Column 2]
in the time of the death of the two daughters. It
is also a remarkable fact that Sister Broadbent
dreamed ten days ago that her daughters were
both dead, and that Sister Folsom died first, and
so it happened." 
	Adding to this singular coincidence, the funerals
of the two sisters were conducted at the same 
hour and day, although two thousand miles apart.
	
ANNIVERSARY OF NUPTIAL DAY OF HYRUM AND
NANCY B. FOLSOM. UTAH PENITENTIARY,
DEC. 29, 1886.

To-day I've been thinking of thee,
And scanned all the scenes of my life,
And a bright spot, Nancy, to me,
Is the day you first were my wife. 

But last night I thought I was old,
When I viewed myself in this Pen.;
To-day as the scenes I unfold
I live all my youth o'er again. 

I can see away back in the years,
As far as a mortal can look,
And read all our past joys and fears,
Like words on a well printed book.

Don't you mind what father did say,
When you both were talking of me,
That you could have Hyrum the day
That he would come back o'er the sea,

Or remember that happy day,
When autumn flowers were all in bloom
Chatting carelessly we strayed away
Just inside of mother's big room.

And then, with our backs to the door, 
Our hands on the old mantle shelf,
I whispered you could leave me no mo
Till I had you all to myself. 

I can see now that scene of bliss,
When you in your bridal array,
Gave me fondly that sealing kiss,
Just Twenty years, Nancy, to-day.

Then when for my sake you did part
With all the dear friends of our youth,
And did join with me hand and heart
To build us a home in the south.

In scanning the crosses through life,
And our darlings laid in the cold grave,
'Twas then in the struggle and strife,
I found you so true and so brave.

But one scene I ever recall,
And truthfully say to you, dear wife,
That deed is the best act of all
To me, it is the gem of your life.

You may have done so in dread,
When you gave me Dear Annie's hand,
But with all your sorrows you said
You knew it was Father's command. 

I honor the vows I took then,
That I would be faithful and true,
And now I'm here in this Pen
For those vows to Annie and you.

I soon once again may be free,
But no prison walls can divide
That love, dear wife I have for thee,
Since the day you first were my bride. 

But there are many bright days in store,
And blessings awaiting for thee,
When prison and bondage no more
Will sever my dear wives from me.


A SLEIGH RIDE.
DEAR EXPONENT:
	I wonder if it would not interest you to get
a glimpse of this north country by going with
us, in the senses, for a sleigh ride this bright
winter's day. Come—try the elixir of the
keen fresh air and the accompaniment of the
merry jingle jingle of the bells.
	Here we go through the streets of the little


[Column 1]
town and it don't take many minutes to pass
the houses and enter the woods. The horses
are as exhilerated as we are, and they canter
on at a great pace, making the snow fly in our
faces; the desire to laugh is irresistible, and
peal after peal echoes down the road—now we
are passing through an avenue of trees of such 
great size and height, I'll not make you stare
by guessing even how many hundred feet high
some of them are; but how close and straight
they grow, like sentinels guarding the forests
from the ruthless hand of man; but, ah! they
are not strong enough for that, even in their
might, for see—here comes the wood-chopper
his ax over his shoulder, and his sharp eye
marking the highest and mightiest monarch
of the forest, on which to show his skill, and
here we are in sight of a "clarin" made by his
brawny arms; there is his little cabin with the
smoke curling gracefully up from the chimney,
into the pure clear air. How funny the
stumps look, capped with "Tam o' shanters" of
snow, some rakishly careering over on one side,
in a 'tipsey stuck up style, as though they were
badly cut up, or more properly speaking, cut
down. Here we are, in sight of the loveliest
green grove of pines, and such a pretty bit of
valley with the river winding through, and all
about us mountains; mountains of green, some
just silvered with "the beautiful," while in the
distance some o'ertopping all covered with the
pines which stand like sheeted ghosts; their
tips just catch the sun's rays, and are crowned
with the delicate roseate glow that artists
strive in vain to duplicate. Now we are near-
ing another patch of woods and only the rosy
clouds that hang above that mountain, hold
the glow of the sunshine.  How dim and silent
is this forest; no sign of life save that we bring; 
but, O, the picture! How beautiful. See, how
the undergrowth of young trees and shrubs
are covered with filmy veils of lace. Verily
Jack Frost thou art a dazzling genius; no lace 
webs that Royal Trosseaux can show, can com-
pare with the intricate, delicate patterns here.
Doesn't it look as if we might lift the frost
from that young pine, as easily as the veil from
the face of a blushing bride!
	Still we race on. Ah—here is indeed a de-
serted village; but not of the Goldsmith type.
The low log cabins stand with doors hospitably
open, yet no sign of  life, within or without.
Look at that queer sign, the letters formed of
quartz specimens, "Assay Office," is it? It
must have been some artistic chap who held
forth there. No ruddy glow comes forth from
the cavernous depths of yon black-smith
shop door, nor sound of music from the silent
anvil. O, look! a covey of partridges just
ahead; what pretty creatures, and so odd with
their bright top-knots.  Well, well; here are
the remnants of the once flourishing mining
town of Eagle—a corner grocery, a saloon
with hotel attachments, a cottage here and
there, that looks as though inhabited. Three
men with pipes in their mouths and hands in
their pockets, staring in a suprised way at
our appearance; several yelping dogs surround
us as we stop for an instant, and some forlorn
chickens are huddled under the edge of the
saloon porch, and a still forlorner woman
peering through the window, are the only life
to be seen.
	Crack the whip—and away we go, for the
snow is like glass and the road in fine condi-
tion. Here we are now, at the garden beds of
our valley, where they raise the finest straw-
berries in the world for size and flavor, and
"praties" which, while we deprecate ther size,
as poor old Mike said "they're maley Major,
ain't they maley, though?"
	Here we are at "Old Mikes" cabin, where he
was found some weeks back by some men, sick
and alone, too far gone for the care and help
they brought him to town for, poor old chap,
he was quite a character and had "foine
dhrames" as he once told us of visiting once

[Column 2]
more his native bantry. Let us hope his
'dhrames' are sweet where he quietly sleeps on
the hill-side under the deep snow. But while
I've been moralizing; we have entered a dense
forest again, and the sun shining through the
snow laden branches, dazzles our eyes with
the glitter of nature's diamonds. At length
we have reached the turning point—here is the
saw-mill; now, pray be careful Mr. Driver, and
don't upset us in that snow bank; now wait for
one moment and let us look about and take in
long inspirations of this delicious air, so 
freighted with balsamic odors and health;
think of spruce gum and be happy; look at the
great logs and see what lovely grains those cedar
planks show. Hurry on, for it won't do to let
the horses get cool now, so we must face the
wind and away on the homeward stretch.
Surely it is raining as we come out of the
depths of the timber, but no; we are only
passing through the clouds which are resting
in the little valley. The horses seem to scorn
"terra firma," and fairly fly, now they turn
toward home, one instant we are in among the
trees, where the light grows dim and then
dazzled with the lights on the snow. Tuck
the furs closer around you; how quickly we
came over that stretch of road past the "Gold-
en King Mill" and the long line of flume.
Through the last bit of woods and here is
"Sunset Peak" showing his snow-crowned
summit with the last rays of the setting sun
hanging like a halo round his hoary head.
Again the streets of the town and the warm
glow of the fire seen through the windows of
the cottage on "Gold Hill."
	Why, I hardly knew I was cold; yet the
thought of a warm fire is most agreeable and
such an appetite as I have brought back for
dinner; may good digestion wait on it, is all I
ask. Certainly our cheeks are rosier and our
spirits lighter for the ride. I hope it has been
a pleasant one for you.
	WOODINA.
	Murray, Idaho. Jan. 25, 1889.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
R. S., Y.  L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS.
ST. JOSEPH STAKE.
	The quarterly conference of the Relief So-
ciety of the St. Joseph Stake of Zion convened
at Thatcher, November 31st, 1888, President
Willmirth East presiding. On the stand, of
the Stake authorities, Prest. Christopher Lay-
ton and Counselors. Meeting opened by sing-
ing, "Once more we come before Thee, Lord."
Prayer offered by Patriarch Philemon Merrill.
Minutes of previous conference read and ap-
proved; also the Stake and local reports.
	After the presidents had reported their
various wards, Bro Merrill, by request, gave a
brief report of the St. David Society, there be-
ing no lady representative of that ward
present.
	Coun. Mary L. Ransome spoke a short time
on the duties of mothers in training their
children in the principles of the Gospel. "We
should be more diligent in teaching them to
attend the Primaries,” etc.
	Meeting adjourned till 2. p.m. Benediction
by Coun. Wm. Johnson.	
	Afternoon session: Opened with singing and
prayer. Prest. East said: “It is the manifesta-
tions of the power of God that has gathered
us from the nations of the earth, and it is left
with us to be subject to the priesthood of God
that is placed upon the earth to guide us; it is
only the faithful that will stand the tests, etc;
it is therefore necessary that we seek diligently
for the Spirit of God to lead us in the paths of
right and rectitude. I am proud to represent
the women of Zion, and pray that I may ever
be found faithful in the performance of my
duties.”
	The Stake and local officers were pre-
[Column 2]
sented before the conference and sustained.
Prest. C. Layton and Patriarch Merrill occu-
pied the remainder of the afternoon, instruc-
ting the sisters in their various duties; were
pleased to know that the poor were cared for;
wished the different branches of the Society to
lend all the aid they could in assisting the
Indian missionaries in their labors. Among
other remarks said: "We should be humble
and prayerful at all times, that we may wield
an influence for good over our husbands and
sons. We are left to ourselves, and in conse-
quence of this, many are flaunting in the pomp
and pride of the world; it is therefore necessary
that we seek diligently to keep the command-
ments of God, that we may gain an inheritance
in His kingdom.”		
	The presidents of the various branches were
instructed to keep on hand material for cloth-
ing the dead. 
	Conference adjourned for three months.
Benediction by Bishop John Taylor.
C. M. GUSTAFSON, Sec.

SAN JUAN STAKE.
	The Relief Society of the San Juan Stake 
held their conference at Bluff, Nov. 27th, 1888,
Prest. Jane M. Walton presiding. Choir sang,
“The Coming Day.” Prayer by Prest. Ham-
mond. Singing, "Devotional Hymn.”  A
goodly numbor of the brethren were present,
including Prest. F. A. Hammond and Coun-
selors, and the Bishops of the Mancos, Burn-
ham and Bluff Wards, Prest Moiselle M. Halls 
and Mary N. Jones, of the Stake Presidency
of the Y. L. M. I. A.; also Prest. Julia Butt,
Stake Prest. of the Primary.
	Prest. Walton hoped to be dictated by the
right spirit while she endeavored to address 
the conference. Considered it a great blessing
to have the privilege of holding Relief Society
conference. It made her heart feel warm to 
see the sisters; felt that the Spirit of God had
been with us during our conference so far.
Knew of a surety that every principle of the
Gospel was true; would rather die than deny the
truth.	
	Minutes of the former conference were then
read and accepted.
	Prest Abigal Stevens of the Burnham Ward,
said it gave her pleasure to report her Society.
The sisters felt well and united: they held one
testimony and two work meetings each month.
Desired to continue faithful and true to the
end.		
	Sister Neilson reported the Bluff Ward So-
ciety. Said the sisters had done as well as
could be expected, owing to their few num-
ber. "If we will listen to the teachings and 
instructions of those in authority we will be
blest; realized there was a great mission for the
sisters to perform.
	Prest. Walton then resigned her position as 
Prest of the Bluff Ward, as she had moved
from that place. Prest. F. A. Hammond
honorably released her, and them made a few 
needed explanations.	
	Coun. Moiselle Halls reported the Mancos,
Society; said the sisters felt more united than
at last conference; held testimony and work
meetings regularly; were storing up grain.
"The teachers are very diligent.” 
	Bishop Nielson felt to return Sister Walton 
a vote of thanks; knew she had done a good
work, and had been very kind to the sick.
The Society at Bluff was then organized as
follows: Kisten Nielson, President; Agnes
Allen and Eliza Redd, Counselors; Jennie Al- 
len, Secretary; Ordella Allen, Treasurer.
Prest. Hammond moved that Sister Kisten
Nielson be honorably released from  acting
First Counselor to the Stake President. Martha
Hammond was then sustained as First and
Josephine Wood as Second Counselor to Prest.
Jane Walton.
The general and local officers of the Relief


[Column 1]
Society were sustained. Coun. Martha Ham-
mond spoke a few minutes, giving good coun-
sel. 
Coun. Wm. Halls said his advice to the sis-
ters was to devote themselves to their office 
and calling. Prayed God to bless all who 
had been called to fill the different positions.
	Prest. Hammond felt to endorse all that had 
been said by the sisters. Prest Walton ad-
journed the conference. Choir sang, “Improve
the shining moments.” Prayer by Bishop Geo.
Halls.
SARAH J. ROGERSON, Stake Sec.

BANNOCK STAKE.
	Minutes of the Relief Society conference
held in Rexburg, Nov. 17, 1888, Prest Tem-
perance Hinckley presiding. Meeting opened 
by singing. Prayer by Coun. Rigby.
Singing. Roll was then called, and minutes 
of previous conference were read and approved.
 The Stake and local officers were presented
and unanimously sustained. Stake Prest.
Temperance Hinckley was pleased to meet the 
sisters of the Relief Society; would liked to 
have seen more present. Exhorted all to 
come to their meetings. “If you feel deter-
mined to come, you can, for ‘where there’s a
will there’s a way.’” Earnestly advised the 
mothers of Zion to be prayerful and humble.
“Attend to your secret prayers, for much good
is derived from secret prayer. If you are de-
pressed in your feelings, if you will kneel 
down and pray God will help you, and your
burden will seem light.” Advised the mothers 
to bring up their children in the fear and ad-
monition of God; too much cannot be said on 
this subject. “The teachers of the Relief So-
ciety should be very diligent, and seek for the 
Spirit of God to help them to perform their 
duties in a right and acceptable manner, for a 
great responsibility is resting on them. You 
should visit the sick, and see that the poor are 
cared for.” Asked the blessing of the Lord to 
be with us to aid us in the truth and all good 
works.
	Stake Coun. Sarah A. Gunnel bore a strong 
testimony to the truth of this work; made some 
good and timely remarks; spoke very encourag-
ingly to the sisters. “Although you have 
many things bearing upon you in body and 
spirit you should not give up; remember, ‘No
cross, no crown,’ and press forward with re-
newed diligence.”
	Stake Coun. Telitha J. Helm spoke with 
power; exhorted the mothers to inculcate good
principles in the bosoms of their children.
“You should teach them to be pure, virtuous 
and chaste, even as our Father in heaven and 
His son Jesus Christ are. Should we curse 
our enemies? No; we should pray for them; as 
Jesus said, when nailed to the cross, “Oh, God!
forgive them, for they know not what they
do.” We should pray for the missionaries 
who are laboring for the work of God, and 
those who are pleading for us in the halls
of Congress. We should live pure lives,
so that when we are called to lay down our 
bodies we can rise crowned heirs in the king-
dom of God.” Asked for the Spirit of God to
rest on all the branches of the Relief Society 
in Zion.
	Sister Sarah A. Barnes bore her testimony 
to the truth of this work. “If any one of 
you do not have a knowledge for yourselves 
that this is the true kingdom of God, take 
the Doctrine and Covenants, Book of Mormon 
and Bible and study them carefully, and it is 
so plain any one can have a knowledge, if they 
will seek with prayerful hearts. I feel that a 
large portion of the Spirit of God has been with 
us; we have heard many good things, and we 
should try to remember them and put them in 
practice. The counsel in regard to bringing 
up our children is good and from God.” Asked
God to bless us and help us to do right.
	Stake Coun. W. F. Rigby exhorted all to 
faithfulness in keeping the commandments of 
God. “We are living in a wicked world, for
many curse God, and it behooves you as 
mothers in Israel to be very prayerful, for un-
less you seek the Father you will not be able 
to keep your sons and daughters from the con-
taminating influences of the outside element.
I realize that a great responsibility rests on the
mothers of Zion; you each have a mission to
perform; if you can bring up your children to 
be honorable and to be saved in the kingdom 
of God, you have done a great work. Visit 
the homes of the sisters; encourage them, and 
invite them to come to meeting.” Gave many 
good and timely instructions. Asked God to 
bless the sisters of the Relief Society.
	Prest. T. E. Ricks addressed the conference;
expressed his approbation of the work of the 
Society. Said he was more than pleased with 
the remarks of the speakers; advised the sis-
ters to prepare in time for the next conference,
and be there, “and you that are here will feel,
when you go home, that you have been richly 
repaid for coming, for a large portion of the 
Spirit of God has been here. You should be 
strict in every word and act, so you can gain 
confidence in each other; cultivate a spirit that 
will bless and comfort your sisters; when any 
one is cast down cheer them up. I think we 
are as free from talking about our neighbors 
as any place I was ever in.” Gave many 
good instructions, which sank deep into the 
hearts of all present; also spoke of the good-
ness of the Lord unto us. Asked God to bless 
us in all we are called to do.
	Prest. T. Hinckley said she was well pleased 
with the meeting; thought we had been well
repaid for coming, “and I have been highly 
pleased with the good instructions we have 
received from Prest. Ricks and Coun. Rigby.
I thank them for their kind remembrance.
	Conference was then adjourned for three 
months.  Closed by singing, ‘'Glorious things
of thee are spoken.” Benediction by Telitha
J. Helm.
SARAH A. BARNES, Sec.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


A COMPARISON BETWEEN THE
CHARACTERS OF DESDE-
MONA AND PORTIA.

 BY ELSIE ADA-FAUST.

	In all his writings Shakespeare has probably
never presented  to us two characters which
would do more to earn for him the name of a
versatile writer than those of Desdemona and
Portia. So different, and yet so peculiar to
the positions which the writer makes them
occupy.
	In Portia we find the most intelligent and
intellectual woman that Shakespeare has
created. In Desdemona a creature fitted only
to grace a position of affection and dependence.
While in Portia we find tenderness and
womanly affection to a high degree, it is tem-
pered by her pride and reason. Desdemona's
reason is not great, and her pride is as noth-
ing, for Brabantio tells us:
	"In spite of nature, of years, of country,
credit, everything," she fell in love with what
she feared to look upon. To her love came as
a whirlwind, sweeping all former duties and
principles from her path; carrying her beyond
the love and obedience to her father, and lay-
ing in waste the condemnation of customs, the
sneers of enemies, the counsel of friends, every-
thing, that she might consecrate her thoughts
and life to the moon.
	To Portia love came only as a summer breeze,
fluttering softly her heartstrings; swaying her
thoughts to the rythmatic tune of hope; rip-
pling the surface of her calmer actions; sug-
gesting a treason to her father's wishes; and

[Column 2]

brushing aside a portion of her modesty to al-
low her to say:

"I pray you, tarry; pause a day or two
Before you hazard; for, in choosing wrong
I lose your company; therefore forebear awhile;
I here’s something tells me, but it is not love,
I would not lose you, and you know yourself
Hate counsels not in such a quality."

	She could make her love subservient to the
trial her father imposed upon the man who
was to win her. And, no doubt, although she
asks, "Is it not hard, Nerissa, that I cannot
choose one nor refuse none?" Had the hardship
been imposed upon her of accepting either the
Prince of Arragon or the Prince of Morroco,
she would have bowed submissively to her fate
with a sort of dignified indifference, and pro-
ceeded to be happy.
	How different Desdemona, who took her
marriage into her own willful hands with so
much disregard of her father's wishes, that he
must learn from a stranger of the event to
which he was so much opposed!
	In her only act of firmness, that of loving
Othello in spite of everything, she displays
her extreme weakness; she shows how plastic
is her nature, and how she is governed by her
strongest impulse.
	The one act usurps all the strength of
which her nature is capable, and in taking all,
appears like a column without a pedestal, the
greater it grows the weaker it becomes.
	All Portia's calm speculations about her
marriage, her witty criticism on the traits of
her many suitors; her hesitating to let Bassanoi
choose; while weaknesses in themselves, be-
tray a strength of character unknown to Des-
demona. They mean in her a determination
to be firm in her duty in spite of her inclina-
tion, to put aside all her own wishes, that she
might fulfill those of her father, and to ac-
cept the inevitable with all possible grace.
	As we cannot imagine Desdemona conceiv-
ing and carrying out the plan of the court
scene that Portia did, so it would be as hard to
think of Portia submitting to a jealous man's
frenzy enough to be quietly smothered with a
pillow.
	With Desdemona's narrowness of intellect
it would have been impossible for her to devise,
as did Portia, a plan to free her husband from
the debt to Antonia, nor can we contemplate
her preparing with a mixture of humor and
seriousness to carry out that scheme, and much
less think of her as successfully and bravely
performing her work so that all difficulties are
cleared away. Desdemona, when she says,

"So that, dear lords, if I be left behind, 
A month of peace, and he go to the war,
The rites for which I love him are bereft me,
And I a heavy interim shall support
By his dear absence. Let me go with him."

shows how hard it would have been to substi-
tute Portia's words of,

"O love, despatch all business, and be gone!"

How difficult for her to have tricked her hus-
band as did Portia; to have received that ring
with Bassanio's words,

	"Alas! it is a trifle;
I will not shame myself to give you this,"

as indifferently as did Portia. She very likely
would have sought in some timid way to at-
tract his attention and perhaps cried when she
found he treated a gift from her so lightly.
	And Portia, with her strength and independ-
ence begging to an unreasonable husband:
	"Kill me to-morrow, let me live to-night,"
would certainly be a strange picture. We can
paint her as being far more apt to calmly wave
him aside, while she deliberately went for
assistance, leaving him to digest a few bitter,
well chosen words.
	And as we have noted a difference in charac-


ter and of the events of their lives so far, so
we must take our leave of them in a different
way. Of Desdemona, dead through tamely
submitting to the wrath of a jealous being
whom she loved beyond everything, and to
screen whom she died with a lie on her lips.
As we leave this martyred creature, of whom
Emelia says,

“Moor, she was chaste; she loved thee, cruel Moor,”

we leave Portia surrounded by friends who
dearly love her, and by all that money can buy.
This creature of whom Jessica says:

“Why, if two gods should play some heavenly match,
And on the wager lay two earthly women,
And Portia one, there must be something else
Pawned with the other, for the poor, rude world hath not


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

IN MEMORIAM.

	DIED, in this city, Dec. 26th, 1888, of paralysis, Ann
Corbett, beloved wife of Daniel Corbett. "Give her of
the fruits of her hands, and let her own works praise her
in the gates."

RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	We, the sisters of the Relief Society, unanimously
adopt the following Resolutions:
	Whereas, it has pleased our Heavenly Father to call
from our midst by the hand of death our beloved sister and
friend, therefore,
	Resolved, That we do recognize in the death of our sis-
ter, Ann Corbett, the loss of an able and efficient treasurer
of the Second Ward Relief Society.
	Resolved, that we esteem her past labors and devotion

[Column 2]

Culbertson, who successfully removed the same, and
afforded considerable relief.
	In the year 1883 her husband died. After the accident
she was unable to visit the sick as formerly, but she would
give advice and medicines; still she continued to practice
as midwife until she died, which occurred September 3rd
1888.
	At her funeral, which was held at her oldest son's resi-
dence, a large number of people assembled, and the ex-
pressions of sorrow were plainly visible on every hand.
Every one felt they had lost a mother and a friend; one who
always had a kind word for them and good advice for their
children.
	She was the mother of ten children, eight of whom are
still living. She remained true to the cause she espoused
on the broad Atlantic, and died full of faith and hope.
		WM, N. PURRINGTON.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.
VOL. 17.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, FEBRUARY 15, 1889.	No. 18.


[For the EXPONENT.]

THE CRUMBS THAT FELL.

Close side by side two neighbors lived,
	And both were poor indeed;
'Twere hard to say, were truth all told,
	Which lived in greater need;
If one had work, his neighbor'd none,
	And each had mouths to feed.

Each one a shaggy pony owned,
	And each a lean ribbed cow,
Which puzzled those of greater means;
	Why do they keep them? how?
For what the brutes eat would feed them,
	I'd sell  them off, I vow!

But oh, the poor folks really prized
	Those creatures rough and dumb,
And pictured them in nobler guise,
	For every heart hath some
Love of possession and kind care,
	Though troubles with it come.

The summer passed with burning drouth,
	The price of hay rose high;
"Good times," said they with hay to sell,
	"Hard times," said those who buy,
And watched their stacks slow sinking down
	As weeks went creeping by.

Now, whether 'twas for company,
	Or whether 'cause 'twas warm,
On the south side of Robert's stack,
	Just after every storm,
Sure as the day came Jamie's stock,
	As though it were no harm.

They leaned across the slender fence
	Till it near broke away,
And calmly licked and calmly chewed
	The precious, high priced hay;
And when well filled they sought repose
	On leaves that round them lay.

Oft Robert's wife all vexed looked out,
	And scarce knew what to do;
"The man's to blame, yet he is poor—
	'So is my husband, too;
The golden creed—'Do unto all
	As you’d have them to you.'

"I think the man has clean forgot,
	Yet, it's as much to me
As 'tis to him. If he should come
	Now to his door and see,
I think he'd take them both away,
	I'd not so careless be."

Then once in wrath she raised aloft
	Her broom, and swift drew near,
To put to flight the hungry things,
	That showed no signs of fear;
And all at once a lesson old
	Came to her strong and clear.

The tale of Lazarus who asked
	The crumbs alone that lay
Beneath the rich man's table, and
	She turned and went away;
"They only get the leaves that fall,"
	An angel heard her say.

So comes to every soul in life
	The choice of right or wrong,
So taught to ev'ry Christian heart
	The tale through ages long;
Though humble be our power to point
	Where we shall yet belong.

		AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.
January 21, 1889.


[Column 1]

UTAH'S LADY DELEGATE.

	Mrs. Emily S. Richards, who left here on
the 13th of January to attend the Annual
Convention of the National Woman Suffrage
Association in Washington, as a Delegate
from Utah, arrived at the Capitol previous to
the opening of the Convention, and was al-
lowed time for an address, notwithstanding the
previously arranged programme. Mrs. Rich-
ards was received with considerable applause,
and made a decidedly favorable impression.
We give her speech in full, as published in a
letter to the Deseret News from the special cor-
respondent in Washington.—ED

	"'Tis better to have voted and been disfran-
chised, than never to have voted at all."
	This is a very prosaic paraphrase of a very
poetical sentiment, but it is true, and behind it
lies a great political wrong.
	I am a native born citizen of the United
States, and for fifteen years enjoyed all the
rights and privileges of the elective franchise.
I voted for every kind of elective officer in the
Territory of Utah, from delegate in Congress
to school trustee. I never once sold my vote,
nor was I influenced in casting it by either
fear or favor. I never attempted to repeat; no
man for whom I ever cast a ballot was ever
charged with malfeasance in office, or with any
other violation of his official oath. Yet to-day
I am disfranchised and on the same political
plane as you, my sisters, and the rest of our
sex in this "land of liberty."
	I think I may safely challenge any male
voter of the United States to show a better
use of the rights of American citizenship. I
do make that challenge for myself, and on
behalf of the thousands of women in Utah
whom I represent, and whose political record
is above reproach. And on their behalf, with
all possible respect to the distinguished gentle-
men who sit in Congressional majesty on Capi-
tol Hill, I protest against the wresting from us,
by the right of might, of that franchise which
we had never abused, and which was our vested
right. 
	In 1870 women were given the elective fran-
chise in Utah.
	Subsequently an act was passed by the legis-
lature striking the word male from the law
prescribing qualifications for holding office, so
that women might be equally eligible with men
to any office in the Territory. This act never
became a law because it was disapproved by
the Governor, who is appointed without the
consent of the people, and his vote is absolute,
even when opposed to the unanimous vote of
the entire legislature. And so the will of the
people was defeated by this unrepublican one
man political power.
	Just as one who has loved and lost is always
deemed the best authority upon affairs of the
heart, so I venture to claim a particular knowl-
edge of the use, the power of the franchise in
woman's hands. And the experience which I
gained with the women of Utah should not be
concealed from this Convention; it belongs to
you; it is essentially a part of woman's history
in this great Republic; it is the best practical
illustration of the problem whiah has yet been
given, and it is likely to obtain that pre-emin-
ence for years to come, because in all the seven-
teen years during which the women of Utah
exercised their right to vote, they held the
balance of power between the two parties, and

[Column 2]

no man was elected to office without their ap-
proval.
	During all these years we voted without
disturbance and without injury to the manners,
morals or domestic comfort of the men. We
were able to determine whom we wished to
sustain by our votes, and no one had the right,
the power, and, I will say for our husbands,
brothers and fathers, the wish or desire to pre-
vent, intimidate, or coerce us in the least de-
gree. We were citizens of the United States,
armed with that all-potent yet peaceful politi-
cal weapon, the ballot, and we challenge the
world to show where, in a single instance, we
wielded it wrongfully. And let me say right
here, that we have not had to contend against
the male opposition common in other places,
for the majority of men in Utah believe that
woman was designed by the Creator to be
man's companion in all things, whether in the
Church or in the State, in time and in eternity,
and to share dominion with him over all created
things.
	While we enjoyed the franchise we took part
in the primaries and nominating Conventions,
acted as delegates, and did our share of the
political business.
	Two years ago Congress abrogated the right
of woman's suffrage in Utah, and thousands of
us were summarily deprived of our political
power. We had committed no offense against
the sacred franchise and had violated no law
of our opponents, and, therefore, in their esti-
mation we were not worthy to vote at all. The
same logic would have disfranchised men as
well as women, but their ballots were pre-
served.
	It has been intimated that the women of
Utah were compelled to vote as required by
the men. This is not true. No coercion was
ever attempted, and, if it had been, the effort
to accomplish it would have failed because,
under the law, we had a perfectly secret ballot,
which put it beyond all human power to find
out how any person voted unless the elector
chose to tell.
	It is also an error to suppose that women
voters of Utah used the ballot to uphold or
perpetuate any system or practice in opposition
to the laws, or the will, or common custom of
this country. No such question was ever voted
on at the polls in that Territory. Votes of
men and women alike were merely cast for
local officers, and we chose to elect such men
as we believed would act honestly, fairly and
for the public good, in the interests of tem-
perance, order, morality and economy, and not
for private ends or personal greed. That was
"the head of our offending."
	The deprivation which we have sustained is
not more cruel to us than to you. The con-
tinuance of our political power meant that you
could have pointed to our work as a proof that
the general cause was worthy of success. But
Congress, in the almost infinite wisdom of
legislative man, decreed to dig up the new
budding tree before it could bear fruit and
give seed. It was not within the male purpose
that this little acorn, planted nineteen years
ago, should grow into the sturdy oak.
	But while we have lost our franchise in Utah,
we still retain our property rights. Unmarried
women have all the legal rights of men, so far
as the acquisition and disposition of property
is concerned, whether real or personal. Mar-
ried women have entire control of all the


[Column 1]
property they held at the time of marriage
and can acquire, hold and sell, or otherwise
dispose of real and personal property after
marriage without the consent of their hus-
bands, but the husband must have the consent
of the wife. In addition to this they have the
right of dower, by which they inherit one-third
of the husband's estate at his death. The law
of inheritance is the same from the wife to the
husband as from the husband to the wife; each
inherits from the other under similar condi-
tions. 
	For many years the women of Utah have
been organized into Relief Societies and Mu-
tual  Improvement Associations. Their sym-
pathies have always been with the Woman
Suffrage movement. After the visit to Utah,
last summer, of Mrs. Colby and Mrs. Saxon,
officers of this Association, who lectured on
“Woman Suffrage; the International Council
of Women and the relation of woman’s ballot
to all reforms,” a Woman’s Suffrage Associa-
tion was organized as an auxiliary to the Na-
tional. Its members number about two hun-
dred and are rapidly increasing. In this move-
ment we did not receive the aid or sanction of
our vice-president, because she did not think
woman suffrage good for Utah. The organiza-
tion was effected by the chairman of the execu-
tive committee. It is complete in every par-
ticular, and the by-laws declare the object of
the association to be “to work for the enfran-
chisement of women without regard to party,
sect, or creed, and to take such steps from time
to time as shall seem to be wise and expedient
for the political advancement of the women
of Utah.”  The Association has sent two dele-
gates to this convention. The very large ma-
jority of people in Utah are in favor of woman
suffrage. As an evidence of this, we secured
8,393 signatures to the national enrollment in
three weeks, 3000 of them being the names of 
men, and we have but just begun our work. 
	All trades, professions and occupations in
Utah are open to women. We have editors,
doctors—who have graduated in the best col-
leges of the country—lawyers, school teachers,
artists, poets, musicians, lecturers, merchants,
clerks, cashiers, photographers, printers, book-
binders, telegraph and telephone operators, silk
workers, weavers, factory hands and artisans
in every kind of work that women are able to 
do, as well as in those domestic labors which
some narrow-minded people think comprise
the sphere of woman. We have no “superflu-
ous women” in the Territory, and none who
need remain without employment; it is often
impossible to hire women for domestic ser-
vice. 
Women, in the male estimation, are not as
fit as men to perform jury service, because it is
said they would be governed by sentiment. If
the premises be true, the argument is still false.
The sentiment which would most often actuate
women is pity. With men it is too often bus-
iness, avarice or social cowardice. Women
might favor the poor and friendless. Men cer-
tainly do show illegal leniency to the rich and 
powerful. 
	We are told that suffrage means turmoil of 
mind, which women should avoid. I do not
concede that this is necessarily so, but I do say
that, if it were so, my sentiment would be that 
of the heroic Roman who said: “Better a dis-
turbed liberty than a quiet slavery.”
	For one, in a general sense, I object to the
cruel withholding of suffrage from women, in
a land consecrated by blood to universal liber-
ty. In a special sense I object to the arbitrary
edict by which thousands of intelligent voters
were deprived of a sacred right, simply be-
cause they were women. 
	The mightiest human problem vexing the
world to-day is the relation of the rich and the
poor—the profit eaters and the profit makers.
Man alone will never solve it. There are
[Column 2]
cases of wounds and illness where woman’s
nursing is absolutely necessary. This is a 
desperate case of social disorder, where woman
must minister with all her skill and tender-
ness to bring the patient through the crisis.
The cry which we now offer up is the cry of
the ages. It is the protest of the few against
the many, the argument of thought against
bigotry. To-day the world is correcting the
active wrongs of last century, and is making
recompense to the demon of intolerance, by be-
ing blind and deaf to the oppressed of to-day.
The wonder which men feel now that their an-
cestors could have been so intolerant will be
felt a generation hence by their sons regarding
these same men.
	The “federation of the world” will not be
accomplished while the most advanced nation
on earth is but half redeemed. But the cause
of woman’s enfranchisement is gaining irresist-
ible momentum. Its triumph must soon come.
When it is victorious, but not till then, will
might cease to her arbiter of right. 
	When a cause is irrevocably lost, it is then 
too late for words. To discuss inevitable sor-
row is only to give intensity to grief. But the
women of Utah do not regard their own fran-
chise as being forever lost. We hope and ex-
pect to see this great struggle terminate speedi-
ly and triumphantly for the cause of woman,
and to see political liberty enjoyed without re-
gard to sex. When that sublime hour comes
it will mark the beginning of the end of tyran-
ny, oppression and misrule. God speed the 
day.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

WOMAN'S SUFFRAGE MEETING.

	There was an interesting meeting of the
Woman's Suffrage Association of Utah held in
the Social Hall at 2 p.m., Saturday, Feb. 9,
1888, First Vice-President Mrs. Lydia D. Alder
presiding. Prayer was offered by Mrs. Zina
D. H. Young.
	Mrs. Alder's opening address: "The hand
that rocks the cradle is the hand that rules the
world." This sentiment was written long ago;
but it is just as true to-day as it was then. It
has from time immemorial been conceded that
the home (that dearest spot on earth), is the
woman's kingdom. And many women there
are, even to-day, who desire no other sphere.
History shows that adown all the ages the
mother who rocks the cradle and carefully
raises her sons, has through them ruled the
world. But shall not our daughters be taught
also, and have just as many privileges as our
sons? Most assuredly. We can look around
us and see many of our sisters who have to
struggle with the hard realities of life. Some
of them are widows, some of them chose never
to marry. They own property, either much
or little, on which they must pay taxes for the
support of the government, both local and
general. Yet they must never have a voice in
the choice of that government's servants.
Woman suffrage is a live question before all
thinking minds and many nations to-day.
Think for a moment of the prospect confront-
ing our own government welcoming as it does
the down-trodden and oppressed of every clime
to its shores. This emigration brings many
who are utterly ignorant of the first true
principles of government. Scheming politi-
cians can buy this vote, also the average negro
vote, for a very small sum of money. This
has conduced to much of the corruption exist-
ing to-day. You can see that when woman
gains the ballot, and we regain our ballot, her
vote, given intelligently and wisely, will offset
the vote of ignorance and fraud. God speed
the day.
	Prof. James E. Talmage following Mrs.
Alder in a short address, said:
	The feelings that animate my mind on this
occasion are peculiar indeed. This is owing

[Column 2]

in some degree doubtlessly to the nature of the
audience, but more especially to the wide in-
terest, and the far-reaching importance of the
topic of the day. I say "far-reaching import-
ance," intentionally, and with no idle meaning
attached to the words; for it is certain that the
problem to which this honorable association is
seeking a proper solution, is of interest to the 
human race at large.
	My humble opinions upon the matter are of
a simple and commonplace kind; but not, I
trust, insincere or immature on that account.
The conclusions at which I have arrived in the
consideration of the subject are based upon no
comparison of legal political advantages or
disadvantages likely to result from conferring
the rights of suffrage upon woman; but upon
the justice of her claims to these rights as wit-
nessed by the word of the Almighty. Man, in
his studied wisdom, has learned this great
lesson of Nature—that the Creator has fitted
every object of His care to the purpose of its
being. Is it then to be thought that woman is
less completely endowed with the capacities of 
her calling than is man for his? It has been
often said that woman, in the sphere allotted
to her existence, is as perfect as man. I would
say in place of this, that in her sphere woman
is as much the superior of man as does man
excel in his proper powers. Nature has been
as liberal in endowing woman—Jehovah's
daughter—as in blessing man—His son.
	The objections that have been raised to the
enfranchisement of women in this land of repub-
lican freedom are mostly based  upon the fear
of the incapacity of women for the offices and
stern duties of citizenship. If these fears
appeared to have  solid foundation, I, too,
would be loth to witness such a change. It
has been wisely said here to-day, that the home
is woman's kingdom; and I feel sincerely that
the true woman, reigning in her home as a
queen, will prove too deeply interested in the
affiars of that sacred domain to desert the
throne in any endeavor for political preferment.
We may repose implicit conficence in womanly
nature; and never need we fear feminine as-
pirations toward positions unfitted for woman's
capacity.
	That the giving of suffrage to woman would
change much of the state at present existiug in
the politics of the day, I have no doubt, but
for such a change I most sincerely hope. My
greatest regret is that woman has to plead and
beg for that which rightly belongs to her. She
is affected by the laws equally with man, and it
is just that her voice should be as willingly
heard as his in the choice of the law-makers
and officials to execute the laws.
	God speed the day when woman shall have
her heaven born rights.
	Mrs. M. Y. Dougall then read an open letter
from the Association to the ladies of America,
and Mrs. Nellie Little read, very effectively,
a synopsis of the speech of Judge Jere Wilson,
which was listened to with marked attention.
	The eloquent speech of Delegate Mrs. Emily
S. Richards, which was given before the Na-
tional Woman's Suffrage Association Conven-
tion at Washington, D. C., was read by Mrs.
Charlotte I Kirby.
	Mr. James T. Cobb, being called upon, re-
sponded in a very acceptable speech on woman
suffrage.
	The meeting then adjourned.
		CORNELIA H. CLAYTON, Secretary.


Feburary 7th, Senator Blair, of the com-
mittee on Woman suffrage, reported back
favorably the joint resolution proposing a con-
stitutional amendment to prohibit the denial
or abridgement of the right to vote by the
United States or any state on account of sex.
It was placed on the calendar; Sen. Cockrell
said a minority report would be made here-
after.—Ex.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


A PRAYER.

TO THOSE WHO MOURN.

God of our fathers, comfort the weary-hearted,
	Heal up the bitter pain
Of those who mourn their departed;

Whose sad eyes turn to the vacant chair,
	To the place unfilled at the table,
And calm Thou their wild despair.

Turn Thou their gaze utno Thee, 
	From the sad earth where sleeps their departed;
Help them, oh, help them to see

Through the vail to the dawn of that Day,
	Unto the City, the Golden—
Where earth-woes have all passed away.

And, oh, unto Thee do I pray,
	For those who can point to no grave stone
As cause of their deep darkened way,

Illumine the gloom with Thy Spirit, 
	Give peace where now agonies reign,
And redeem from the woes we inherit.

God of Thy children below,
	Father, Redeemer and King,
Help us Thy hand e'er to know;

And in all of the perils of life
	To cling to Thine Anchor of love,
To guide through the billows of strife.

Unto the haven of rest—
	Even that City—the Golden—
The home of the Ransomed and blest.
		HOPE.


FRIENDSHIP.

TO MRS. AURELIA S. ROGERS.

There are many friends in springtime,
	When the air is pure and sweet;
When the roses blossom round you,
	And the lilies at your feet.
How they seek to win your favor,
	Gain your approbative smile,
And with words and deeds of kindness,
	Your fond, loving heart beguile.

There are many friends in summer,
	When prosperity abounds;
When kind fortune smiles upon you,
	And the air with praise resounds;
How you gaze at them and wonder
	Who'll be firm and true to trust,
Who will stand the test of friendship,
	Who will say thy cause is just.

When the stormy clouds of autumn
	In the horizon appear,
Friends false and faint and fickle,
	One by one will disappear;
Let them go, false friends,
	For such friends are worse than none,
In an hour when most they're needed:
	There you look, and lo' they're gone.

But the friend that is true in winter,
	When the frosty, chilling blast
Wraps its icy mantle round you,
	That is friendship that will last.
Such a friend is like a jewel,
	Inlaid in pearl and cased in gold;
Time, nor dust, nor heat will change them
	And like diamonds, they'll not mould.

And though earthly friends may wander
	Far away in time of need,
There is one who rules in heaven,
	He is a friend, a friend indeed.
Ferns may fade and roses wither,
	Leaves decay and mosses die;
But if you will retain his favor,
	You will to victory soar on high.

		LUCY A. CLARK.
Farmington.


[Column 1]

WOMAN AND HER SPHERE.

	Woman and her sphere is a theme that has
agitated the minds of thinking men and women
of all ages, but because of the darkness that
has covered the earth, there is a great diversity
of opinion regarding this matter—the proper
sphere of woman—and we find that in this,
as well as other great subjects which effect the
welfare and happiness of the human race,
great mistakes have been made; when, if man-
kind had only been willing to accept the sim-
ple laws of God, which said in the beginning,
"She shall be a helpmate for man," there
would have been no need for controversy on
the subject, for woman would have stood where
the great Father designed she should—at the
head of her family, side by side with man, his
equal and companion, neither aspiring to su-
premacy on the one hand, nor abjectly feeling
herself an inferior or a slave. The question
may be asked, "Is she inferior to man in physi-
cal strength?" She may be in some respects,
while in others her more refined and delicate
organization is peculiarly adapted to the part
she was designed to play in the creations of
God, and among whatever people the refining
influence of woman is most felt, there will be
found the most worthy and noble men; for is
she not a wife and mother? and a truly virtu-
ous woman is a crown to her husband; her
children rise up and call her blessed, and she
is neither degraded as an inferior or regarded
as a plaything; and were it not that the world
have wandered far from God, woman would
stand in the dignity and purity of her woman-
hood, and desire to fill no other position than
that which an alwise Creator designed for her.
And man instead of seeking to lower or de-
grade her, would cherish and respect her as
the brightest jewel and greatest blessing a bene-
ficent Father had bestowed upon him.
	Nor have instances been wanting in which
women have played an important part in the
history of nations. We will pass by the many
instances recorded in profane history in which
women have distinguished themselves in states-
manship and sometimes as warriors, when they
sometimes saved a state and led armies to
victory. Although many such instances might
be enumerated, we pass them by and turn to
the Scriptures of eternal truth, where we read
of Miriam, who was a prophetess; of Deborah,
who judged and led the armies of Israel; of
Esther who took her life in her hands to make
intercession for her people, saying, "I will go
unto the king, and if I perish I perish;" but
more particularly in the New Testament do we
find an Elizabeth who received the ministra-
tion of angels; of Mary, who was counted
worthy to became the mother of the Holy
One; of Anna, a holy woman, a prophetess,
who departed not from the temple, but served
God day and night. It was a woman who
oftenest ministered to the wants of the Son of
God, who believed in and followed Him; but
it was not a woman who betrayed Him.
Women were last at the cross and first at the
sepulchre, and it was to a woman that He first
revealed Himself after His resurrection, and
honored by sending with a message to His dis-
ciples with the joyfal tidings that He had risen
from the dead; and oh! how Mary's bleeding
heart must have rejoiced. And if woman was
the first, by reason of her transgression of the
law to bring the penalty of death into the
world, she was also the first to receive the glad
news that Christ had arisen from the dead, if
not to understand that through His death and
sufferings the original sin of our first parents
had been atoned for, and through His resur-
rection He had obtained the keys of death and
hell, and opened the door whereby we could,
by obedience to the laws of the Gospel, once
more enter the presence of the Father.
	Thus we find that woman has often been

[Column 2]

honored of God; and woe! be to man if he
cannot give a good account of his dealing with
her, who often remains faithful to him through
evil as well as good report, and will minister
to his wants when all others have forsaken him;
and when prostrated with pain, sickness or
sorrow, it is woman who helps him bear his
burdens; it is her soothing hand that lulls the
pain of his aching head, wipes the chilling
dews of death from his brow, and weeps over
him when all others have departed.
	But woman shines brightest in her sphere as
a mother, and to her was the first promise
given of a future Messiah; that notwithstand-
ing she should bring forth children in pain
and sorrow, it was through her seed that the
head of the old serpent should receive a deadly
bruise; and how anxiously women of olden
times desired children; perhaps each one may
have secretly cherished the hope in her heart,
that she may have been the one honored above
all others and the mother of the promised
Savior.
	We believe that in these latter days woman
must still play an important part, and it will
be found that she is still as willing to make
sacrifice for the sake of establishing what she
believes to be the principles of truth and
righteousness in the earth, as she was in olden
times to follow the scorned and persecuted
Savior to the foot of the cross; for it is a well-
known fact that when times of trial and perse-
cution have arisen women have always been
as willing to suffer as men, and even to lay
down their lives for conscience sake.
	Let the women of the latter days lift up
their heads and rejoice, for a brighter day for
woman is near at hand. Let them prepare
themselves, for the old days of thraldom, that
dwarfed their intellect and marred their use-
fulness are rapidly passing away. Let women
be true to themselves, and their power and in-
fluence will soon be felt in the land. Take a
stand against wickedness, in whatever shape it
may appear, for a great and glorious day for
woman is near at hand.
		PHEBE C. YOUNG.


EXTRACTS. 

	Mary Maynard, in her Washington letter to
the Woman's News, has some very witty para-
graphs; we quote the following in regard to
Harrison's Cabinet. —ED.
	"The Woman's Suffrage Convention has
come to be as certain a happening as the meet-
ing of Congress, and some of the people of
Washington have begun to mark the year by
the appearance of Miss Anthony and her fol-
lowers.  As the housekeeper unfolds her even-
ing paper she exclaims, 'Hellow, Miss Anthony
is here and Spring is coming.' And Washing-
ton has grown to have great respect for Miss
Anthony and her brave army.  It hopes it
will come long and come annually forever.
They fight an honest battle, they have won
great victories, and the future is full of promise.
Mrs. Lillie Devereux Blake, one of their 
breeziest speekers, said, after satirizing mascu-
line political methods:  "There is a great curi-
osity as to who will be the members of Gen.
Harrison's Cabinet.  I'd like to present a few
names.' For Secretary of State she named
Elizabeth Cady Stanton; for Secretary of the
Treasury, Mrs. May Wright Sewall; for Secre-
tary of War, our great fighting captain, Susan
B. Anthony; for Secretary of the Navy, Mrs.
Zerelda G. Wallace; for Secretary of the Inte-
rior, 'noble Clara Barton,' and for Attorney
General, Mrs. Laura De Force Gordon, of
California.  She maintained that a Cabinet
composed of that material would give Gen.
Harrison such advice and assistance that his
administration would be unparalled and above
criticism."
