SALT LAKE CITY,	APRIL 15, 1889.


[Column 1]

FIRST GENERAL CONFERENCE OF THE
RELIEF SOCIETY.

	(The first general conference of the Relief So-
ciety was held in the Salt Lake Assembly Hall
Saturday evening April 6th, 1889)  Besides
about fifteen representatives of the various Stakes
the following ladies were upon the stand. Prest-
Zina D. H. Young; Counselors Jane S. Richards
and Bathsheba W. Smith; M. I. Horne, Sarah M.
Kimball, Emmeline B. Wells, Romania B. Pratt,
and Presendia L. Kimball. (President Zina D. 
H. Young presiding;) congregation sang
"O, My Father Thou that dwellest," prayer
by Presinda L. Kimball; singing "Praise to the 
man who communed with Jehovah."
	Prest. Zina Young said: "My sisters, upon this
occasion I hope you will give me your faith and
prayers. As I look upon the sisters around me, I
realize we miss our beloved Sister Eliza, who on all
such occasions was with us. In the providence of
the Lord shewas taken from us. She mingles
with those above; we revere her memory. As this
is our first meeting of this kind I hope we can
unite our hearts as one that the Lord will bless us
in so coming together. May everything be agree-
able that takes place here this evening."
	Romania B. Pratt, the assistant secretary, then
read the totals of the general statistical reports,
and the names of the Stake presidents of the
Relief Society. On account of existing circum-
stances Prest. Zina Young had written what she
wished to say, and it having been approved by 
Prest. Woodruff, Mrs, Wells then read the paper.
PRESIDENT ZINA D. H. YOUNG.
	The Relief Society, under whose auspices we
have met together here to-night, to receive in-
struction, and advice in our several duties, was
first organized nearly half a century ago, by the
Prophet Joseph Smith; after the pattern of the
Holy Priesthood, and under its direction, to dis-
pense temporal blessings to the poor and needy:
and to give encouragement to the weak, and re-
strain the erring ones, and for the better develop-
ment, and exercise of woman's sympathies, and
charities, that she might have opportunity to at-
tain spiritual strength, and power for the accom-
plishment of greater good in the work of the re-
demption of the human family.
	We should be diligent in all the duties of life,
as mothers and wives, and as members ot the
Church, remembering to attend the meetings of
the Saints, including our Fast-day meetings, and
pay willing offerings to the poor, and our dona-
tions to the Temple; and with whatever belongs,
especially to the mothers, observe the law of
Tithing that the blessing promised may follow,
and that the little children may be taught this
important principle by example and educated in the
path of duty, which is the path of safety. Teach

[Second 2]

them to keep the "Word of Wisdom," and to be
temperate in all things; avoiding all evil habits
and pernicious practises, that they may have
strong bodies, and be entitled to the promise that
follows obedience to this commandment.
	Let us be careful to speak with wisdom before
our little ones, avoiding fault-finding, and slang
phrases, and cultivate the higher attributes of
our nature, that will tend to elevate, refine and
purify the heart, and make the home the centre of
attraction, where the spirit of love, peace and
unity will dwell, and that sweet charity that
thinketh no evil will ever abide; that we might
always be possessed of that heavenly principle
embodied in the song "Nay speak no ill."
	We should take the utmost pains to teach the
children of Zion to be honest, virtuous, upright
and punctual in all their duties; also to be indus-
trious and keep the Sabbath day holy; not to be
rigid or too severe, but as far as possible make all
duties pleasurable. Mothers should never speak
a word detrimental to the father's best interest
before the children, for they are close observers.
Sow good seeds in their young and tender minds,
and always prefer principle to policy, thus you will
lay up treasures in heaven.
	Our sisters who have been called to preside in
a Stake capacity, over several branches of the
Relief Society, should plead for wisdom from
above to direct their efforts, that the greatest
possible good may be accomplished. They
should visit each branch society in the Stake at
least once a year, and oftener if circumstances
will permit. Where sisters can do so, it would be
desirable and we think profitable, to visit each
other's organizations and become acquainted; it
will tend to union and harmony, promote confi-
dence, and strengthen the chords that bind us to-
gether, for there is more difference in our manner
of speech, than in the motives of our hearts.
	If the President of the society cannot be pre-
sent to preside, it is her duty to see that one of
her counselors attends to take charge of the meet-
ing, for the success of an organization depends
very much upon the efficiency and promptitude of
its officers. When a vote is required one of the
Counselors should make the motion, and after it
has received a second, the President shonld pre-
sent the motion, and call for the vote. President
Joseph Smith's instructions to the Relief Society
were, that the meetings should be conducted in a
parliamentary manner.
	President Young, in great wisdom we believe,
established the custom of holding quarterly con-
ferences, for the more perfect unity of interest in
the Relief Society, and President Woodruff ad-
vises us to continue in this respect as we have
been doing in the past. We should also keep
correct minutes of our meetings, that we may 
have a complete record, and then with very little
trouble exact reports can be made to the General
Conference.
	I trust the sisters will not neglect or grow
weary in the duty required of them in storing
up wheat. If it needs changing to preserve it,
the Presidency advise, in the case of loaning it,
the giving of written security, by the Bishop,
for the full amount, and something added to
allow for shrinkage, that the loan may be a safe
business transaction, and that all interested may
be satisfied.
	When Relief Society Conferences are to be
held, the Presidency of the Stake and the Bishops
should be informed of the time, and invited to
attend the meetings, that harmony and union
may exist, and strength and encouragement be
given to the diligent sisters, and that others see-
ing their good works, might be induced to come
to meeting, and enjoy the same good spirit and
feeling, that pervades the hearts of those who
meet often one with another, to be instructed in
their duties, and to gain spiritual strength and
wisdom for the various ylcissitudies of life.

[Column 3]

	It is the privilege of the sisters, who are faith-
ful in the discharge of their duties, and have re-
ceived their endowments and blessings in the house
of the Lord, to administer to their sisters, and
to the little ones, in times of sickness, in meek-
ness and humility, ever being careful to ask in
the name of Jesus, and to give God the glory.
	Do not find fault with the Providences of God,
it will not better our condition and only make
our burdens heavier to bear; but rather be
patient through all the trials of life, and speak
not against the Lord's anointed.
	It is our duty to be self-sustaining, and to foster
and encourage home industries, and home manu-
factures, and patronize those institutions that have
been established for the best interests of Zion;—
the co-operative stores; and be careful not to use
the means we have been blessed with, to build up
those, who are seeking to destroy and overthrow
the work of God, and to plant discord, strife and
dissension in our midst.
	The silk culture is one of the branches of home
industry, that demands our earnest attention. I
have never yet doubted, but, that it would event-
ually become a fruitful source of revenue to this
country. In the first sermon that I heard Brigham
Young preach in this valley, he remarked "there
is silk in these elements," and I have heard a
number of silk weavers say, "Utah silk was the
best fibred silk, they had ever seen." Sisters do
not be discouraged in any of the duties we have
undertaken to perform; I am aware we have many
responsibilities resting upon us, and also realize,
that we all have great need to be diligent and
prayerful. The Deseret Hospital is one of the insti-
tutions that needs to be sustained, in order that we
may have a place where the sick and the afflicted
among our people, may go when it is necessary,
and receive the medical and surgical care, nursing
and attention suited to their condition; and where
they can have the Elders of the Church to ad-
minister in the sacred ordinances of the holy
priesthood. We should not forget, or neglect our
duty to this charitable institution; keep up your
monthly donations for the Society, and continue
to be members of the Association, and remember
"he that giveth to the poor lendeth to the Lord
and He will repay."
	We should use our influence, too for the estab-
lishment and maintenance of educational institu-
tions, in which our children and young people,
may be taught the true principles of the everlast-
ing Gospel; and discourage any tendency to out-
side influences, calculated to lead them into doubt
or unbelief. In all things possible, let us en-
deavor to cultivate our home talent and stimulate
our sisters to read, and to write, that they may be
intelligent wives and mothers; read good books,
especially the Bible, Book of Mormon and
Doctrine and Covenants. The EXPONENT, is at
present, the only paper we have where the voice
of woman is sent forth especially, to instruct us in
our duties, and encourage the brave and deter-
mined ones to persevere in the midst of difficulties,
and also to give expression to the views and
opinions of the women of Zion on subjects of vital
importance. And we recommend that the sisters
subscribe for it, and read it, that they may be
posted upon the condition, growth and progress of
the various organizations of the women in Zion,
and elsewhere; and become familiar with the
efforts being put forth, for the uplifting, and
further advancement of all womankind; and of
the changes made from ttme to time in the direc-
tion of progress.
	Sisters, let us be as one grand phalanx and
stand for the right; let us be humble and firm,
honor truth, and be valiant in sustaining it, not
presuming, but trust-worthy in all things. Have
we not shouldered the cross of the world's scorn,
and braved its anathema with more valor, than
the warrior the cannon's fury? Death with him,
and then all is over, but we endure to live


[Column 1]

﻿eternally. Purity, love and integrity, let these
virtues live in our hearts; then the sunshine of a
loving Father's smile will be ours. Do not doubt
the goodness of God or the truth of the work in
which we are engaged; but learn obedience, it is
better than sacrifice; follow our file leaders and
fear not.
	May we do a work acceptable to our Heavenly
Father, and that will meet the approbation of our
brethren, who are called upon to endure for the
truth’s sake, what the Ancients did, when seeking
to establish righteous principles to benefit
humanity. May we as women of Zion, ever know
and honor our true position, and continue to grow
in grace, and abound in good works, until He
whose right it is to reign shall come.
	Counselor Jane S. Richards then said: "I trust
my beloved sisters, I may have an interest in your
faith and prayers for they avail much. I feel
many times that it is the greatest pleasure we
have—the privilege of praying—for no one can
rob us of this. The instructions from Sister Young
are such as we ought to adopt. I for one feel
like accepting them. We miss Sister Snow, and
although she encouraged us saying "Don't get
faint, go ahead," Sister Young now fills her place
and gives the same advice.
	I was thinking how befitting the words of
Sister Young in regard to the Hospital were. If
we do not have to go there, there are others with-
out homes coming a long distance, who find care
and attention in this place for the sick. The
greater willingness of the sisters, daily, betokens
an increase in our charity. The charity we want
is that which is manifested in kindness and for-
bearance towards one another. We frequently
do little things which try each other, and it is
then we should exercise our charity. Especially
should we be forgiving towards the young;
they have a long life before them, and if
in spending a few moments frequently with them
we probably can teach them to avoid mistakes we
have made, let us do so. Let us seek for the
spirit that characterized our Savior; in Him was
perfect kindness.
	I desire to say a word in regard to the EXPO-
NENT. In it, sisters, we can learn a great deal in
regard to our daily duties. It has been edited
with great patience by Sister Wells. Do we ac-
knowledge it as we ought ? I believe we have
been a little careless in this regard." The
speaker invoked the blessing of God upon the
congregation.
	President Zina Young stated she was anxious
to hear from those representatives who had come
a great distance.
	President Anna Ivins, president of Relief
Society of St. George Stake represented the or-
ganization of the same, stating that although the
country was hard, they possessed the necessaries
of life if not the luxuries. "The attendance in the
twenty four wards of the Stake is small in com-
parison with yours in this section. It takes a
month for me to visit the wards."
	Counselor Martha Hammond of San Juan Stake
stated that in attending conference in San Juan
they had to travel over a hundred miles, neverthe-
less the sisters were true and faithful in attending.
The speaker realized that if we were blessed as
we expected, the settlements would soon grow
closer, thus easing matters and enabling the First
Presidency of the R. S., to visit them.
	President Hinckely from Bannock Stake said they
had fifteen ward branches. "The attendance with
some is regular with others not so. We need very
much the visits of the sisters of the presidency.
The speaker expressed great joy in receiving such
good instructions as were imparted in the meeting.
	Emery Stake was represented by President
Annie U. Larsen who stated that she in company
with several others had recently visited the seven
branches in that Stake and found them in fairly
good order. They had grain on hand. There

[Column 2]

could not be found any destitute among their
portion of the church. In several instances the
Relief Society had assisted missionaries.
	Counselor B. W. Smith expressed great joy in
meeting with the sisters as they were assembled.
"When the Relief Society was first organized
as I well remember, there were but few, but those
few had a good spirit. I can hardly realize it to
have grown so much. We should set an example
before every one worthy to be followed, being
punctual, diligent and charitable. We should
cultivate the Spirit of the Lord and thus avoid be-
ing led astray."
	The General and Stake authorities of the
Relief Society were then presented to be sustained
by vote by Sister Sarah Kimball, the vote was
unanimous.
	The roll of stakes was called and responses
made as follows: Bannock, Bear Lake, Box
Elder, Davis, Emery, Juab, Kanab, Millard,
Oneida, Salt Lake, St. George, San Pete, Sum-
mit, Weber, Morgan, (presidents.) Beaver,
Parowan, Panguitch, San Juan, (counselors.)
	(Sister M. I. Home then spoke, saying she con-
sidered it a great privilege to meet together,where
by the blessing of God correct principles could
be advanced and we learn to become more per-
fect. She realized that Sister Eliza's mantle had
fallen on Sister Zina just as Elijah's desended
upon Elisha. Sister Eliza had been with us long,
and we felt her absence, and there was another
associated with us formerly whom we missed,
Mother Whitney, who had so often in our meet-
ings sung in the gift of tongues in the language
spoken in the Garden of Eden and blest us all
and lifted us for the time above the cares of the
world, and we cherish their memory in our hearts,
their loving words and wise counsel can never be
forgotten. The reward for our labors is not to
be obtained in this life but awaits us at the gates
of Heaven. The speaker bore her testimony to
the fact that, in the face of the seemingly most
insurmountable difficulties it was the duty of the
sisters to press on in their good and noble work.
These difficulties appear when our minds are
clouded with unstable thoughts, but if we but
think, we can see the munificence of God's bless-
ings on every hand. The Lord is our friend and
helper and in trusting in Him are we aided.
	The Kingdom of God is a kingdom of order.
Joseph Smith's instructions in regard to the con-
ducting of our meetings were, that parliamentary
rules should be observed. Always transact your
business first. Then bear your testimonies. The
Secretary should be very particular in taking
minutes, not necessarily long, but complete. The
Secretary and Treasurer just before a conference
should meet and compare notes, making their re-
ports perfect.
	President Young invoked the blessings of God
upon the congregation, after which, the meeting
adjourned for six months. Congregation sang on
page 107 "Lord dismiss us with Thy blessing."
Benediction pronounced by Sister Harriet Snow.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

EDITORIAL NOTES.

	The minutes of the Woman Suffrage Meeting
held in the Salt Lake Assembly Hall, on Thurs-
day April 11th, came too late for this issue, but
will appear in our next.
	The General Conference of the Church held in
the Tabernacle in this City April 6th, 1889 was
probably the largest Conference ever held since
the organization of the Church. It was an event
of very great importance and will never be for-
gotten by those who were present especially the
meeting on Sunday afternoon when the various
quorums of the priesthood seated according to
their order, and all the members of the Church
voted for the reorganization of the First Presi-

[Column 2]

dency and to sustain  all other officers in their
several positions and callings. The First Presi-
dency as sustained by the Conference are Wilford
Woodruff President, George Q. Cannon, First
and Joseph F. Smith, Second Counselors.
	The Annual meeting of the 8th Ward Primary
Association was held March 19th in the meet-
ing house, Mrs. Jane Cowan presiding. Visitors
present were Mrs. Louie Felt President of the
Central Board of the Primary, Mrs. Lillie
Freeze her First Counselor, and Mrs. Ellen
C. Clawson and Lydia Ann Wells of the Stake
Board, also Mrs. E. B. Wells and Elizabeth
Howard. The house was nearly filled and the
exercises were of a very edifying character besides
being well rendered. A class of little ones re-
eited  "The Floral Alphabet," which was an interest-
ing feature of the occasion. The children all per-
formed their parts in a creditable manner. Re-
marks calculated to inspire faith and stimulate to
good works were made by Mrs. E. B. Fletcher and
Mrs. C. Palmer, and also by Bishop Sheets and
Elder John Needham. A tempting collation was
spread for all; the children had their picnic in an
adjoining room where they made merry to their
hearts content in feasting.
	Seven of Drs. Shipp students in obstetrics
graduated March 30th, and received their Di-
plomas from the examining committee, and they
were also blessed and set apart for their work as
practitioners among the people, at the Historian's
Office preparatory to leaving for their homes.
Their names and places of residence are as fol-
lows: Miss Emma Hillstead, S. L. City, Miss Mary
Sawyer, American Fork, Utah Co., Mrs. Parthenia
A. Holt, Hoytsville, Summit Co., Mrs. Christina
Peterson, Scipio, Millard Co., Mrs. Mary Sorenson, 
Teton Bingham. Co., Idaho Mrs. Louisa Gibbs,
Paradise, Cache Co., Mrs. Libbie Bybee, Hooper-
ville, Weber Co. There was an interesting pro-
gramme for the evening's entertainment in which
recitations were given, songs were sung, instru-
mental music on the piano, and speaking by
several of the brethren and sisters who were
present. Dr. Milford B. Shipp, President
Zina D. H. Young, Dr. Maggie C. Shipp, Mrs. E.
B. Wells, President A. M. Cannon, Mrs. Mary A.
Freeze and others. Congratulations were ex-
pressed and good advice given to the fresh grad-
uates. Ice cream and other delicate refreshments
were served and the evening passed off pleasantly.
	The friends of Mr. and Mrs. George Alder of
this city offered their congratulations on the
twenty-fifth anniversary of their marriage on Mon
day evening April 8th, at their residence on South
Temple Street at which time the happy couple
celebrated their Silver wedding. It was also the
forty-fifth anniversary of the marriage of Mrs.
Alder's father and mother, who were present, and
received their share of the congratulations and
good wishes of the company of relatives and dear
friends, assembled to do honor on the happy
occasion. The bride looked charming and as
happy as though it was really the wedding day and
the sons and daughters were kept busy enter-
taining the guests. The parlors were brilliantly
lighted and handsomely decorated, and everything
betokened joy and festivity. Miss Margie Dwyer
rendered in her happy stlye some instumentalt
music on the piano, and Messrs. Alder, Barrat
and Dwyer, sang one of their favorite pieces.
Miss Dean Caine also favored the company with
one of her choice selections in song. Refresh-
ments in rich abundance, with ice cream and
wedding cake were tastefully served during the
evening and all went merry as a marriage bell.
The usual supply of wedding gifts from particular
friends were received, and there seemed nothing
wanting to add to the happiness of either host or
hostess or their sssembled guests. May their
future life be serene and happy, and their ex-
periences ever unite them still more closely to-
gether and their children rise up and call them
blessed.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


REFLECTIONS IN FASTDAY MEETING.

	My dear Sisters, especially those whose whole
souls are centered in the interests, and redemp-
tion of Zion; I am perfectly satisfied in my
mind that there are many at the present time, that
are like minded unto myself, whose burden of
their prayer is the redemption of Zion, it seems to
be the theme and topic of our Tabernacle and
Ward meetings, and it is a generally acknowledged
belief, that, that event is not far distant. But it is
very possible that even among the saints, there
may be some to whom this prayer for Zion's re-
demption may become a matter of habit, like unto
the professing Christians of to-day; praying daily
for God's Kingdom to come, without ever once
seriously asking themselves the question, do I un-
derstand what I am asking for, and am I doing my
part towards bringing it about? Well if this is the
situation that we many of us are found in to-day,
it is high time that we were aroused out of our slum-
ber, lest that day should come upon us, as has been
foretold, like a thief in the night; therefore with
a view to this end, and an eye single to the glory
of God, (though I feel myself one of the least in
His Kingdom.) I feel led as a matter of duty to
write the reflections of my mind on this great and
important subject, namely the redemption of
Zion.
	It has for some time been the most attracting
sound that met my ear, so when the opening
prayer was offered at the fastday meeting, March
7th, 1889, in which a petition was offered, for the
time to be hastened when Zion should become a
power in the midst of the earth, it caused many re-
flections to arise in my mind. It seemed to
cleave unto my mind with such force that I could
not shake it off, and the more I looked into it, the
more real it seemed to be, that small as the chosen
few of the members of God's Kingdom set up in
these last days appear to be, compared to the
nations and kingdoms of the whole earth, it is
nevertheless true, that Zion will yet become a
power in the midst of the earth; hence, the
question arose in my mind, but how long will it
take to bring that about under present existing
circumstances? and especially with the spirit of
Babylon increasing so fast in our midst; for it
seems as if instead of Zion purifying herself for
that glorious time, the saints are daily manifest-
ing an increased desire for the things of the world
and the pleasures and vanities thereof, instead of
seeking after the things of the Kingdom of God.
	Such were the reflections of my mind, as
testimony upon testimony was added, more or
less bearing on that theme or subject, until a
brother arose and spoke in tongues, and I felt at
the commencement of his testimony, that there
was a mighty power of the spirit of God in the
place, which seized my whole frame, and just as
quick as lightning, the veil was withdrawn from
my eyes, and as the tongue was acknowledged
though uninterpreted, to be on the redemption of
Zion, I saw it right close at hand just as soon as
the saints would wake up to a sense of their situ-
ation, as in the light and sight of God, and turn
round and forsake the ways of the world, and do
His will and keep His laws and commandments
with all their heart; just so sure was he waiting
ready to stretch forth his mighty arm to deliver
them from their enemies; and also with attending
the Relief Society meeting in the afternoon, and
having the privilege of listening to the wise
counsel and instructions of Sister Higbee from
Salt Lake City, who so plainly and powerfully laid
before us, the follies and abominable ways that the
daughters of Zion had fallen into, which in the
sight of God, have been a great burden on my
mind ever since I came to the land of Zion.
	This caused me to reflect, and to say to myself,
would to God that I could do anything toward
bringing about a better state of things? and the
answer to the question came to me in this manner,

[Column 2]

I will ask all my sisters that are earnestly plead-
ing for and waiting for the redemption of Zion, to
oin with me in pleading with God our Eternal
Father that he may so inspire the hearts of His
servants, that have the leadership of His Church
and Kingdom, with such a measure of His spirit
that they may be led to see and realize the pride
and vanity that exists in His Church, in the light
that He sees it, and that they may be led to do as
is spoken of in the word of God, to one of the
Ancient Prophets, namely, "to lift up their
voices, and cry aloud and spare not, and show
Israel or Zion her transgressions, and His people
their sins. And I feel as sure as I live, that just
as soon as we as a people are led to see ourselves
in the light of truth and the spirit of God enlight-
ening us, and turn round and do His will, and
walk in His ways, will that be fulfilled, spoken of
in 102 Psalm and 13 verse, namely, "Thon shalt
arise and have mercy upon Zion, for the time to
favor her, yea, the set time is come." So as I
had suffered myself to be held back from testify-
ing of the light that was given unto me, I felt
prompted to write the feelings of my mind, in
hopes that it may do some good towards the up-
building and rolling forth of the work of God;
realizing that quenching the workings of His
spirit, gives Satan more power over us for the
future. I would not have ventured to dare to take
this upon me, feeling my littleness and insignifi-
cance in the Kingdom of God, but was encour-
aged by remembering the instance found in the
1st chapter of St. John, where Andrew after hav-
ing found the Saviour, and believing that he was
indeed the Christ, was anxious after his brother
Simon Peter, and it states, "he sought him and
brought him to Jesus," and in that act he took one
that became the most mighty and valiant in His
service. So in hopes that in like manner, though
this come out of weakness, it may be received by
some of the same spirit and power as the Apostle
Peter, that it may be carried forth to the honor
and glory of God; and that we my dear sisters,
may be led to cease not our supplications unto
God our Eternal Father, and keep not silence be-
fore Him, until He makes Zion a praise and a
power in the midst of the earth.
	Your humble sister in the cause of truth,
		M. A. C.


[Column 1]

R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS.

UTAH STAKE.
	The semi-annual conference of the Y. L. M.
I. A., was held in the Provo meeting house, at
2 and 7 p.m., with President Zina Lyons pre-
siding. The afternoon was occupied in reading
the semi-annual report of the associations, and
hearing reports from the various wards show-
ing the majority in a flourishing condition.
	Territoral President Elmira S. Taylor felt
proud of the progress made, and interest
manifested in the association, and instructed
the presidents in regard to the programs.
	Sister Annie K. Smoot, spoke of her early
experience in the Church, and the circum-
stances that caused her to join; also of the evil
resulting from bad associations.
	In the evening, after a few opening remarks
by Counselor Grace Smith, Stake president,
Zina Lyons instructed the congregation in re-
gard to sorrounding circumstances; advising
them to be examplary in all their actious.
Thought they could find more pleasure in the
performance of their religout duties than any
other way.
	Bishop H. H. Cluff thought the young ladies
were of Holy lineage and needed to watch and
pray in order to be kept in the right path that
they might gain the glory promised to the
faithful, which would cause them to sacrifice
earthly things which could not be understood
in any other way, and knew of no pleasure as

[Column 2]

satisfactory as that derived through its work-
ings.
	Brother D. John spoke of the noble mission
of woman, and the future happiness in store
for the faithful daughters of Zion; and con-
sequences resulting from evil associations,
	Presidhnt E. S. Taylor advised all to have
pure thoughts, and desiers to lead lives of
honor. Many had come among us for what
they call a refining purpose, instituting schools
of learning in our midst, and proving they
are not followers of the meek and lowly Jesus.
They are as restless as the tides of the ocean
because of the pleasure they are seeking which
is not as lasting and enjoyable as that experi-
enced through our most holy religion. Desired
the young ladies to verse themselves on the
scriptures in order to have a weapon that they
might defend themselves, God being their
helper. Counseled them in regard to choosing
a companion; not allowing themselves to give
their affections until they know of God what
was right; and to select one that would lead
them onward and upward in God’s kingdom.
Closed by bearing a firm testimony to the
truth of the work.
	An unusual portion of the Holy Spirit was
enjoyed during our conference, and the interest
ef the young people is increasing in the work
of Improvement.
		ZINA LYONS, President.
		EVLYN BILLINGS, Secretary.

UINTAH STAKE.
	The conference of the Relief Society of
this Stake of Zion, convened in the Ashly
Ward meetinghouse, March 3, 1889, President
Sarah Pope presiding. Siuging "Come all ye
Saints that dwell on earth," prayer by Brother
J. Hatch. Singing "Praise ye the Lord."
Minutes of previous conference were read and
approved. The reports of the different Socie-
ties were then read.
	President Sarah Pope made a few opening
remarks; asked an interest in the faith and
prayers of those present. Spoke of their visit
through the Stake; said there were some tried
with riches and some with poverty; spoke of
the need of charity, for there are those among
us that cannot help themselves; said if we done
the best we could, we would not be perfect;
we should try and overcome evil as much as
possible. Prayed the Lord to bless us through
this conference.
	Sister A. K. Bartlette asked an interest in
the faith and prayers of the people; bore a
faithful testimony to the truth of the Gospel.
Spoke on the resurrection of our Savior and
the mission placed on woman to perform.
Thought all should be very careful how they
partake of the sacred emblem of the Lord's
Supper; gave the sisters some good instructions
to which all listened with intense interest.
	Brother J. Hatch made a few very appro-
priate remarks which were listened to very
attentively.
	Conference adjourned until 2 p.tn. Singing
"Let those who would be Saints indeed,"
benediction by Brother I. J. Clark.
	Afternoon session. Singing "Afflicted Saints
to Christ draw near," prayer by S. R. Bennion.
Singing "Mortals awake with angels join."
Sacrament was administered by the Ashley
Ward Priesthood.
	Brother B. S. Collett then arose to address
the congregation; desired an interest in the
faith and prayers of the Saints. "We have
met to-day in the capacity of a Relief Society
conference. We find that after Joseph Smith
had organized the Church, he felt as though
there were something ladking, and therefore
organized the Relief Society." Gave the
sisters of the different wards some good in
structions in regard to their duties. He had
ever taken an interest in the young and rising
generation, and gave some good counsel to


[Column 1]

the same. He had received a great testimony
of the truth of the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints, and gave much more
advice, occupying a great portion of the
afternoon.
	President Bennion made a few closing re-
marks, which were very interesting to all.
Conference adjourned until the second Monday
in September. Singing. Benediction by
President Sarah Pope.
		ADDIE LONGHURST, Secretary.

BOX ELDER STAKE.
     The quarterly conference of the Relief So-
ciety of Box Elder Stake convened in the
tabernacle on March 12th, at 10 o'clock a. m.
	President Harriet Snow presiding: after the
opening exercises President Harriet Snow said:
"Sister Zina Young sent her love to all, but
could not be present with us, Sister Richards
is expected with the first train." Aunt Zina
"prayed that we may have a Pentecost. If we
exercise faith for the speakers, that the Lord
may speak to us through them, we may be en-
couraged and fed."
	Statistical and financial reports from 15
different branches were read by Secretary Mary
E. Snow. Treasurer's report read by Sister
Fannie Graehl.
	Sister Richards and Sister Brown from
Ogden arrived.
	President Hubbard from Willard: "I re-
joice in the society of the saints, in the prin-
ciples of the holy gospel, we know our depend-
ence on the Lord, let us be true to each other,
true to our religion and God."
	President Perry from Three Mile Creek:
"we live in a great day, if there is anything we
love more than our Father in heaven, it be-
comes our God; and He has said: "Thou
shalt have no other God." Let us strive to set
a good example and be willing to make sacri-
fices for our religion's sake."
	President Jane Richards: "I love to meet
with my sisters, I regret I did not hear your re-
ports. This is a great work, we learn many
things, although they may seem small, we need
the spirit of God, we are nothing without it.
I think the one who speaks with the most
humility, charity and love is the best speaker.
How blest and favored we are of the Lord; our
enemies are in the dark; many will yet see
their error, that time will come when we will
feel thankful that we passed patiently through
persecution. I have been 49 years in this
church but I never saw the time that I dared
doubt the principles of the Gospel." Spoke of
the death of her first child, how President
Brigham Young taught her to acknowledge
the hand of the Lord and give up her child in
faith.
	President Mary Jansen, Third Ward: "trials
are blessing in disguise we can better compre-
hend it after, than at the time we pass through
them.  I rejoice when I see old sisters still
faithful and not tired."
	Adjourned till 2 o'clock. Singing: "How
will the saints rejoice to tell," benediction by
Counselor Olivia Widerborg.
	Afternoon session. Singing: "May we know
the joyful sound," prayer by Counselor
Susannah T. Booth. Singing: "We are not
ashamed to own our God," roll called. Con-
densed report read by the secretary.
	Emilia D. Madsen felt that even in our
sympathies and in doing good, we can improve,
we must be thoughtful and considerate. I
consider the teachers position the most delicate,
but in blessing others, they themselves are
blest.
	Susannah Facer: "It is necessary for us to
be intelligent and filled with light, there are
many things we need to learn and become
wiser."
	Sister Hattie Brown: "I feel to be one with

[Column 2]

my sisters, I have no doubt in this work, never
had. It is a grand blessing to be organized as
a Relief Society. We all have to be tried but
not always in the same manner, therefore we
must have charity, we cannot live too faithful
too prayerful, the Lord is merciful, He knows
the desire of our hearts, I wish to be honest in
all my acts."
	Sister Harding: "I know this is the work
of the Lord, we should live so we might merit
the approbation of the Lord."
	President Harriet Snow: "The more spiritual
food we get, the more capable we are of receiv-
ing it. We never should allow ourselves to
harbor feelings against any one. Let us live
humble and prayerful, that we may rejoice in
the principles of our religion."
	President Jane Richards: Spoke of the
Hospital, it is hard to get along, they need
means and encouragement, told some of the
reasons why it is not in a flourishing condi-
tion. Explained why it is necessary for us to
organize the Suffrage Association. "How little
we know, I like to get that spiritual light, that
I may always be prompted correctly."
	Counselor Adolph Madsen: "I hope the
members do not expect too much of the officers
but exercise charity, remember they are called,
they have not sought an office themselves, if I
am a bishop, should I feel I am a little better
than the members? it would be wrong, I
should feel I am a servant to my brothers and
sisters, I presume you feel the same in your
offices, judging from the remarks I have heard.
	Conference adjourned for three months.
Singing: "Oh, my Father thou that dwellest."
Benediction by Bishop Hansen.
		EMILIA D. MADSEN, Cor. Secretary.

SALT LAKE STAKE.
	Minutes of the quarterly conference of the
Y. L. M. I. A. held in the Assembly Hall
March 15th, 1889, Mrs. Mary A. Freeze pre-
siding. Opened by singing "Who are these
arrayed in white," prayer by Mrs. E. Howard,
singing "When shall we meet Thee." Minutes
of morning session of previous conference read
and approved. Written reports were read from
the 3-4-6-7-8-10-11-14-15-17 wards.
	Mrs. Freeze was pleased with the reports,
especially the increase of testimonies, would
like to have more manuscript papers edited and
as far as possible have them original. "We all
have come to gain a blessing, we shall not be
left without light, the Holy Ghost is the
greatest instructor, we are in possession of this,
we have had this gift sealed upon us and by
united faith we will receive blessings. The
foundation is to know this work is true, but it
is necessary for all to work."
	President Zina D. H. Young: "Obedience is
better than sacrifice" "and I arise this morning
to exercise the Holy Ghost and receive the
blessings following this act. The spirit of our
Heavenly Father is intelligence, we should be
in possession of it at all times, use it in all our
home duties.
	"It is to-day with us not yesterday nor to-
morrow, but to do our duty to-day. By over-
coming our selfishness we will have pleasant
feelings toward each other." Gave some ex-
cellent instruction on the nature of our con-
versation with each other, how our influence is
felt in future generations even beyond the veil.
"There is a power in closing our eyes during
prayer, also the response at the close should be
general. Prayed we might be humble and
faithful."
	Reports were read from the 19, Farmers, Mill
Creek, South Cottonwood, South Jordan, Herri-
man, Taylorsville, Draper wards.
	Mrs. Howard: asked all with faithful
hearts to ask of our Heavenly Father His
spirit, "by united prayer we will receive the
blessing. Many are looking for something new.
We do not yet live as we should that which

[Column 3]

we have received." Urged the practice of
punctuality. "It is the practical part of our
religion I desire to speak of. Good behavior in
houses of worship should be observed, when
a speaker arises to speak, think we are being
instructed by the spirit of the Lord, cease
whispering, remain in your seats till services
are closed. Politeness is another thing we
should practice, the pleasure it brings in re-
ceiving polite attention from each other." Had
thought of having printed the constitution of
the United States and Declaration of Inde-
pendence and placed where our young ladies
could study them, and understand our political
situation, be prepared to be wives that can
stand side by side with our husbands at all
times. Learn to be grateful for the blessings
you are in possession of, read good books, study
that which will be for your good, overcome
frivolity and vanity." Prayed that the spirit
of the Lord would be with us.
	Dr. Maggie Shipp: "if we come for com-
fort and blessings, and are united in our faith
and prayers, the spirit will inspire such things
to be said as are for our good. Our souls
need food, and feel strengthened after attend-
ing these meetings." Testified of the manifesti-
tations in the Manti Temple. "Our greatest
sorrow should be if we have grieved the spirit
of the Lord. Live so we can listen to the still
small voice and follow its whisperings." Spoke of
the duties of a wife and preparing for the same,
prayed our influence might be felt for good.
	Mrs. M. Y. Dougal desired what she said
to be influenced by the spirit of the Lord.
Young ladies should do their work themselves
and serve the Lord in their youth. Spoke of
the influence we wield at home and in our as-
sociations with each other. "Do not devote so
much time to novel reading it destroys the
memory and is ruinous. The object of our as-
sociations is to teach us the principles of our
religion. Learn to be polite and polished."
Prayed we might realize our positions as
daughters of Zion.
	Adjourned till 2 o'clock; singing "Consider
the lilies. Benediction by Mary P. Young.
     Afternoon session. Opened by singing.
Prayer by Dr. R. B. Pratt, singing. Roll
called showing representatives from 20 associa-
tions. Minutes of afternoon session of previous
conference read and accepted.
	President Freeze said: "she desired the as-
sistance of Sisters Jane Freeze and Della
Eardley to aid in the work of visiting Associa-
tions. They were unanimously accepted by
the conference.
	Counselor Mary P. Young said: "It does my
heart good to see the members present; I be-
lieve there is a general waking up of the young
in all their meetings, which is very encourag-
ing. If we do not improve it is because we
turn a heedless ear to the many good teach-
ings we receive. We have good books and
good counsel with us continually and the time
will come when every thing we learn will serve
us." She closed by bearing a strong testimony,
of her knowledge that this is the true Church
of Christ.
	Mrs. Maria Holt said she desired to live
to see the Latter-day Saints grow and increase;
to see them always strive to do good. Said she
could see with the eye of faith that these young
ladies would be more mighty than their mothers
had been; and they should cultivate faith for
much is expected of them. The glorious spirit
of God makes us at all times happy. Con-
cluded her remarks by saying: "My desire is
that we may watch our footsteps well and pre-
pare our minds for the greater things that are
to come. We must be humble, faithful and
true unto the cause of truth, I pray God to
bless us that the earth may be better for our
living."
	Mrs. Jane Freeze desired the mother
to visit the associations and see wha


their daughters were doing; "they will become
more interested for such visits; teach them to
respond when called upon to take part in the
programme, and in this way they will become
more edified."
	Miss Edith Samson thought everything was
made easier by the Spirit of God: "and if the
young will only listen to the promptings of
God's spriit they will not go astray."
	Mrs. Clara S. Clarlyle said that through her
inability to attend meetings her mind had be-
come spiritually dwarfed: "we should not give
up to enjoyment at the expense of our meet-
ings but should strive to have the spirit of the
Holy Ghost with us at all times for which
spirit we should pray and live for always."
	Dr. R. B. Pratt said she was pleased to
bear her testimony to the truth of the Gospel:
"every young lady can say she believes this to
be the work of God, on a basis that is firm and
reasonable and by such a course a true knowl-
edge will come to her, and by such a course
the spirit of God does not become grieved;
we cannot understand the things of God ex-
cept by His spirit.
	Bishop Thorn said he remembered when he
was not favorably impressed with the idea of
women preaching but his mind had changed
and felt the work they had done was import-
ant. Bore testimony to the truth of the re-
marks he heard in the meeting. Prayed that
the blessings of God would forward the cause
of the work we are engaged in: "many great
events are transpiring and greater things are
yet to come. I can comprehend the work as
it is now rolling on. We will have many trials
but all will yet be well."
	Brother W. B. Dougal had observed the
good results of this work in the past. He
observed a great dearth of testimony bearing
on the part of the young and knew that the
only sure foundation was to obtain a testimony
for ourselves, which enlightens the mind for
the teachings of the servants of God: "These
teachings without the true spirit are sometimes
considered tyrannical by the young. A knowl-
edge of the truth would also reduce in number
the union of our young people with disbe-
lievers and should receive much consideration."
	President A. M. Cannon said the remarks
he had heard in this meeting were good and
would aid our cause. "That Mormon creed
'mind your own business' speaks volumes and
should receive consideration." He referred to
the subject of marriage saying "man could not
exist in a perfect state alone for man without
the woman is not in the Lord." Urged upon
the congregation the necessity of obtaining a
testimony, for nothing could dwarf a man's
efforts so effectually as an infidel wife. "God
has called us to be subject to His divine law, if
we do so we will be rewarded.  So may God
glorify and make us immortal if we do His
holy will."
	President Cannon, Bishop Thorn, and
Brothers Dougal and Curtis then set apart
Sisters Mary P. Young as first counselor to
Mary A. Freeze president of these organiza-
tions of this Stake: Sisters Maggie C. Shipp,
Edith Samson, Maria Holt, Clara S. Carlyle
and Jane Freeze as assistants; Elizabeth Smith
Secretary and Romania B. Pratt as Treasurer.
	Conference adjourned for three months,
singing, "Guide us O Thou great Jehovah."
Prayer by President A. M. Cannon.
		E. SMITH, Secretary.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


THE UNION.
	The corresponding secretary of the Ameri-
can Woman Suffrage Association has taken
a vote of the members of the Association, by
mail, on the new constitution proposed for the
National American Woman Suffrage Associa-
tion, and the vote is favorable, by a large
majority. This practically concludes the


Union. The next meeting will be held at
Washington under the name of the National-
American W. S. A., and the call for it will be
signed by the general officers of both associa- 
tions. Until the next meeting, the present
officers of both associations will hold over.
	Woman’s Journal.	H. B. B.


A PLEASANT GATHERING.
	On the 28th day of March, the sisters of
Pleasant Green Relief Society wended their
way with baskets filled with the bounties of
earth,to spend a few sociable hours with one of
their co-laborers, Sister Nancy Deardon, Second
Counselor in the Society, who has been afflicted
for the last year and a half so that she could
not leave her home; and our good sisters think-
ing their presence would cheer and buoy her
up all came out enmasse for a few hours; and all
cast in their mite and presented her with a
handsome easy chair. Elder Samuel G.
Spencer composed a few verses for her in be-
half of the sisters, underneath them were en-
rolled the names of the members of the Society
these were put in a handsome frame and were
read during the entertainment. The sisters
also gave her a small sum of money. We had
a pleasant time and all felt well repaid and we
left Sister Nancy feeling that she was not en-
tirely forgotten.
			ALVIRA L. HIRST, President,
			FANNY H. JENKINS, Secretary.


OBITUARIES.
	It is with feelings of sadness we announce the death of
Sister Sarah Holmes, who passed peacefully from mortality
at her residence in St.Charles Idaho, February 26th, 1889.
	She had been connected with the Church for many years,
and was familiar with the trying scenes which so many of
the Saints have endured. She has for a number of years
resided in St. Charles, and since 1883 has filled the position
of Counselor to the President of the Primary Associations
of Bear Lake Stake, which she has done nobly and well.
	Notwithstanding she was advanced in years she was
never known to shirk any duty relative to her calling.
She was a peaceful unoffending woman, a faithful Saint,
and her precepts and examples will long be remembered.

Upright and honorable, chaste and refined,
Frugal and wise, thou wert good to mankind.
Brave thou hast been in defence of the right,
Seeking continually wisdom and light.
Friend of the motherless, who needed thy care
Teaching them always of sin to beware;
Trying to teach Zion's children to stand
True to each principle God should command.	
Striving to shield our dear children from sin.
By pointing the way where the victory they'd win.
How often when duty stared thee in the face,
Though thy body was weak, thou wert found in thy place;
And though storms might be raging and winds blowing high,
If appointments were made thou wert sure to be nigh;
But now thou hast passed from thine earth-friends away
To mingle with others in Heaven’s bright day.
And we feel thou hast finished thy mission on earth,
And gone to that home which was thine before birth;
Gone to meet the dear friends, thou didst love here so well,
Forever in peace with the righteous to dwell.
										AMY.

	Sister Rachel Davenport died at her residence  in
Iron County, Utah, Feb. 25th, 1889. She has been
a sufferer from Asthma and Dropsy for about eleven years,
and has borne her afflictions with patience; was always
cheerful, and had a kind word, and good advice to give
everyone, and was a strong believer in all the principles
of the Gospel. She was born April 6th, 1854, at Stockfort,
Lanchshire, England. She was the daughter of Thomas
and Mary Ann Wamby Pass, aged 34 years 10 months and
19 days.
	Her parents joined the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints in the old country. She left there when
but eight years old, and emigrated to Utah with Brother
Willivm H. Dame, who was on a mission in that part of
the country; her parents emigrated some years afterwards,
but she remained in his family; Sister Louisa Dame watched


over her as mother. She was always a good, faithful and
obedient child.
	In the fall of 1872 she was married in the Salt Lake
Endowment house to James B. Davenport, and has since
been a faithful wife and mother. She was a member, of 
our Relief Society, and was willing to do all that laid in
her power for the advancement of the work. She always
manifested in her labors that meek and unselfish disposition,
characteristic of a true Saint of God.
	She leaves a husband, three children, and a great num-
ber of relatives and friends who miss her sadly.
					ELIZA BARTON, President.
					ANNIE M. TOPHAM, Secretary.


Training School for Nurses
     On the fifteenth of May, the Second Term of Dr.
Hughes’ Training School for Nurses begins, a class
of twelve (12) students completing their first term of
three months on the fourth of May. This is the
first Training School for Nurses established in
Utah, and the instruction given embraces the best
methods of teaching of the Eastern Schools, whose
systems have been thoroughly investigated by the
teacher. The purpose of this school is to give a
wider knowledge of waiting upon the sick, and all
the branches pertaining to nursing will be thorough- 
ly discussed in the successive terms. The second
term will be held in Dr. Hughes’ new residence, on
First West Street. Students desiring to enter, should
make application as early as possible, so that ar-
rangements can be made for securing text books. 
	TUITION,	-	-	-	$25.00.
     Special arrangements can be made for Obstetrical
teaching. Address:
M. P. Hughes,
P. O. Box 575.				Salt Lake City, Utah.
