WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.
VOL. 17.                   SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MAY 15, 1889.                 No. 24.


NELLIE'S CHILD 
We have brought one more dear Jennie,
	To share your quiet rest;
A habe, whose lips but yesterday 
	Were warm on its mother's breast.

But lo! the world's grey reaper,
	In the silent midnight came 
And folded the babe in his mantle;
	And tenderly breathed her name,

To the ever watchful angels,
	As one too fair and sweet,
To battle with earth's temptations,
	Too fragile its storms to meet.

So he hushed her to rest on his bosom,
	And folded the small white hands,
And kissed her lips, as her spirit passed 
	To the waiting angel bands.

Oh! Weep for the mother's anguish,
	Weep, weep, for the father's pain;
As he laid the beautiful lifeless clay, 
	In her outstreched arms again. 
		
For the sounds of that sobbing midnight,
	Though years may come and go,
Will never pass from that darkened home,
	Or the hearts that loved her so. 

No pearl in its case of velvet,
	No flower, just snatched away 
From the parent vine, was ever so fair,
	As the babe we mourn to-day.

So we brought her to you, dear Jennie,
	To the silent city that lies 
Out under the sunlit heavens,
	Out under the starry skies.
		
With its still white stones, like fingers,
	Pointing ever one untrod way,
Pointing up, through the western shadows.
	Pointing up, through the dawn-light grey.

Where, under the arch of the rainbow,
	And the pitiful falling rain,
We laid you to rest from labor,
	We hid you away from pain.

When we covered you up with blessings 
	For the deeds that your hands had done,
And hallowed your last sweet slumber,
	With the love that your life had won. 

And twice, since we brought you hither,
	With sorrowful hearts and sore,
We have come to thy sacred dwelling,
	And stood by an open door.

Once with your own loved child, and now,
	With the babe of your eldest born;
And the doors are closed to be ope'd no more,
	Till the last great, glorious morn.

So we leave them with you, dear Jennie,
	In the silent city, that lies
Out under the starry heavens,
	Out under the sunlit skies,

With the still white fingers pointing,
	From the dust that our teet hath trod,
To the broad blue path that leadeth,
	To the home of your rest with God.
								MILLICENT.
Zenos, A. I. April, 1889.


[Column 1]
THE VOW OF WASHINGTON.
	The following is a copy of Whittier's poem, read at the 
Washington Centennial Celebration in New York on
[Column 2]
﻿Tuesday last by Clarence M. Bowen, Secretary of the
Centennial Committee:

	The sword was sheathed; in April's sun 
	Lay green the fields by freedom won;
And severed sections, weary of debates,
Joined hands at last and were United States.

	O city sitting by the sea !
	How proud the day that dawned on thee,
When the new era, long desired, began,
And, in its need, the hour had found the man! 

	One thought the cannon salvos spoke;
	The resonant bell-tower's vibrant stroke,
The voiceful streets, the plaudit-echoing halls,
And prayer and hymn borne heavenward from 
		St. Paul's!

	How felt the land in every part 
	The strong throb of a nation's heart,
As its great leader gave, with reverent awe,
His pledge to Union, Liberty and Law! 

	That pledge the heavens above him heard,
	That vow the sleep of centuries stirred;
In world-wide wonder listening people bent,
Their gaze on Freedom's great experiment.

	Could it succeed ? Of honor sold 
	And hopes deceived all history told.
Above the wrecks that strewed the mournful past,
Was the long dream of ages true at last? 

	Thank God! the people's choice was just,
	The one man equal to his trust,
Wise beyond lore, and without weakness good,
Calm in the strength of flawless rectitude !

	His rule of justice, order, peace,
	Made possible the world's release;
Taught prince and serf that power is but a trust,
And rule, alone, which serves the ruled, is just;

	That Freedom generous is, but strong 
	In hate of fraud and selfish wrong,
Pretense that turns her holy truths to lies 
And lawless license masking in her guise. 
 
	Land of his love ! with one glad voice 
	Let thy great sisterhood rejoice;
A century's suns o'er thee have risen and set,
And, God be praised, we are one nation yet.

	And still, we trust, the years to be 
	Shall prove his hope was destiny.
Leaving our flag with all its added stars
Unrent by faction and unstained by wars.
 
	Lo ! where with patient toil he nursed 
	And trained the new-set plant at first,
The widening branches of a stately tree,
Stretch from the sunrise to the sunset sea.
 
	Aud in its broad and sheltering shade,
	Sitting with none to make afraid,
Were we now silent, through each mighty limb,
The winds of heaven would sing the praise of him.

	Our first and best!—his ashes lie
	Beneath his own Virginian sky.
Forgive, forget, O true and just and brave,
The storm that swept above thy sacred grave ! 

	For, ever in the awful strife 
	And dark hours of the nation's life;
Through the fierce tumult pierced his warning word,
Their father's voice his erring children heard ! 

	The change for which he prayed and sought
	In that sharp agony was wrought;
No partial interest draws its alien line 
'Twixt North and South; the cypress and the pine !

	One people now, all doubt beyond,
	His name shall be our Union bond;
We lift our hands to Heaven, and here and now,
Take on our lips the old Centennial vow.
[Column 3]
	For rule and trust must needs be ours;
	Chooser and chosen both are powers
Equal in service as in rights; the claim
Of Duty rests on each and all the same.

	Then let the sovereign millions where
	Our banner floats in sun and air,
From the warm palm lands to Alaska's cold,
Repeat with us the pledge a century old !

Oak Knoll, Danvers, Mass.


KISSING. 	
	To kiss according to Webster means "to
salute with the lips.” And the pleasure derived
there from depends entirely upon the relation-
ship, existing between the kisser and the kissed.
If the souls meet, a thrill of joy and pleasure
permeates the whole system; but if the object
kissed be not a human being, the effect can
not possibly be the same, though the impres-
sion produced may be just as powerful,—forin-
stance when the Idolater kisses his Idol, a re-
ligious sensation comes over him—all his better
nature is aroused, and the baser and more
selfish part of his composition remains in the
back ground. He lives only for his God, and
at that moment, any or every earthly posses-
sion would be sacrificed to further any purpose
believed to be desired by the Idol he worships.
While in such moods the Priests of the dark
ages, used to exert their influence, to lead the
people, and gained control of the earth, so great
their exertions, and so patient their toil.
	When a fond mother takes her babe upon
her knee, she feels all the enrapture of mater-
nal affection, as she presses kiss after kiss upon
the tiny forehead of the little one who nestles
close to the one person, in all the world, whom
it loves. Such pure unadulterated love exists
nowhere else in all humanity. —
	Though the next is the expression (through
the lips) of that deep and all powerful love,
which a noble man bears toward a noble wo-
man, and without which no woman is safe in
becoming a wife—Wicked and unprincipled
men are incapable of such a deep feeling love,
and what the soul does not feel the lips can not
portray.
	Again there is the kiss of deceit, such as
a false friend would administer, the same that
Judas gave his Master in the betrayal—and
the sympathetic kiss given by a parent to a
suffering child, or a living friend to one who
has passed to the other side. In such kisses
the heart speaks, there is love and tenderness
iu the touch of the lips, and sometimes great
relief is realized from penned up grief by
bringing tears to the eyes, which have before
refused to be moistened.
	But if the lips are the most sensitive, the
most impressive, and the most delicately con-
structed organs in the whole body;—how easily
disease can be communicated from the sick or
dead, even from heir-looms, to the devoted
friend or relative, who tries to sooth pain, or
whose reverence of God and Ancestors
prompts him to press his lips to the frequently
handled “Holy Bible” or to any of the many
articles left him by his fore fathers.
	After the same manner can the most dreaded
disease, known to mortal man, be transmitted
from father to son, and from sister to sister;
when it was designed by the Almighty that
only those who should abuse their natural gifts
should suffer from so great a punishment. X.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS
		      BEAVER P. A.
	The 10th anniversary of the Beaver Primary
Association was held March 23d, 1889, con-
sisting of a sumptuous dinner, and a programme
for the aged people; all those of seventy years
and upward were invited, and at 1 o'clock p.m.
the aged were seen wending their way to the
First Ward assembly hall, where a table
spread with the good things of the earth
awaited them; the blessing was pronounced by
Patriarch Johnathan Crosby; after which meet-
ing commenced. President Mary A. Lolton
presiding. Singing, "Beautiful Zion." prayer
by Bishop J. X. Smith. Singing. "Beautiful
Day." Minutes of the previous meeting were
read and approved. The programme was as
follows:
	Roy Sheppard recited "The golden keys,"
Carrie Bird sang. Lucy Jones recited "Good
resolutions." An essay on "Let us be useful,"
was read by Jane Grimshaw. Esther Clay-
borne sang. May Cox recited. Willie Ash
sang. Lillie Stoney recited "The dead doll."
A song was rendered by Willie Ash Wilford,
and Frank Burnett. Ella Pitts recited, A
dialogue was rendered by Cassie O'Donnell
and Nora Baker. Vina White recited "Be
kind to the aged." Lydia Hutchings and
Cassie O'Donnell rendered a duett. Sister
Jane Gillis made a few remarks; Counselor
Mary Fernley sang.
	Counselor Agnes Hutchings spoke a short
time, gave some excellent instructions to the
children. Meeting adjourned for one year
singing "O, come children, come," benediction
by Patriarch Daniel Tyler.
			MARY A. LOLTON, President.
			KATIE SMITH, Secretary-

	    SALT LAKE STAKE PRIMARX.
	The quarterly conference of the Primary
Associations of the Salt Lake Stake, was held
in the Salt Lake Assembly Hall, Saturday,
March 16, 1889 at 10 a.m., Counselor Camilla
Cobb presiding. Meeting opened by singing
"Gladly meeting." Prayer by Sister E. How-
ard. Singing, "Good morning," by Mr. Evan
Stephens Primary class; minutes of previous
conference were read and accepted. Recita-
tion by a little boy from the 3d, Ward;
dialogue, two girls from the same ward.
Roscoe. Eardley sang a song. Reports were
then read from the Primaries of the 3,4,5, and
7th wards. Duett by two girls of the 5th,
ward, "Buy my flowers." Reports read from
the 8,10. and 14th wards. Recitation by a
little girl from the 9th ward. "The children
are praying for me," was sung by little Miss
Bowring; reports from the 16,17,20, and 21st
wards.
	Sister E. B. Wells addressed the children;
regretted there were not more boys attending
our primary. Told the children about the Or-
phan Asylum, and that they should be mindful
every day of their blessings in Zion; urged
them to cultivate Faith; related a story illus-
trating her remarks upon the principle of
Faith. Also ureged them to be obedient to 
their parents and to those who preside in their
associations.
	A little boy of the 8th ward sang a song,
also annie Sorenson of the 9th ward sang, and
two girls of the 10, ward sang "Why did they
dig mamma's grave so deep." Reports read
from the 13,15, and Sugar House wards. Song
by a little girl of the 12th ward.
	Sister Zina D. H. Young praised the child-
ren's exercises; wished the Presidents to hand
in the names and ages of the children who
took part in the conference. Asked the child-
ren to repeat after her in chorus:
	'Tis education forms the common mind,
	Just as the twig is bent, the tree's inclined.
Johnnie Merrill and Master Fox each recited
[Column 2]
very nicely. A dialogue was given by five
girls of the 16th ward. Questions were an-
swered by four girls and two boys of the 17th
ward. More reports were read making nineteen
in all. Essay, Master Woodruff of the 21st
ward. Willie Stevens and a little girl of
Union Ward each recited.
	Sister Mary A. Freeze spoke a few minutes
to the children on order and "The word of
Wisdom." Sister E. Howard made a few in-
teresting remarks. Counselor Camilla Cobb
spoke a few words, and adjourned the con-
ference for three months; singing "Good
night," prayer by Counselor Lydia Ann Wells.
					BESSIE DEAN Sec.
		MARICOPA STAKE.
	Our Relief Society conference was held
March 22d, at the Mess Hall commencing at
1 p. m., President S. M. Pomeroy presiding,
President C. I. Robson and counselors and
other brethren were present; opened by sing-
ing and prayer. President S. M, Pomeroy,
desired the faith and prayes of the brethren
and sisters present, to sustain her in her new
position, presumed we should all miss the kind
face of our former President Sister E. S. Mc-
Donald and none more than herself; prayed
that our hearts might be unitedly raised in
prayer to God, for blessings upon this con
ference, that all who may speak may be
filled with His Spirit.
	Roll of officers called showing a full at-
tendance; minutes of previous conference read
and approyed. Presidents of the several
branches gave verbal reports, showing a
marked improvement among the sisters, espec-
ially in the attendance of meetings. Sister
E.G. McDonald having arrived by the Mesa
stage, was listened to with pleasure while she
addressed the meeting a short time: was happy
to be in our midst again; had enjoyed her
visit while away. Spoke briefly concerning
the prospects of our people who had gathered
to Mexico; thought their hardest trials were
over and their prospects were good for the
future. Thought the authorities there were
friends to our people; they had enver shed the
blood of the Saints or persecuted them; they
were kindly disposed, thought that would be a
place of refuge for the Saints. Spoke of the
principle of Charity. "Our trials cause us to
draw near unto the Lord; we should avoid the
spirit of selfishness, in giving and aiding each
other we are blessed." Spoke of the influence
of the evil one, who is continually laying
snares to entrap those who is continually laying
snares to entrap those who are the hightest, or
who are doing the most good.
	S. M. Pomeroy was pleased Sister McDonald
was with us but regretted it would be for such
a short time. Was gratified with the reports
from the different branches, they were cer-
tainly encouraging. Had visited in company
with her counselors the several places in the
Stake, and cold testify to the truth of the
reports. Spoke of the Indian mission, where
some of our sisters are laboring so faithfully
to benefit and redeem them from their degraded
state; "we should assist them with our faith and
prayers, and not stop there, but donate liberally
to the support of these faithful brethren and
sisters. We should exercise the spirit of
Charity towards each other, avoid slander,
and the spirit of ridicule, which is pernicious
in its effects, and is explained by a famous
German critic as the wit of an ignorant or
stupid person; it is indulged in thoughtlessly
by some just for fun, but somebody is always
hurt." She desired to honor the position she
was placed in, and work in harmony with
the authority of the Stake. The statistical
and financial reports were read and the Relief
society officers sustained.
	Counselor Henry Rogers was glad to see the
growth of the Society; felt that the kingdom
of God was growing; referred to the duties of
[Column 3]
a Teacher. It is no trifling thing to be an
officer in these organizations; let each one re-
alize the responsibility resting upon them and
at the same time be joyful."
	President Charles Robson had listened with
pleasure to the remarks that had been made:
had yet to attend a meeting of the Latter-day
Saints where there was not good instructions;
“it is left with us to carry them out. It is our
duty to live by every word of God unto us.
Whilst the wife is given as an helpmeet, if she
exercises her powers rightfully, she becomes
the larger half.” Felt to bless the sisters and
honor them in their calling as a Relief Society.
Made many more interesting remarks which
were timely and to the point.
	Counselor Sarah Russell spoke briefly: felt
that life was too short to indulge in ill-feelings
towards each other.  "We can assist others in
many ways with words of sympathy and love.”
	Brother Charles Allen spoke a short time, in
a very interesting and encouraging manner;
felt the sisters were doing a good work among
the Saints; and gaye many good instructions to
mothers in Israel. Brother A. F. Stewart and
Oscar Stewart each bore testimony of the
good work being done by the sisters.
	Sister E. G. McDonald wished to make a
few remarks in regard to out new President;
urged the sisters rtq sustain her in her posi- 
tion; bore testimony to her faithfulness as
president of the Teachers and Stake secretary.
The Teachers had heen faithful in their duties,
and the success of the Society was mainly due
to their faithfulness. Their meetings were
among the best she had attended; also bore
testimony to the faithfulness and diligence of
her former counselor Sisters E. Morris and
Sarah Russell, felt this was a duty she owed
them. Thanked the sisters for the faith and
confidence they had eyer shown her.
	President S.M. Pomeroy made closing re-
marks. Felt to thank Sister McDonald for
her kindly interest in her behalf. Felt desir-
ous of doing her duty regardless of fear or
criticism. Thanked the brethren for their
good instructions in regard to our duties, felt
it obligatory (upon us to treasure them up and
put them in practice in our lives.
	Sister Addie Passie was apppointed repre-
sent the Maricopa Stake Relief Society at the
conference to be held in Salt Lake City in
April. The President also recommended the
sisters of the Stake to subscribe for the EX-
PONENT. Conference adjourned for three
months. Singing. Benediction by H. Rogers.
			SUSAN WILCOX, Secretary.


Miss Mary A. Brigham, of Brooklyn, the
new president of Mount Holyoke Seminary
and College, is an alumna of that institution,
and a native of Westboro', Mass. She has
taught at Mount Holyoke and at Ingham Uni-
versity, Leroy, N. Y., besides in Brooklyn, and
has been offered a professorship at Smith
College and the presidency of Wellesley.
	Cases of infection that could be accounted
for in no other way, have been explained by
the fingers as a vehicle.  In handling money,
especially of paper, door knobs, banisters, car
straps, and a hundred things that every one
must frequently touch, there are chances in-
numerable of picking up germs of typhoid,
scarlatina, diphtheria, smallpox, &c. Yet
some persons actually put such things in their
mouths, if not too large! Before eating, or
touching that which is to be eaten, the hands
should be immediately  and scrupulously
washed. We hear much about general clean-
linness as "next to godliness." It may be
added that here, in particular, it is also ahead
of health and safety. The Jews made no mis-
take in that "except they washed they ate not."
It was a sanitary ordinance as well as an ordi-
nance of decency.			Sanitary Era.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


SISTER MARTHA.
Dear, patient, suffering one, how very small;
Aught I can do to aid thee, if at all.
One glim'ring ray of light I may extend
To cheer thy loneliness, My Mother's Friend.

My mother loved thee, ere I knew the worth
Of friendship, such as her's and thine, on earth;
She's found that home, of peace and rest so sweet,
Where thou wilt soon, herself and others greet.

The lips of innocence thy name shall bear
Unto the Father; and in guileless prayer,
Tell Him how longingly thy soul doth wait,
Eager to enter the Eternal Gate.

And it shall be, when shades of evening fall,
Thine ears shall hear sweet Seraph voices call,
Bidding thee welcome to the heavenly place,
'Neath the approving smiles of God's dear face.

Peace, peace, oh weary heart! And from this hour,
Be lifted far above each darkening power.
Though things of earth, thine eyes no more behold,
Within thy soul, shall heavenly light unfold. 

The time no longer shall seem long to thee,
Henceforth thy spirit shall be glad and free;
And soon—yea, very soon shall waft away,
From night of gloom, into Celestial day.

To thy poor eyes, all sightless many a year,
How doubly glorious will that light appear;
Which shall reveal, in beauty, and in truth,
Faces and forms, which thou hast loved in youth.

How near, to me, that world of spirits seems!
How oft dear friends, from thence, return in dreams.
Oh, sister Martha, to my parents there,
Wilt thou, for me, a message kindly bear?

Tell them, I'm striving to be meek and pure,
Like them, life's crosses bravely to endure;
That I would tarry till my boys are men,
And help them learn God's holy will and then—

Well, I'll not state what I may then desire,
I'll only ask that God will me inspire,
That all my ways may be his ways for me;
Please tell them this my constant aim shall be.

A few more days for thee—then will be drawn
Aside the veil—from us thou wilt be gone;
Gone to thy loved ones, to thy sure reward;
Dear Sister Martha, Praise, oh, praise the Lord.
								LULA.
April 15th, 1889.


THE POET’S SEASON.
	It has been said, and truly, that Spring is
the poet’s season.  There is a peculiar fresh-
ness in the earth, and in the air, which pene-
trates, as it were, to the very centre of all in-
animate things, and causes the blades of grass
to shoot upward, the leaf and the floweret to
burst forth.  The soul, which is higher than
any of these forms of beauty, must in conform-
ity with creative forces be in sympathy with
all these, especially the soul of the poet, that is
ever more or less en rapport with nature.  The
poet feels this beauty without, this overflowing
of nature’s fountains, and the same impulse is
intensified in him, because the higher order of
his creation demands expression.  In the
Spring the birds carol their sweetest music,
warble forth their most charming melodies;
they sing for joy that new life has sprung up.
The poet Thomson, in his "Season," beseeches
the birds for melody:

	"Lend me your song, ye nightingales!  Oh pour
	The mazy running soul of melody 
	Into my varied verse, while I deduce
	From the first note the hollow cuckoo sings
	The symphony of Spring."

	There is so much music in Springtime that it
is little wonder one tries to catch the inspira-
tion, and utilize some of the rich material to
embellish life in its sober work-a-day phases.

[Column 2]
How beautiful the power of the keenly sensi-
tive, gifted soul who can gather up the sun-
beams, the dewdrops, the music of wood and
glen, in flowing fountain or in song of birds,
and apply its essence and its sweetness to rhyme
and rhythm, and thus carry the pleasures of
Spring into the homes of the people, who, while
they are employed in the various occupations
of life, can nevertheless enjoy the sentiments
others have expressed, and in unison with the
poet exclaim, “These are my own thoughts and
aspirations.” As the chemist extracts and
condenses the strength of the elements of sub-
stances, and uses them for the various purposes
to which they are necessary for man, in food
and medicine, so the poet adds to the richness
of the inner life, the metaphysical, elevating its
tone and giving grace and beauty to the mind
and heart.
	It is not alone the birds who feel the in-
fluence of Spring, but the young lambs skip
and gambol, the calves frolic as though con-
scious of the delights of nature everywhere ap-
parent to man.  Indeed, all the greatest efforts
of nature seem to have culminated through in-
nate forces and assumed tangible forms, or to
have wakened after the long rest of Winter
into newness of life.  The affections are
stronger, too, or at least we so comprehend it,
at this season of bud and bloom, and Tennyson
tells us,in that oft quoted stanza from Locksley
Hall:

"In the Spring a fuller crimson comes upon the robin's
	breast;
In the Spring the wanton lapwing gets himself another
	crest;
In the Spring a livelier iris changes on the burnish'd
	dove;
In the Spring a young man’s fancy lightly turns to
	thoughts of love."

	All the pulses of nature are quickened and
the corresponding human pulses likewise, when
the body is in good condition.  Lowell says:

	"What we call nature, all outside ourselves,
	Is but our own conceit of what we see,
	Our own reaction upon what we feel—
	I find my own complexion everywhere."

	Coleridge has said, too:

		"We receive but what we give, 
And in our life alone doth nature live.
Ours is the wedding garment, ours the shroud."

	We might go on quoting the opinions of the
poets on this inexhaustible subject, which has
been so much talked of and still remains more
or less of a mystery, but suffice it to say, the
poet is an interpreter of the beautiful in nature,
but the scientist covers the entirety, for he an-
alyzes nature; and instead of giving us, as the
poet does, its music and beauty inspirationally,
he gives quality and utility, and styles himself
the true interpreter of nature’s forces. 
	Having skimmed over a little of the ground,
and dwelt upon birdnotes and melody, we next
consider the flowers in this connection. 	
	The flowers of Spring are as necessary to in-
spire the poet as are the songs of birds, or
the babble of brooks; and flowers are not only
one of the elements of poetry, but also symbols
of love.  Music, flowers, poetry and love are
very nearly related.  Who can faithfully de-
lineate the effect of flowers upon the senses.
They speak to us forcibly in a silent language
none fully understand, but all interpret in a
measure.  They feast the eye gratefully, and
their fragrance is exhilarating to every sense
we possess.  To enumerate the varieties and
delights of flowers would be impossible, we all
have some favorites, and we all comprehend
what a wilderness this earth would be without
them.  Mary Howitt expresses our own
thought, as true poets ever do, when she
says;

[Column 3]
	"God might have made the earth bring forth
	    Enough for great and small,
	The oak tree and the cedar tree,
	    Without a flower at all;	
	He might have made enough—enough
	    For every want of ours,
	For luxury, medicine and toil,
	    And yet have made no flowers."

	From time immemorial flowers have been
associated with love and tenderness, indeed,
tenderness is a part of love, for what we love
most we are most tender of.  Whatever appeals
to the heart fosters and strengthens the affec-
tions.  It is perhaps because the Spring is the
season when flowers are freshest, and birds sing
sweetest, and skies are bluest, that love is
strongest.  And one’s own heart is so full of
the tender passion that one wonders what the
birds say to each other, and what the flowers
think, and the poet sings of their secrets in
strains like these:

	"I wonder what the Clover thinks,	
	Intimate friend of Bob-o-links,	
	Lover of Daisies slim and white,
	Waltzer with Buttercups at night,

	Oh, who knows what the Clover thinks 
	No one unless the Bob-o-links!"

	Who would sing such a song except one in
love, yes, in love with nature?  And who that
has felt the divine sentiment in its purity and
intensity does not know that to be in love
glorifies all beauty?  In Eastern lands, where
love is said to be most intense, there flowers
abound and fruits are most luxuriant.  Flowers
in the fields and meadows feast the eye of the
traveler and inspire the pen of the poet, but
flowers in and about the home are indications
of beauty in life and character.  Their influ-
ence is always good and brightening.  The
lowliest cottage is handsomely adorned when
flowers are blooming round it, and graceful
vines trail o’er its doorways or its portico.
Even, those who pass by respect its inmates
more, and to the indwellers these simple adorn-
ments are often of more value in adding to
their happiness and enjoyment than the ele-
gancies and splendor of the rich.
	Nature herself is rich in embellishment, if
we will but dispose her varied gifts becom-
ingly.  At all seasons of the year the poet may
find himself in harmony with nature, but the
Spring has many advantages, many charms to
beguile the poet into musical, mazy rhythm.
Therefore we should be lenient with even the
humblest aspirant who sips at the perennial
fount of poesy, remembering that all are weak
compared to the great Author of that nature
to which we are indebted for our subjects
Undoubtedly Spring is the poet’s season, for it
is then God’s goodness strikes us most forcibly,
and the poet must drink freely from the pure
fount of knowledge, and  all intelligence
comes from Him, and He is love.
							AUNT EM.

will take part:
	Mrs. Ednah D. Cheney; “Civil Marriage,”

ment of Prisoners,” Maria P. Bray: “House-
keeping as a Profession,” Miss Ripley: “The
Sacrifice of Education to Examinations,” Dr.
Smith: “Common-Sense in the Nursery,” Mrs.
Myers: “What Authors Influence the Mind of
Youth To-day,” and Mrs. H. L. T. Wolcott
“Horticulture.”
