SALT LAKE CITY,	-	-	MAY 15, 1889.


[Column 1]﻿
WASHINGTON’S INAUGURATION.
	On the 30th, of April a century ago in the city
of New York a ceremony took place which has
just now been commemorated throughout the
United States in a fitting manner after the lapse
of a hundred years. The inauguration of George
Washington the first president of the Great Re-
public is an event of such importance that the
nation can well afford to celebrate with charac-
teristic observances the hundredth anniversary.
“First in war, first in peace, and first in the hearts
of his countrymen,” was this great General, hero
and President. After all these long years his
name is everywhere spoken with tenderness and
reverence.
	An interesting and memorable centennial cele-
bration of the anniversary of the first inauguration
is indeed a just and honorable tribute to the mem-
ory of this illustrious General patriot and states-
man, and awakens in the hearts of the people an
appreciation of the blessings that have come down
to them as a heritage from the efforts and labors
of those heroic men whom God inspired to es-
tablish religious liberty and freedom upon this
goodly land, America.
	New York City was very different then from
what it is to-day. The ceremonials of the time
were however very grand and elaborate, and the
display was on a magnificent scale. The formal
official ceremony took place in the Senate
Chamber of the Federal State House, which
looked out upon Broad Street.
	“The windows and the roofs of the houses were
crowded and in the streets the throng was so
dense that it seemed as if one might literally walk
on the heads of the people. The balcony of the
hall was in full view of this assembled multitude.
In the centre of it was placed a table with a rich
covering of red velvet and upon this on a crimson
velvet cushion lay a large and elegant Bible.”
	General Washington’s “entrance on the
balcony was announced by universal shouts of joy
and welcome. His appearance was most solemn
and dignified. Advancing to the front of the
balcony he laid his hand on his heart bowed
several times, and then retired to an arm-chair
near the table. The populace appeared to under-
stand that the scene had overcome him and were
at once hushed in profound silence. After a few
moments Washington arose and came forward,
Chancellor Livingston read the oath according to
the form prescribed by the Constitution, and
Washington repeated it resting his hand upon the
table. Mr. Otis the Secretary of the Senate, then
took the Bible and raised it to the lips of Wash-
ington, who stooped and kissed the book. At
this moment a signal was given by raising a flag
upon the cupola of the hall for a general discharge
of the artillery of the Battery. All the bells in
the city rang out a peal of joy, and the assembled

[Column 2]
multitude sent forth a universal shout: The Presi-
dent again bowed to the people and then retired
from a scene such as the proudest monarch never
enjoyed."
	The ball which was to have been given on the
evening of Inauguration Day was postponed until
May 5th, 1789. Of the ladies who were present on
that occasion much has been said and written and
the subject has been revived during the last few
weeks, and the dresses and jewels worn by them
as well as their illustrious names received due
prominence. The ball was given in the Assembly
Rooms which were in a large wooden building
standing upon the site of the Old City Hall or at
115. Broadway where the Barrell building now is.
About three hundred were present and it is said
Washington danced in the cotillion and in the
minuet at this ball.
	The general centennial celebration of the in-
auguration of George Washington took place in
New York on the 30th of April last, although in
all parts of the United States there were services
and ceremonials and it was observed everywhere
as a National holiday. Utah was not an exception
to the general rule. In the large Tabernacle in
Salt Lake City, also in Ogden and in Provo, there
were suitable services, speeches and music. The
orator of the day in Salt Lake City was Lieut. R.
W. Young a grandson of Brigham Young. In
Provo Bishop O. F. Whitney delivered the ora-
torial address. Both these young gentlemen
acquitted themselves admirably and delighted the
assembled populace. In Salt Lake City the Hon.
Geo. Q. Cannon made a thrilling patriotic speech;
we should be glad to publish in full the speeches
made on the occasion but that is not practicable.
The daily newspapers give full reports of the
wonderful proceedings, parades, speeches and
music, in all the cities and towns throughout the
country. Hereafter we may give some sketches
of the proceedings in New York City.


WEDDING BELLS.
"Hail !  Wedded love, 
Perpetual fountain of domestic sweets."  
	On Tuesday at half past 8 a. m., the 30th, of
April 1889, a marriage ceremony was solemnized
at the residence of Major W. W. Woods, in
Murray, Idaho, the aged Father Joset of the "Old
Mission,” officiating.  In this marriage the writer
has a particular interest, the bride being her
grand-daughter Verona May Woods, who only a
few months ago left the city with her parents who
moved from here to Northern Idaho.  The young
lady was for some time an employee of this office
and her face was familiar to those, who are in the
habit of frequenting it from time to time. The
bridegroom is Mr. Barry N. Hillard, of Murray,
formerly of San Francisco.  The young couple
started the morning of their marriage on their
wedding tour coming direct to this City where
Mrs. Hillard has many relatives and friends.
They were met at the depot on their arrival the
evening of May 2d. by a party of ladies, with a
warm welcome—greetings and congratulations
and a profusion of flowers.  They remained here
only a few days, the time being fully occupied with
visits and calls.  They left on the morning of May
7th, for Chicago where they will spend a few days
in sight-seeing returning home via the Northern
Pacific R.R. taking in St. Paul’s and Minneaopolis
enroute.  May every blessing possible rest upon
the newly wedded pair and the richness of love’s
fruition crown their lives.
	“But happy they!  the happiest of their kind!
	Whom gentle stars unite, and in one fate
	Their hearts, their fortunes, and their beings blend.”


A SAD BEREAVEMENT.
	Only a few months ago we published the death
of the wife of Heber M. Wells, and now it be-


comes again our painful duty to record the sad
death of his little son, Manning Beatie Wells, who
departed this life May, 10th, at half past five p m.
His death was peaceful and wholly free from pain,
sinking as though falling into a profound slumber,
the immediate cause being partial paralysis of the
throat.  He was, no doubt, ushered into the pre-
sence of angels," who rejoiced at his coming, 
though unseen to those who waited by his bedside
with sobs and moans.
	For the father, in his lonliness, so suddenly be-
reft, the most intense sympathy is felt.  But he
has the consolation of the promises of the Gospel
to trust in, and two other little children who need
his loving care and constant attention.

	They are not lost!  They are within the door
		That shuts out loss, and every hurtful thing--
	With angels bright--and loved ones gone before,
	In the Redeemer's presence evermore, 
		And God himself, their Lord, and Judge and
			King."

	Manning was born in this City August, 1884,
and was a most sensitive and interesting child.
Fondly attached to his mother, he seems to have 
longed for her ever since her death in October
last.  His last words to his agonized father who
sat by his dying bed, breathed more than whis-
pered, were, "Papa, I'm going home!" strikingly
significant and prophetic were those parting
words.  Who shall say that this little child had
not an intuition of the joy of meeting his be-
loved mother; and that just upon the verge of
that "better land," he paused to breathe this
farewell to the beloved one he was leaving 
behind.

	"Better than earthly presence, e'en the dearest,
		Is the great blessing that our partings bring;
	For in the loneliest moments, God is nearest, 
		And from our sorrows heavenly comforts spring, 
			If God be with us."

	The funeral was held at the residence on Sun-
day, May 12, at 12 m., Bishop O. F. Whitney pre-
siding.  The speakers were Bishop Whitney,
Elder John Nicholson, and Apostle H. J. Grant.
The singing was almost heavenly; and the casket
in which lay the form of the little one was literally
buried with flowers, tokens of love from those 
who mourned.  At the grave the scene was pitiful
but all that could be done, to comfort the father
and relatives, seemed to have been accomplished.
The grave was dedicated by Apostle H. J. Grant,
and the choir sang "Rest on the hillside."


SILVER WEDDING.
	Quite a large party of friends of Mrs. Emily H.
Woodmansee assembled at her residence in this
City on the evening of May 7th, to offer their con-
gratulations on the anniversary of her wedding
day twenty-five years previous, and to express
their esteem for one who has contributed so much
to home literature in Utah.  For more than thirty
years Mrs. Woodmansee has written from time
to time, poems that have voiced the sentiments of
the “Mormon” people, and which invariably con-
tain the true ring of the pure metal.  Many choice
gifts of silver articles were brought as small
tokens of friendship and the appreciation of her
labors.  The house was handsomely decorated
with flowers and brilliantly lighted for the occa-
sion.  One thing detracted somewhat from the
enjoyment of the affair; it was the absence of one
of the principal parties in the contract twenty-five
years ago—Mr. Woodmansee himself-—however
the guests were “on pleasure bent,” and there
was much feasting and merriment.  Several fine
singers rendered favorite melodies and simple
ballads awakening sweet and tender memories,
among them were Mrs. Maggie Romney once a
popular songstress, but whom we seldom hear


now-a-days, also Miss Fanny Graehl, Miss Nettie
Raliegh and Miss Gladys Woodmansee—and we
must not neglect to mention in this connection
Mr. C. W. Stayner and Mr. C. R. Savage, The
principal feature of the entertainment, however,
was a poem written by Mrs. Woodmansee and
read by her during the evening as a welcome to
her guests.
		A WORD OF WELCOME.
				———
ADDRESSED TO AN ASSEMBLAGE OF FRIENDS.
				———
	Welcome ! welcome, friends and guests—
		To the best my house can give;
	I am honored, pleased and blessed, 
		Your kind notice to receive.
			Near and dear ye are forsooth
			In the bonds of love and truth.

	From the utmost ends of earth,
		From the kin we call'd our own;
	From the lands that gave us birth,
		Came we here for truth alone;
			Heaven hath made us rich amends.
			Heaven hath sent us sterling friends.

	"In the mountain of the Lord,
		Where resplendent Temples rise,
	Where Jehovah's law and word
		  Goeth forth" to make us wise;
			Where congenial spirits meet,
			Social bliss is most complete.

	High and low, the pure of heart
		On an equal platform stand;
	Wherefore should we drift apart,
		"Be ye one" 'tis Christ's command,
			Pride of riches, pride of race
			Is, in Zion, out of place.


	False distinctions mar the age,
		Haughty souls contempt inspire; 
	Goodness ! is God's standard guage,
		Yet, will modest worth mount higher.
			Children of the King of Kings,
			Be not slaves to friv'lous things.


	Subtle foes our homes surround—
		Waiting on their prey to spring;
	Honest friends are captive bound—
		Close together should we cling.
		 	Link'd by love, both hand and heart—
			Each to each can strength impart.


	Though we drain Life's mingled cup,
		Joys we have beyond compare.
	"Saints of Latter-days" look up !
		Days await us bright and fair.
			Tribulation, loss and pain,
			Yet shall yield us endless gain.

	'Neath this roof, to-night, my friends,
		May you some enjoyment see;
	Cordially my heart extends—
		Unto each a welcome free.
			Welcome, welcome, great and small,
			Welcome, welcome one and all.
					EMILY H. WOODMANSEE.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


EDITORIAL NOTES.
				———
	THE next number of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT
June 1, will commence Volume 18, and after care-
ful consideration, we have decided to place this
little home publication within the reach of every
family in Zion, by reducing the price of the sub-
scription, instead of improving the appearance of
the paper.  The price for the coming volume will
be one dollar per year to all subscribers, except
to those living in this city whose paper is sent by
post and that will be 25 cents extra for postage
and also on all foreign papers.
	The regular meeting of the Woman Suffrage
Association of Utah was held in the Social hall
Thursday, May 9, 1889.  Owing to the indisposi-
tion of the President, Mrs. Caine, the chair was
occupied by Mrs. P. J. Riter. Miscellaneous
business having been completed, the following
programme was carried out: Mrs. M. Y. Dougall


read a sketch of Mrs. Mary A. Livermore which
contained a great many exemplary features; as
she was not only a model wife and housekeeper,
but also a lady of superior intellectual ability.
Hon. A. Miner favored the association with an in-
teresting speech upon the object and advancement
of the suffragists.  Miss Viola Pratt, in her
charming manner, favored the assemblage with a
solo entitled "Last Night," which was highly ap-
preciated.  The time having fully expired, a
speech from the Beaver Suffrage association, to be
read by Mrs. Nellie Little, was postponed until
the next meeting.  The association then adjourned
for one month.
	The ladies of Nephi met in the tabernacle at 2
p. m. May 11th, for the purpose of organizing a
Woman Suffrage association.  The meeting was
called to order by Mrs. Mary Pitchforth.  Mrs. 
Pitchforth was nominated chairman of the meet-
ing and Mrs. M. E. Teasdale secretary.  Singing by
the choir:  prayer by Mrs. S. A. Andrews. The
secretary then read the call.  The following
officers were elected:  President, Miss L. A.
Schofield; first vice-president, Mrs. E. B. Bryan; 
second vice-president, Mrs. M. E. Neff; third
vice-president, Mrs. M. A. Cazier; secretary, Miss
Lou Udall; corresponding secretary, Mrs. Annie
Atkin; treasurer, Mrs. L. A, Hartley; executive
board, chairman, Mrs. M. A. Grover; Mrs. M. E
Whitmore, Mrs. Mary Wright, Miss Emma
Adams, Mrs. Ella Jones, Mrs. S. E. Abbott, and
Mrs. Julia Paxman, members of the board.  The 
constitution was then read and adopted. Speeches
were made on woman suffrage by the follow-
ing named ladies and gentleman:  Miss L. A.
Schofield, Mrs. M. Pitchforth, Mr. F. W. Chappell,
Mr. W. H. Jones, Mr. J. W. Paxman and Mr.
Charles Sperry.  The Payson brass band
rendered sweet music at intervals.  The meeting
was well attended by the ladies of Nephi, and a
very pleasant and profitable time was spent.


[Column 1]
WOMAN SUFFRAGE IN OGDEN.

	An official call having been previously issued,
signed by influential women of Ogden, for a 
meeting of suffragists to be held in the Ogden
tabernacle, Monday, April 1, 1889,  accordingly,
in response, there was a large number of ladies
in attendance.  The meeting was called to order
by Mrs. H. C. Brown.  Mrs.  Emily S. Richards,
having been appointed chairman of the Executive
committee for Utah, at the last Annual Conven-
tion of the N. W. S. A. in Washington D. C.
April, 1888, was nominated chairman, protem, of
the meeting.
	The chairman then stated that the first business
in order was to elect a Secretary protem.  Mrs.
Mattie A.  Cannon was elected to that position.
	Meeting opened by singing, "Know this that
every soul is free."  Prayer by the chaplain, Mrs.
Sarah Richards.
	Mrs. Ellen Hamer moved that the chairman
appoint a committee on Constitution and By-laws.
The chair appointed, Emily M. Shirtliff, Ruthinda 
Monch, and Mamie Richards.  While the com-
mittee retired, Mrs. Jane S. Richards, by request,
stated briefly, the object of the meeting.
	Mrs.  Shirtliff being ready to report, read the
By-laws, and upon motion, they were adopted as
read.  The chairman stated: "It is in
order to adjourn for ten minutes, so that
those who wish to become members may have the
privelege of signing the Constitution and becom-
ing members, by paying the initiation fee of
50 cts.
	Ten minutes being up, the house was called to
order by the chairman, who stated the next busi-
ness in order was the election of the permanent 
officers of the Association.
	Mrs. Ella Woodmansee moved that the chair

[Column 2]
appoint a committee to nominate permanent offi-
cers.  The chair then appointed Mrs. Jane S.
Richards, Mrs. H. C. Brown and Mrs. Sarah
Herrick as the committee.
	The following ladies were appointed.  Eliza-
beth Y. Stanford, President; Mamie Richards,
first vice-president; Charlotte Driver, second vice-
president;  Ellen Pingree, secretary;  Aggie
Herrick, assistant secretary; Maggie Chambers
corresponding secretary;  Ella Woodmansee,
treasurer.
	Executive committee—Chairman; Rose Canfield
—Marian Burton—Ellen Hamer—Sarah Bel-
nap—Rose Scowcroft—Belle Littlefield—Nellie
S. Thomas;—Harriet Nye.
	Mrs. A. E. Monch addressed the association
upon the topic of Woman Suffrage.  "Many men
believe that a woman's knowledge should be
limited to the home circle; but when we have
other knowledge, such as an understanding of
Civil Government and kindred subjects, we im-
prove our humble cottages, our home life.  An
eminent writer says: "Show me the mothers of a
nation and I will tell you its character."  The
lady closed with the hope of trying to encourage 
all in the noble cause.
	Mrs. Emily S. Richards responded when called
upon, and expressed herself as being very much
interested in this great question.  Did not see
why we should not have the franchise.  The
ladies should be well posted in politics, and able
to hold many public positions as well as men,
and therefore wished to encourage her co-lobor-
ers to regain their votes if possible.
	President E. Y. Stanford was nominated as a
Delegate to attend the Convention held in Salt
Lake City April 9th.  For a short time President
E. Y. Stanford spoke by request.  "Woman has
charge of the rising generation, and for this
reason she should feel interested in the question
of Woman Suffrage and everything that tends to
enlightenment.  Woman's opinions and rights
should be respected; especially, when considered
that it is to benefit the human race, not only in
government, but in every place where her influence
extends, and she should have the largest liberty
to gain intelligence, and to exercise it in promot,
ing the greatest good."  The Association ad-
journed for one month.  Prayer by the chaplain-
Mrs. Sarah Richards.
 			ELLEN PINGREE, Secretary.

	The monthly meeting of the Woman Suffrage
Association of Ogden was held in the County
Court House, May 2d, '89.
	The Association was called to order by Presi-
dent E. Y. Stanford.  Prayer by the chaplain E.
H. Nye, "Glee" by the choir under the leadership 
of Prof. L. D. Edwards.
	An address by Mrs. L. D. Alder, which con-
tained many excellent ideas, relating to the posi-
tion of woman political societies, and was so in-
teresting, that the strictest attention was de-
manded.  The lady came from Salt Lake by in-
vitation of the officers of the Association, and
her address was very much appreciated.
	Mrs. Maria L. Newman of Ogden also made an
excellent address which proved to be instructive
as well as entertaining.
	A delightful song was rendered in sweetest
style by Flora Purdy, accompanied by Prof. L. D.
Edwards.
	Mr. Joseph Stanford kindly consented to speak
for a short time.  He gave many excellent views
upon woman's privileges, encouraged the ladies to
strive to regain their rights.  Referred to Glad-
stone who always counseled with his companion,
and to Lord Palmeston whose wife was a constant
help to him acting as his secretary.  Made happy
allusions to the respect in which great men had
held their wives and taken their advice and sug-
gestions in their high career as statesmen and


politicans, conversing with them upon all subjects
relative to politics and government, as well as
other matters of importance.  There are many
positions in public life to which women are
admirably adapted.  School teaching and the
training of the young is a legitimate field for
women to occupy.  And for which women are more
especially adapted.
	Many other things were referred to by the
speaker that were excellent.  A vote of thanks
was given to all who assisted in carrying out the 
programme.  The names of new members were
read by the secretary.  Glee by the choir; meet-
ing  adjourned for one month.  Prayer by the 
chaplain.  
				ELLEN PINGREE, Secretary.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
	  WOMAN SUFFRAGE MEETING.
			[CONTINUED]
	A well written address was then delivered
by
		DR. MARTHA P. HUGHES.
	To make men and women nobler, or purer,
or more intelligent, is the object of every
worthy enterprise, political and social, and
without such an aim, every undertaking should 
fail. That such has been the endeavor of the
earnest workers in the field we are now investi-
gating is beyond a doubt in my mind. It is
remarked by one of our modern thinkers,
"that it is not enough to be prompted by a
good motive or be inspired with a zealous de-
sire for the welfare of men and women, for
often honest enthusiasm and conscientious
finaticism have been the exponents of ruinous
schemes and fearful woes, neither is it sufficient
to simply find the instruments for the work of
progress and reform, there must be a combina-
tion of motive and work, a blending of in-
tellect and emotion, a strong desire with a firm 
hand, a righteous purpose with competent skill
to accomplish any permanent good, or to carry
to success any needed reform."
	In the woman's suffrage movements of recent
times there has been exhibited nearly every con-
ceivable combination of motive and instru-
mentality, and the success attending the labors
of philanthropists who have been engaged in
this work of reform has been in many ways
equally vacillating, complicated and discon-
nected, yet out of the work of the last forty
years, those interested in woman's advance-
ment, can find much to encourage and repay
them, and woman to-day has attained a posi-
tion legal and social far above that she held
half a century ago. The women of to-day have
obtained from men their much desired and
much deserved liberty. In these times the
standards of value have been readjusted, and
whereas once physical force and reckless cour-
age, were held in the highest and almost exclu-
sive esteem of mankind, now the jewels of wis-
dom, purity, culture, beauty, love and skill
have a value, that outweigh and can purchase
that brute force once so priceless. Once to be 
born a female. was to become a plaything and
a slave, to-day in the best and most powerful
circles, woman is the peer of the noblest man.
	There was a time when women possessed
none of the rights which they have to day, and
the increasing respect, which the law-makers
are showing them, marks with its various gradu-
ations the advancement of civilization and
Christianity. The regard which men have for
culture, refinement and purity is measured by
their love of women.  "The grade of a man's
mind, and moral attainments, can be safely es-
timated by the respect he shows his lady
friends." To feel the value of true woman-
hood, and to appreciate its delicacy, requires a 
nice and acute intellectual power. Brutes 
never feel it. Barbarous or half civilized men
never recognize it.

[Column 2]
	"The laws of a land are the best indices of
the people's acquirements, and are throughout
all history, the standard by which we judge of 
national advancement in civilization and
morals. We have but little reason to be
ashamed of our judicical standard today, for
the most impartial minds admit that our legal
practice is more just and reasonable in its deal-
ing with women, than that of any nation, or of
any time mentioned in history. There is much
however remaining for great minds to do, and
for the masses to accept, before either male or
female citizens can be said to enjoy perfect free-
dom and perfect equality. Certain it is how-
ever that never has the code of any people
given to woman such extraordinary privileges,
as have been bestowed by the judicial decisions 
and statute laws of our land."
	With the exception of the right to vote and
hold certain political offices, there are no rights
given to unmarried men which unmarried wo-
men do not possess. But it is this right to vote
that we as women of Utah are now contending
for. Having once enjoyed the elective fran-
chise, and having had it taken away from us
without cause, we are justified in appealing to
the nation to regain that which rightly belongs 
to us. That the privilege of voting, and the
holding of political offices by women is going
to do all that the zealous advocates of woman's
suffrage now claim is doubtful in my mind, still
there are no justifiable grounds on which these 
privileges should be withheld from them.
	All men and women are created free and 
equal. All are morally responsible for their
own wrongs. "The difference in their natures is
not a difference in value; but in the kind of 
merit. He has qualities which she has not, she
possesses traits and genius he does not; they
differ from each other and yet, in their worth
to the world, and to God, they are equal with
each other."
	Every human being of sound and matured
judgment should be personally responsible for
his or her own criminal conduct. Sex merely
should not debar any person from public office
or emolument if a constituency be found desir-
ious of his or her service.
	"No privileged class either of sex, wealth, or
descent should be allowed to arise or exist, all
persons should have the legal right to be the
equal of every other, if they can." After wo-
men have attained equal rights in every respect
before the law, that men now enjoy, and it is
only a matter of time when this will be the
case, there will doubtless then be some
drunken, brutal husbands, some parsimonious
and contemptible heads of families, fraud and
wrong doing, aching heads and aching hearts,
perhaps as many as now, but the skirts of the
law will be clean, and will no longer be com-
pelled to bear the blame; and woman will then
stand where she rightfully belongs, on an equal
platform with man before the law.
	"Nature, civilization, Christianity and the
chivalry of the law, as it comes to us from 
Knights of English fendal ages, all combine to
impress upon us, the moral and religious pre-
cept that women should have more rights be-
fore the law than men." But we simply ask for
equal recognition, still I am forced to feel that
many of the hardships that women endure,
arise more from custom and fashion, than from
disabilities before the law.
	"Woman as much as man is the architect of
her own fortunes. There are yet many realms
of silence to be made vocal, many scientific
truths to be discovered, many arts to be per-
fected which require the hands and the minds
of woman. She must feel her new responsibil-
ities." She must begin as nearly all great men
have begun, and give herself to the acquisition
of wisdom and skill, and her very opposition
and difficulties will be gain. Woman must throw
off her bondage to fashion, her submission to

[Column 3]
folly, and desire for frivolities. She must de-
feat the enticements of pleasure, the flatteries
and censures of men. It is a shame, even a
crime under divine laws for a woman to lace
herself into clothing, which makes a consump-
tive suicide of herself, and deteriorates the
physical being of her offspring. It is folly for
a woman who has leisure, to spend her hours
in idle gossip, in reading trashy novels, in fash-
ionable cold-hearted calls after the custom of
the world, instead of storing her mind with
useful knowledge, or instead of visiting the
sick, teaching the young, or assisting in the 
support of herself and others. 
	"All prejudices, all notions of propriety, tend
to genius and culture, a well disciplined mind
and a generous heart will look the Lion Opin-
ion in the eye and move on! He will quail be-
fore her and will assail her no more."
	May the day be hastened when we will not
only have our rights, but also know them, and
knowing dare maintain.
	The next speaker called upon was the
President of the Woman's Suffrage Association
of Ogden,
		      MRS. STANFORD
	She did not understand that the ladies of
this organization claimed equal political posi-
tions with men. They did not ask for the
offices which men only were fitted to fill. They
claimed the right of voting—the right to select
men who are to make the laws which both
sexes are required to obey. Women desired
that such men should be honorable, upright
and truth-loving. She contended that woman
ought to be acquainted with political as well as
other affairs, and that the world would be
better for her knowledge of and participation 
in them.
		HON. C. W. PENROSE
was called upon and he explained that the
right to vote and the right to hold office were 
not identical. For many years the women of
Utah exercised the former, but not the latter.
He was in favor of making no discrimination
against woman on the statute books. There
were offices which women might be able to fill
with ability and profit to themselves and the
public; there were others for which they were not
adapted. The good sense of the voters should
determine this. There were men who were
not fitted for office, but this did not debar them
from voting. He mentioned favorably a num-
ber of the leading ladies in the National Wo-
man Suffrage movement, and said he believed
they were inspired to work for the elevation of
their sex. There was one mistake they made,
and that was in demanding as a natural right
that which had been decided to be but a politi-
cal privilege. Citizenship and the suffrage
were distinct and separate. Every person born
in the United States is a citizen thereof. A
wife, daughter or widow of a citizen, even if of 
foreign birth, is a citizen. Citizenship comes
by birth or naturalization; the right to vote is
conferred by law. A woman has as much in-
herent right to vote as a man, but it is not in
either. The Legislature prescribes the quali-
fications of voters. If voting was an inherent
right, one would have a right to vote as soon as
born. The legal qualifications for voting
differed in different States and countries.
Another mistake made by some so called
“strong-minded” women suffragists was in be-
rating "the monster man" from whom they ex-
pected to obtain the elective franchise. This 
was not good policy, to say the least. A great 
obstacle in the way was the fact that most of 
the women were either opposed or indifferent
to woman suffrage. The work before the asso-
ciation was to spread information and convert
their own sex. When the women of the United
States unite to demand the elective franchise
they will get it. He considered a mistake was


[Column 1]
﻿made in trying for an amendment to the
national Constitution. The suffrage was a 
matter for each State to regulate and if one
State could be captured for woman suffrage
others would follow and eventually all would
come into line. Man and woman should be to-
gether in all things. The male and female ele-
ments were found in all nature and they were
so designed by the Creator in the beginning
and redeemed and exalted men and women
were to have dominion together for all the ages
of eternity. 
	HON. GEORGE Q. CANNON
having entered the building was requested to
address the meeting. He said: I take great
interest in every question that effects men and
women. I have always believed that if there
should be any qualifications for suffrage, sex
should not enter.  Women should have the
right to vote equal with men. I have seen
very many ignorant men exercise the right of
suffrage when they knew as little about the
questions involved as an animal, and at the
same time intelligent women were deprived of
that right. I think this a great injustice. Pro-
hibition is one of the issues of the times. The
question of intemperance, to whom does it ap-
peal most strongly, who is most interested?
The woman, naturally. It has a deeper inter-
est for woman than any one else. Woman's
voice in this and other affairs would doubtless
have a most excellent effect. I do not think
that because a woman was allowed to vote she
would necessarily be called into service in the
army or navy.
	There are those who argue this way, but I
think this is begging the question. I believe
that there are offices that woman could fill, and
many that she would not want to fill. For in-
stance, I do not imagine that a woman would
make a good constable to arrest men, or a
sheriff, to hang men. I believe the right of
suffrage would broaden the minds of the women
of this country. I have never seen any effects
in connection with woman suffrage to deplore.
The speaker referred to the good work per-
formed by able women for their sex and to the
voluntary defense of the people of Utah by
several notable ladies in Washington. He
closed by saying: I pray God to bless you in
this movement. Do not allow division or
jealousy to arise in your midst, but show to all
that you are laboring unitedly for the ad-
vancement of a great cause.
	MRS. NELLIE WEBBER.
	At the close of the present meeting an op-
portunity will be offered to all ladies who have 
not yet joined the Woman Suffrage Associa-
tion of Utah, to enroll their names with the
secretary.
	Fully believing that "in union is strength,"
we have associated ourselves with the National
Woman's Suffrage Association, so that we may
more effectively prosecute the aim and end we
have in view, namely, the enfranchisement of
woman and placing her upon a higher plane
politically than she occupies to-day. To be-
come an auxillary of the national association
we are required to pay a fee of 25 cents each.
For the first fifty enrolled members we are en-
titled to three delegates, and for each addi-
tional twenty-five members an additional dele-
gate. Our enrollment should be as large ss 
possible, because we not only want to show to
our eastern sisters that we are in real earnest
and have the cause of woman's political ad-
vancement at heart, but it gives us greater in-
fluence in the councils of the national associa-
tion, at the last meeting of which we had two
delegates to represent us, and I am pleased to 
say that our cause was most ably and satis-
factorily presented.
	For the benefit of the associations now being

[Column 2]
formed throughout the Territory, who may per-
chance adopt our present by-laws, I will say
that after having worked under them for three
months, we find that they need amending. This 
was under consideration at our last executive
session, and we hope soon to complete and pub-
lish an amended and more perfect set.
	In placing our initiation fee at 25 cents we
were laboring under a misunderstanding as to
the amount per member to be paid to the
national association. Now we learn that in-
stead of its being 25 per cent of our fee it is 25
cents per member, and as the matter now
stands our treasury is empty of funds. But
to increase the fee now would be an injustice to
incoming members, so it has been decided to
allow the present charge for membership to re-
main to the end of the year, when the fee will 
be increased. 
	Associations wishing to become auxiliary to
us can do so upon application and payment of
10 cents for each member enrolled by them.
	For the government of our meetings and ex-
ecutive sessions we have adopted "Roberts'
Rules of Order" and recommend them to all
new organizations. 
	Ladies' should take an interest in this matter
and aid us to place woman side by side politi-
cally with her husband and brother. Our
fathers contended that "taxation without repre-
sentation" was a wrong, and if it were so a hun-
dred years ago, it is no less so today. There
are many women in this Territory who pay
taxes and yet have no voice in the selection of 
public officers through whose hands this money 
is disbursed. I am satisfied that when women
hold the franchise universally we shall not only
have a better administration of public affairs,
but greater economy in all public expenditures;
and I therefore urge upon the ladies to come
forward at once and enroll their names in the
great cause for which we are struggling.
	A vote of thanks was given to the speakers
and the meeting was adjourned subject to the
call of the Secretary; to be published in the 
papers. Several ladies’ came forward and en-
rolled their names as members. The movement
is worthy the support of the women of Utah
and we hope to hear of branch associations all
over the Territory.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


NOTES AND NEWS.
	
	The Queen of Greece is a clever painter.
	Mrs. Elizabeth Cady Stanton will spend the 
summer at Hampstead, Long Island, N. Y. 
	Julia Ward Howe's 70th birthday will be 
celebrated May, 27, in Boston. 
	Mrs. Olive Wright, of Colorado, has patented
a filter, which renders hard water soft, and
may be used on any kitchen stove.
	The Woman's News is now published in its 
own office, printed on its own type, managed,
controlled, and all the work done by women. 
	Mrs. Clara B. Colby and Mrs. Laura De
Force Gordon are in Washington Territory,
at work for woman suffrage.
	The Pennsylvania Senate has voted an ap-
propriation of $10,000 to the Woman's Hospi-
tal. 
	On April 25, the Woman's Press Associa-
tion of Washington, paid their respects to Mrs.
Harrison. 
	The Women's Penny Paper, of London, has
begun the publication of a serial story by 
Mabel Collins, dealing with a mother's right to 
her child, and entitled "A Mother's freedom:"
	Two hundred girls are now being educated
in the medical schools of India, and Madras

[Column 2]
has already supplied six fully qualified lady 
doctors for the northern part of the country.
	Among the literary women who favor crema-
tion are Grace Greenwood, Ella Wheeler
Wilcox, Marietta Holley (Josiah Allen's wife)
Rose Terry Cooke, Kate Field, Cella Thaxter'
and Rebecca Harding Davis.
	Governor Hovey, of Indiana, recently ap-
proved of the Commissions of Willametta 
Mench, Minnie Young, Minnie Mobley and 
Anna Gilgour as Notaries Public for the town 
of Columbus, Ind. 
	At Galesburg, Ill., the Council meeting was 
attended by women, ministers and teachers of 
the public schools.  Petitions were presented 
by Knox and Lombard Colleges, and speeches
were made by women urging the Council not 
to grant dramshop licenses. 
	The first annual meeting of the Association
for the Advancement of Household Science
was held in the Chapel of Pardue Univer-
sity, LaFayette, Indiana, on the evenings of 
May 14th and 15th, 1889.  The object of the 
Association is to consider advanced methods of 
housekeeping and the improvement of the
home.
	The petition of John G. Whittier, Mary A. 
Livermore, William Lloyd Garrison, Julia 
Ward Howe, Lucy Stone, Henry B. Black-
well and others for the incorporation of the 
Massachusetts Woman Suffrage Association,
was admitted in the Massachusetts Legisla-
ture, May 1, under a suspension of the rules 
moved by Mr. McCall of Winchester. 
	Women are taught that their highest duty 
is to marry and bear children, yet laws are
framed to punish its fulfillment as a crime.
The minister of railways in Belgium has just 
issued an edict that all married women who 
are mothers, even if their children are grown
up, shall resign their posts in the telegraph
service.—Woman's Standard.
	Miss A. A. Crisp, a professional nurse, in 
charge of a hospital in New Zealand, and who 
had received royal decorations for her service 
in South Africa, Egypt and Turkey, was 
married recently to one of the doctors of the 
hospital, and wore as her wedding costume the 
uniform of her profession.  Nineteen nurses
also in uniform were her bridesmaids. 
	The New England Women's Press Associa-
tion held a business meeting last Wednesday at 
the Parker House, in Boston, the president,
Mrs. Sallie Joy White, in the chair.  Mrs. M.
Elizabeth Green, editor of the Quincy (Mass.)
Patriot, and Miss Louise Imogen Guiney, were 
elected members.  A "high tea" will be served 
to members and friends at the Parker House 
at the next meeting.
	It is late to mention the fact, but one so note-
worthy should not be forgotten, that it was a 
notable gathering of women which took place
in New York in March, when sixty delegates 
representing various women's clubs throughout 
the country, responded to the invitation of 
"Sorosis" to meet with it and form a Confedera-
tion of Woman's Clubs.  Mrs. May Wright 
Sewall spoke on the occasion, of the National 
Council of Women.  There is something 
significant in the present tendency of progres-
sive women, to draw closer together, through 
co-operative work and sympathy unions,
unions within unions, a concentrated yet ever 
widening circle of circles, which can scarcely 
fail to be a strong bulwark of civilization, and 
at the same time moving onward, like a solid 
phalanx, which few obsticles can withstand.—
Woman's News.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


IN MEMORIAM.

EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	Dear Sister—Last Sunday morning, the 7th inst.,
Sister Emma P. Toone died at her residence in this ward.
For the past six months her health has been very delicate,
but until the last five weeks she has not been wholly con-
fined to her bed; although at times during her illness, her
sufferings have been very intense, yet the cause of her de-
mise may be truthfully styled, General Debility.  In 
labors of love and devotion to the work of salvation for
mankind, her spirit was still willing, but the fleshy taber-
nacle was worn out.  Sister Toone would have been
seventy years old, had she lived until the 26th inst. Ever
since the organization of the Relief Society in 1868,
Sister Toone, until her death, has been a most efficient
and faithful laborer as a Teacher, and for many years has
presided over the quorum of teachers in the 20th ward
branch of the society.  For ten years she has also labored
with indefatigable zeal and most loving tenderness for the 
welfare of the children in the ward in the capacity of the
President of the Primary.
	At her funeral, held in the ward meeting house,
and very largely attended, several of the brethren testified
of the purity of her life, and the wisdom and energy which
characterized all her works; faithful in all things as
a wife and mother, and also as a benefactor to humanity in
the truest sense of the word.  Among the speakers were
Bishop John Sharp, President Francis, of Morgan Stake
and Bishop George Romney, who presided at the funeral.
	For several years Sister Toone's birthday has been cele
brated by many of her friends, who would congregat
about her, with tokens of loving appreciation and es-
teem on that anniversary.
	The following lines were written for and presented to
her on one of those occasions, it being her sixty-third
birthday.

			TO SISTER EMMA P. TOONE.

	Only the ones who are faithful in sorrow,
		Ever can gain the position you hold;
	Earth's richest mines could not purchase or borrow
		Thy fadeless honors, thy treasures untold.

	Here are thy children, and grand children round thee
		T call thee their savior, and say thou art blest;
	Here too, are ties which the Gospel hath found thee,
		Affection and friendship the purest and best.

	Better than all is the sweet, Holy Spirit,
		Which the great Father, alone, can bestow,
	Thou hast sought, thou hast welcomed, and right-
		fully bear it,
	Tenderly teaching thee which way to go.

	Thon hast been steadfast, and nothing could move
		thee;
		Through keenest sorrow thy portion hath been,
	Father hath chastened thee, only to prove thee,
		Cleansing thy soul from all darkness and sin.

	Patient and contrite, submissive and humble
		Doing for others, to bless and to cheer.
	Those who walk constantly thus, will not stumble;
		Dear faithful one, thou hast nothing to fear.

	Over thy sorrows, long buried, yet tearfully,
		Hearts full of sympathy lovingly yearn;
	E'en while we wish for this birthday most cheefully,
		Many a grateful and blessed return.

	If you are happy and honored in seeing,
		Each smiling face which this greeting extends,
	We, too, are honored and happy in being
		Worthy to stand as your sisters and friends.

	Still looking forth, for a brighter tomorrow,
		Which the grand future will shortly unfold—
	Only the ones who are faithful in sorrow,
		Ever can gain the position you hold.

	In the glad morn of the First Resurrection,
		In beauty's freshness, oh, glorious boon;
	Greeted by children, by friends and connections,
		'Mong the sanctified faithful will stand
			Sister Toone.

	The words of this poem express some of the sentiments
given by the brethren who spoke at the funeral, and will
meet a response from all hearts who have known the sub-
ject of this sketch.  Sister Toone was a native of Here-
fordshire, and embraced the Gospel in Warwickshire,
England, in December, 1849.
					LULA GREENE RICHARDS.


[Column 1]﻿	 
			OBITUARIES.

	Died Feb. 27th, 1889, at her residence at St. Charles,
Bear Lake County; Sarah Ingersol Holmes. Deceased
was born Sept., 16th, 1816, at Gloucester, Essex County,
Mass., her father’s name was Isaac Harvey, mother’s name
Dorcas Curtice.
	Sister Holmes crossed the plains in 1853 and went
through to California, stayed there one year then went back
East where she stayed till 1862; in that year she came back
to the Valley and settled at Farmington, Davis County,
She was well known as the Stake President of the Relief
Society of Davis Stake. She was the president of the
Relief Society of Farmington for a number of years pre-
vious. Since her residence at St. Charles, she has been
connected with the Relief Society and Primary of that
Stake, 
	Sister Holmes retained her faculties, until the last, her
son Brother Leonard Floyd, of St. Charles brought her
remains, according to her own request, to be buried be-
side those of her husband in the cemetery of Farmington.
The funeral services were held at the meeting house which
was crowded with her friends, President W. R. Smith,
Anson Call, Bishop Secrist, Elder J. T. Smith, Sisters
Mary S. Clark, Hortensia Leonard and Aurelia Rogers
made remarks on the life and conduct of the deceased.
	She was one of the noblest of women, of her it might be
said "Faithful and true in all things” she lived and died a
saint in the hope of a part in the first resurection. 
	 									COM.

	Died, at Vermillion, Sevier Co., U. T., March 7th, 1889,
Augusta Amelia, beloved wife of Niels Anderson.
	She was born July 3rd, 1845, in Sweden. Was baptized
into the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints July
30th, 1867, emigrated to Utah in 1865. She married Niels
Anderson Feb., 17th, 1866, entering into the principle of
Celestial Marriage. She was a firm believer and advocate
of all the principles pertaining to the New and Everlasting
Covenant. She held the office of President of the Y. L. M.
I. A., of this ward, until compelled by ill-health to resign.
Since the organization of the Relief Society she has been
an active member, and her many acts of kindness have en-
deared her to all.	
	She leaves an aged husband and six children besides
numerous relatives and friends to mourn her departure.
	 			PAULINE L. DASTRUP, Sec., R. S.
	Vermillion, April 17th, 1889.

	We feel it our duty to acquaint you with the death of
two of our beloved sisters, here in Monroe. Sister Mary
Griffiths, wife of David Griffiths, died March 1st, of
punemonia at this place.
	She was born at Little Lever Lancashire, England, Feb.
9, 1811. She leaves a husband, two sons, and two
daughters, and several grand children to mourn her loss.
	Sister Griffiths has been a faithful member of the Relief
Society since its organization: she was also faithful in all
the duties of a true Latter-day Saint, and rejoiced in the 
enjoyment of the blessings of the Gospel of Jesus Christ,
always trying to do right, and to be humble before the
Lord.
	She has done some work in the Temple, and felt to
praise God for His goodness and mercy. She has also
fulfilled a glorious mission among our sisters, and will long
be remembered by many.

	At Monroe, March 29th, '89, Sister Charlotte Doxford
born April 10, 1813, County of Durham, England. She
was the wife of Joseph F. Doxford. Sister Doxford's last
sickness was dropsy, and for upwards of three months be-
fore her death, she has suffered at times quite severely, yet 
always trusting in the Lord, and depending upon his
goodness and mercy. She has been a true and faithful
mother in Israel, and leaves a husband, two sons and two
daughters, and many grand children to mourn her loss.
	She has gone through many of the hardships encountered
in settling new countries, but was always ready to do all she
could wherever her help was required. She selected the
hymns to be sung at her funeral, which were sung ac-
cording to her request.
	The funeral services of both these sisters, were held at
the meeting house, where the sisters of the Relief Society
had draped the stand and arch over the coffin with white,
and had flowers suitable for such solemn occasions.

		May they rest in peace until the resurrection, 
		When all the faithful will receive their reward.

	Our Relief Society is in a prosperous condition; the
sisters are taking quite an interest in our monthly testimony

[Column 2]
meetings. We have also started weekly meetings again;
they are very well attended. Our teachers are alive to
their duties. 
		Respectfully your sisters in the Gospel, 
				CLARINDA WASHBURN, President.
				MARY SWINDLE, Secretary.


Training School for Nurses
	On the first of June, the Second Term of Dr.
Hughes’ Training School for Nurses begins, a class
of twelve (12) students completing their first term of
three months on the fourth of May. This is the
first Training School for Nurses established in
Utah, and the instruction given embraces the best
methods of teaching of the Eastern Schools, whose
systems have been thoroughly investigated by the
teacher. The purpose of this school is to give a 
wider knowledge of waiting upon the sick, and all
the branches pertaining to nursing will be thorough-
ly discussed in the successive terms. The second
term will be held in Dr. Hughes’ new residence, on
First West Street. Students desiring to enter, should
make application as early as possible, so that ar-
rangements can be made for securing text books.
	TUITION,	-	-	-	$25.00
	Special arrangements can be made for Obstetrical 
teaching.  Address:
			M. P.  Hughes,
P.O. Box 575. 				Salt Lake City, Utah.
